topics.title: Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller Architecture, 8051 Pin Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller Architecture, 8051 Pin Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Microprocessor

A **Microprocessor** is a single-chip, general-purpose, Central Processing Unit (CPU) that contains the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, **Control Unit (CU)**, and a small set of **registers** on a single Integrated Circuit (IC). It is fundamentally a *computing engine* that requires **external peripherals** such as memory (ROM/RAM), Input/Output (I/O) ports, timers, serial communication interfaces, and interrupt controllers to function as a complete computing system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A microprocessor is *not* a computer — it is the *brain* of a computer. It must be interfaced with external memory and I/O via a system bus to become functional. Example: Intel 8085, Intel 8086, Motorola 68000.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Microcontroller

A **Microcontroller** is a single-chip computer that integrates a **CPU**, **fixed amount of ROM (Program Memory)**, **RAM (Data Memory)**, **I/O ports**, **Timers/Counters**, **Serial Port**, **Interrupt logic**, and often an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** on a single silicon die. It is designed for *embedded control* applications where the task is specific, repetitive, and cost-sensitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A microcontroller is a *computer-on-a-chip*. It is "application-specific" by design and is the heart of any embedded system. Example: Intel 8051, PIC16F877, Atmel ATmega328 (Arduino Uno), ARM Cortex-M0.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant vs. The Tiffin Box

To make the architectural difference instantly intuitive, consider two real-world analogies:

| Analogy | Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| **The Restaurant** | A microprocessor is like a **large restaurant kitchen**. It is powerful, flexible, can cook *any dish* in the world — but it has *no plates, no tables, no waiters, no cash register*. You must bring everything externally to actually serve a customer. | A microcontroller is like a **packed tiffin box (lunchbox)**. Everything you need — rice, curry, pickle, spoon — is *integrated inside* one compact container. It is designed for *one specific purpose*: feeding a particular person. |
| **The Swiss Army Knife vs. The Hammer** | A **Swiss Army Knife** — has many tools, but you need to manually open each one; raw power, no integration. | A **Hammer** — has *one job*, but it does that job perfectly with all parts integrated. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Choose a **microprocessor** when you need high computational throughput, multitasking OS support, and flexible memory expansion (e.g., desktop PC, server, laptop). Choose a **microcontroller** when you need low cost, low power, deterministic real-time response, and compactness (e.g., washing machine controller, microwave oven, anti-lock braking system in cars, IoT sensor node).

## 1.4 Architectural Classification — Von Neumann vs. Harvard

The two foundational processor architectures that govern how microprocessors and microcontrollers handle memory and instructions are:

- **Von Neumann Architecture (Princeton Architecture):** A *single* memory and a *single* bus are used for *both* instructions and data. Used by most general-purpose microprocessors (8085, 8086, ARM7 in certain modes).
- **Harvard Architecture:** *Separate* memories and *separate* buses exist for instructions (code memory) and data memory. This enables *simultaneous* fetch of instruction and operand. The **8051 microcontroller** uses a **modified Harvard architecture** (it has a common data bus but separate address spaces, with an internal dedicated bus for program fetch).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip for Exams:** Remember the name "**Von-NEU-MANN**" → **ONE** memory (Neumann = One-mann). Harvard = **TWO** memories (instruction + data).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Architectural Difference — Operational Logic Breakdown

A microprocessor-based system and a microcontroller-based system differ at the **chip level, board level, and cost level**. The following bulleted logic captures the operational distinction step-by-step:

### Microprocessor System Architecture
1. **CPU is the only thing inside the chip.** ALU, registers, and control unit reside internally.
2. **Memory is external.** ROM (for program storage) and RAM (for data) are connected via the *system bus*.
3. **I/O is external.** I/O devices are interfaced through ports (e.g., 8255 PPI) connected to the data bus.
4. **Peripherals are external.** Timers (8253), interrupt controllers (8259), serial communication (8251) are all separate ICs.
5. **Address and Data Bus are exposed.** A typical 8085 system uses a **16-bit address bus** ($A_0$–$A_{15}$) and an **8-bit data bus** ($D_0$–$D_7$).
6. **The system is "open architecture."** The designer can add or remove memory, I/O, and peripherals based on need.
7. **Cost is higher** and **power consumption is higher** due to multiple chips on the board.
8. **Used where the task is variable, complex, and computationally heavy.**

### Microcontroller System Architecture
1. **CPU is inside the chip** along with **fixed ROM, RAM, I/O, Timers, Serial Port, and Interrupt Controller**.
2. **Memory is internal (on-chip).** 8051 has **4 KB of internal ROM** and **128 bytes of internal RAM**.
3. **I/O is internal.** Four 8-bit I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) — totaling **32 I/O lines** — are provided.
4. **Peripherals are internal.** Two 16-bit Timers/Counters, a full-duplex UART, and a 5-source interrupt structure are integrated.
5. **Bus is internal and not fully exposed.** Only Port 0 and Port 2 act as multiplexed address/data bus when external memory is used.
6. **The system is "closed architecture"** in the sense that memory cannot be arbitrarily expanded without limit (8051 max external memory = **64 KB program + 64 KB data = 128 KB total**).
7. **Cost is very low** and **power consumption is low** because everything is on a single die.
8. **Used where the task is fixed, deterministic, and embedded.**

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

> The following table is the **single most important comparison** for KTU exams. Memorize it — it appears in nearly every Module 1 question paper.

| Parameter | Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | CPU only | CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals |
| **Architecture** | Von Neumann (typically) | Harvard / Modified Harvard |
| **Memory** | External (ROM + RAM) | Internal (small ROM + RAM) |
| **I/O Ports** | External (via PPI like 8255) | Internal (e.g., 4 ports in 8051) |
| **Timers/Counters** | External (e.g., 8253) | Internal (e.g., 2 in 8051) |
| **Interrupt Controller** | External (e.g., 8259) | Internal |
| **Serial Port (UART)** | External (e.g., 8251) | Internal |
| **Registers** | Few (A, B, H, L, PC, SP in 8085) | Many SFRs + GPRs (8051 has 21 SFRs) |
| **Instruction Set** | CISC (8085/8086) or RISC (ARM) | CISC (8051) or RISC (AVR) |
| **Cost** | High | Low |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low |
| **Clock Speed** | Higher (GHz range in modern µP) | Lower (typically ≤ 100 MHz in classic µC) |
| **Applications** | PC, Laptop, Server, Workstation | Washing machine, AC, Car ECU, IoT, Toys |
| **Example** | Intel 8085, 8086, Core i7 | Intel 8051, PIC18F, ATmega328 |
| **Bit-width examples** | 8-bit (8085), 16-bit (8086), 64-bit (Core i7) | 8-bit (8051, PIC), 32-bit (ARM Cortex-M) |
| **Decision** | "What to do" depends on software | "How to do" is fixed by hardware + software |

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield 8051 Pin Configuration Sheet

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Type | Primary Function | Alternate Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1–8** | P1.0 – P1.7 | I/O | 8-bit bidirectional Port 1 | None (dedicated I/O) |
| **9** | RST | Input | Reset (Active HIGH, ≥ 2 machine cycles) | — |
| **10–17** | P3.0 – P3.7 | I/O | 8-bit bidirectional Port 3 | RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD |
| **18** | XTAL2 | Output | Crystal Oscillator Output | — |
| **19** | XTAL1 | Input | Crystal Oscillator Input | — |
| **20** | VSS / GND | Power | Ground (0 V) | — |
| **21–28** | P2.0 – P2.7 | I/O | 8-bit bidirectional Port 2 | High-byte address bus (A8–A15) when external memory is accessed |
| **29** | PSEN | Output | Program Store Enable (active LOW) | Used to read external code memory |
| **30** | ALE / PROG | Output | Address Latch Enable / Flash programming pulse | Latches low-byte address into 74373 |
| **31** | EA / VPP | Input | External Access (LOW → use external ROM) | VPP applies +12 V during Flash programming |
| **32–39** | P0.0 – P0.7 | I/O | 8-bit bidirectional Port 0 (no internal pull-up) | Multiplexed low-byte address (A0–A7) and data bus (D0–D7) |
| **40** | VCC | Power | +5 V supply | — |

### KTU High-Yield P3 Alternate Pin Functions (Frequently Asked)

| P3 Bit | Pin | Alternate Name | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | 10 | **RxD** | Receive Data for serial port |
| P3.1 | 11 | **TxD** | Transmit Data for serial port |
| P3.2 | 12 | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | 13 | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | 14 | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | 15 | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | 16 | **WR** | External Data Memory Write strobe (active LOW) |
| P3.7 | 17 | **RD** | External Data Memory Read strobe (active LOW) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for P3 alternate functions:** Read the pin numbers in order: **"Rx, Tx, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD"** — sounds like a small sentence: *"Are-Ex-tee-oh, tee-aitch, double-you-are, are-dee."* — In KTU exams, students often confuse **PSEN** (program read) with **RD** (data read). **PSEN is for ROM/Code, RD is for RAM/Data.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor systems** are still dominant in applications requiring high-speed computation: industrial automation servers, image processing, AI inference engines, and edge-computing gateways.
- **Microcontroller systems** dominate *embedded control*: According to industry reports, **over 50 billion microcontrollers are shipped annually** — one for almost every person on Earth per year. They are in cars (60–100 MCUs per modern car), medical devices (pacemakers, glucometers), home appliances, drones, and IoT nodes.
- The **8051** specifically, despite being introduced by Intel in **1980**, remains the *most widely taught* microcontroller globally due to its **simple architecture, well-documented instruction set (255 opcodes), and rich ecosystem**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — 8051 Memory Addressability

The 8051 is an **8-bit microcontroller** with a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** and a **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)**. Let us analytically derive why the 8051 can address exactly **64 KB** of program memory and **64 KB** of data memory — and not a byte more.

### Step 1: Number of address lines in Program Counter

The 8051 Program Counter is a 16-bit register. The number of unique memory locations it can address is given by:

$$
N_{prog} = 2^{n}
$$

where $n$ is the number of address lines. For the program counter, $n = 16$:

$$
N_{prog} = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ locations}
$$

### Step 2: Convert to kilobytes (KTU standard unit)

$$
65536 \text{ bytes} = \frac{65536}{1024} \text{ KB} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step 3: Repeat for Data Pointer (DPTR)

DPTR is also a **16-bit register** (formed by DPH + DPL — two 8-bit Special Function Registers). It is used to address external data memory (XRAM).

$$
N_{data} = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ locations} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step 4: Derive the internal ROM size of 8051

The original 8051 has **4 KB of on-chip ROM**. The number of address lines required to address 4 KB is:

$$
4 \text{ KB} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
2^{n} = 4096 \implies n = \log_2(4096) = 12
$$

So internally, only **12 address lines** ($A_0$–$A_{11}$) are decoded to access 4 KB of on-chip ROM.

> **Conversion logic:** Since $2^{10} = 1024 \approx 1 \text{ KB}$ (binary Kilo), we have $2^{12} = 2^{10} \times 2^{2} = 1024 \times 4 = 4096 = 4 \text{ KB}$.

### Step 5: Derive the internal RAM size of 8051

The 8051 has **128 bytes of on-chip RAM** (00H to 7FH).

$$
2^{n} = 128 \implies n = \log_2(128) = 7
$$

So the internal RAM is addressed by **7 address lines**, occupying addresses **00H to 7FH** ($0$ to $127$ in decimal).

### Step 6: Total memory addressable by 8051

$$
\text{Total} = 64 \text{ KB (Code)} + 64 \text{ KB (Data)} = 128 \text{ KB}
$$

This is the **physical address space** the 8051 can reach *with external memory expansion*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — 8051 Oscillator Frequency vs. Machine Cycle

The 8051 divides the external crystal oscillator frequency by **12** internally to generate one **machine cycle**. This is a classic KTU derivation.

### Step-by-step derivation:

$$
f_{osc} = \text{Crystal frequency} \quad (\text{e.g., } 11.0592 \text{ MHz})
$$

$$
f_{machine\ cycle} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}
$$

$$
T_{machine\ cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{machine\ cycle}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

**Numerical Example 1 (KTU-favorite):** If crystal frequency = **11.0592 MHz**, calculate the time for one machine cycle.

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{mc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} = 1.085\ \mu s
$$

**Numerical Example 2:** For a 12 MHz crystal:

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1\ \mu s
$$

> **Conversion logic explanation:** The "divide by 12" comes from the fact that the original 8051 design used a 12-clock oscillator period per machine cycle to allow the slow bipolar processes of 1980 to reliably execute instructions. Modern 8051 cores (e.g., from Nuvoton, Silicon Labs) run at 1 clock per machine cycle (1:1) and are 12× faster, but the textbook 8051 retains the 12:1 ratio.

### KTU High-Yield Derivation Table

| Given $f_{osc}$ | $f_{mc} = f_{osc}/12$ | $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc}$ | Instruction Time (1 µs × N) for 1-byte, 1-cycle instr. |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12 MHz | 1 MHz | 1 µs | 1 µs |
| 11.0592 MHz | 0.9216 MHz | 1.085 µs | 1.085 µs |
| 24 MHz | 2 MHz | 0.5 µs | 0.5 µs |
| 6 MHz | 0.5 MHz | 2 µs | 2 µs |

> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This is a *non-obvious* KTU favorite question. The value **11.0592 MHz** is chosen because it is **divisible exactly by 12** AND by the standard baud rate divisor **192**, giving **11.0592 MHz ÷ 12 ÷ 32 = 28800** and **÷ 192 = 57600**, producing error-free standard UART baud rates (9600, 19200, etc.). With 12 MHz crystal, the baud rate divisor would produce a non-integer value and cause serial communication errors.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Breakdown — Why EA and PSEN are Critical Pins

| Pin | Logic Level | Meaning | Bus Activated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EA = 1 (HIGH)** | Internal ROM used | CPU fetches code from on-chip ROM first; goes external only above 4 KB | PSEN for external code fetch |
| **EA = 0 (LOW)** | External ROM forced | CPU ignores on-chip ROM; all code fetched externally | PSEN active for *all* fetches |
| **PSEN = 0 (LOW)** | Program memory read strobe | Output signal that *enables* the external code memory chip to place data on P0 bus | — |
| **RD = 0 (LOW)** | Data memory read strobe | Output signal that enables external data memory (XRAM) read | — |

> **Conversion logic for exam answer:** *PSEN reads CODE (ROM), RD reads DATA (RAM), WR writes DATA (RAM). PSEN is the "Program Store ENable."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller

This block diagram illustrates the architectural difference at the system-board level. The 8051 is used as the canonical microcontroller.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MPU["MICROPROCESSOR SYSTEM (e.g., 8085)"]
        direction TB
        CPU1["CPU CHIP (8085)"]
        EXTROM["External ROM"]
        EXTRAM["External RAM"]
        EXTIO["External I/O 8255"]
        EXTTMR["External Timer 8253"]
        EXTSER["External UART 8251"]
        EXTINT["External Interrupt Ctrl 8259"]
        BUS1["System Bus (Address + Data + Control)"]

        BUS1 --- CPU1
        BUS1 --- EXTROM
        BUS1 --- EXTRAM
        BUS1 --- EXTIO
        BUS1 --- EXTTMR
        BUS1 --- EXTSER
        BUS1 --- EXTINT
    end

    subgraph MCU["MICROCONTROLLER SYSTEM (e.g., 8051)"]
        direction TB
        CPU2["CPU"]
        INTROM["Internal ROM 4KB"]
        INTRAM["Internal RAM 128B"]
        IOBLOCK["I/O Ports P0 P1 P2 P3 32 lines"]
        TMRBLK["Timers T0 T1 16-bit"]
        SERBLK["UART Serial Port"]
        INTBLK["Interrupt Controller"]
        INTERNAL["Single Silicon Die"]

        CPU2 --- INTERNAL
        INTROM --- INTERNAL
        INTRAM --- INTERNAL
        IOBLOCK --- INTERNAL
        TMRBLK --- INTERNAL
        SERBLK --- INTERNAL
        INTBLK --- INTERNAL
    end
```

## 4.2 8051 Pin Diagram — 40-Pin DIP Package Layout

This is the most-tested diagram in KTU exams. Below is a structured pin map. Pin 1 is at the top-left (when notch is at the top), and pin numbering proceeds **counter-clockwise**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        RST["RST Pin9"]

        P3F["P3.5 T1 Pin15"]

        P3H["P3.7 RD Pin17"]

        XT1["XTAL1 Pin19"]
        GND["VSS GND Pin20"]
    end

    subgraph RIGHT["PINS 21-40 (Right Side)"]
        direction TB
        P2A["P2.0 Pin21"]
        P2B["P2.1 Pin22"]
        P2C["P2.2 Pin23"]
        P2D["P2.3 Pin24"]
        P2E["P2.4 Pin25"]
        P2F["P2.5 Pin26"]
        P2G["P2.6 Pin27"]
        P2H["P2.7 Pin28"]
        PSEN["PSEN Pin29"]
        ALE["ALE PROG Pin30"]
        EA["EA VPP Pin31"]
```

## 4.3 Port 0 Internal Structure (Why It Needs External Pull-up)

The schematic below shows the *internal architecture* of a single pin of **Port 0**, which is unique among the four ports because **it has no internal pull-up resistor**. This is a classic KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BUS["Internal Data Bus D"]
    LATCH["D Latch Q output"]
    MUX["Internal MUX Selector"]
    P0PIN["P0.x Physical Pin"]
    PU["External Pull-up Resistor 10K to VCC"]

    BUS --> LATCH
    LATCH --> MUX
    MUX --> P0PIN
    PU --> P0PIN
```

> **Conversion logic explanation:** When Port 0 is used as a **general-purpose I/O output**, the internal MUX disconnects the address bus and connects the D-latch Q to the pin. The output transistor is an **open-drain** configuration, meaning it can only *pull the pin LOW actively*; to make the pin HIGH, current must flow *through an external pull-up resistor* to VCC. This is why external memory interfacing circuits *always* show a 10KΩ pull-up on P0 lines.

---

## 4.4 Harvard Architecture of 8051 — Modified Harvard Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["8051 CPU"]
    PROM["Program Memory 64KB address space 4KB internal plus 60KB external"]
    DRAM["Data Memory 64KB address space 128B internal plus external XRAM"]
    IBUS["Instruction Bus"]
    DBUS["Data Bus"]

    CPU <--> IBUS
    CPU <--> DBUS
    IBUS <--> PROM
    DBUS <--> DRAM
```

> **Conversion logic explanation:** This is the **Modified Harvard** architecture: separate *physical address spaces* for code and data, but the CPU uses a *common internal data bus* for both while the *instruction fetch* is decoupled. PSEN is asserted only when fetching *code*, while RD/WR are asserted only when accessing *external data memory* — preventing bus collision.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. List any three applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU only on a single chip | CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on a single chip |
| 2 | Memory and I/O are external | Memory and I/O are internal |
| 3 | High cost, high power, high speed | Low cost, low power, moderate speed |
| 4 | Von Neumann architecture | Harvard / Modified Harvard architecture |

**Applications of Microprocessor (1 mark):** Personal computers, laptops, servers, supercomputers, industrial automation controllers, gaming consoles.

**Applications of Microcontroller (1 mark):** Washing machines, microwave ovens, anti-lock braking system (ABS), pacemakers, IoT sensor nodes, toys, drones, smart watches.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the function of the following 8051 pins: (a) PSEN, (b) EA/VPP, (c) ALE/PROG.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) PSEN — Program Store Enable (Pin 29):** This is an *active-LOW output* signal. It is asserted by the 8051 CPU whenever it is fetching an instruction byte from **external program memory** (ROM). PSEN enables the external ROM chip to place the instruction opcode on the Port 0 bus. It is *not* asserted for internal ROM fetches or for external data memory accesses. **(1 mark)**

**(b) EA / VPP — External Access / Programming Voltage (Pin 31):** This is a *dual-function input* pin. When **EA = 1 (HIGH)**, the 8051 fetches code from internal ROM for the first 4 KB and from external ROM for addresses above 4 KB. When **EA = 0 (LOW)**, the CPU ignores the internal ROM and fetches *all* instructions from external ROM (used in 8031 systems). During Flash programming of the 8051, this pin is raised to **+12 V (VPP)** to enable programming mode. **(1 mark)**

**(c) ALE / PROG — Address Latch Enable / Programming Pulse (Pin 30):** This is an *output* pin. During normal operation, ALE emits a positive pulse at the beginning of every machine cycle. Its primary role is to *latch the low-byte address* (A0–A7) from the multiplexed Port 0 bus into an external 74HC373 latch, separating address from data on the shared bus. During Flash programming (PROG mode), this pin receives the programming pulse. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 1, Choice 1]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the architecture of a typical **microprocessor-based system**, listing the major functional blocks. Compare it with a **microcontroller-based system** using a comparative table of at least **eight points**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **8051 pin configuration** in a **40-pin DIP package**. List the **alternate functions of Port 3** with pin numbers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A**

**Part (a) — Microprocessor-Based System Architecture (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram of Microprocessor System (3 marks):**

```
+----------------+      +----------------+
|   CPU (8085)   |      |  ROM (2716)    |
|  ALU + CU + R  |      +----------------+
+--------+-------+              |
         |      +---------------+
         |      |  Data Bus D0-D7  |
         |      +---------------+
+--------+-------+              |
| Address Bus   |              |
| A0 - A15      |              v
+--------+-------+      +----------------+
         |             |  RAM (6264)    |
         |             +----------------+
         v
+--------------------+
| I/O Port (8255)    |
+--------------------+
+----------------+
| Timer (8253)  |
+----------------+
+----------------+
| UART (8251)   |
+----------------+
+----------------+
| Interrupt (8259)|
+----------------+
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) for arithmetic and logical operations, the CU (Control Unit) for decoding instructions, and a small set of working registers (A, B, H, L, PC, SP in 8085).
2. **System Bus:** Three buses connect all components:
   - **Address Bus (16-bit, unidirectional):** Carries memory/I/O addresses from CPU to peripherals.
   - **Data Bus (8-bit, bidirectional):** Carries actual data between CPU, memory, and I/O.
   - **Control Bus:** Carries timing and control signals like RD, WR, IO/M, ALE.
3. **External Memory:**
   - **ROM (e.g., 2716, 2732, 2764):** Stores the program permanently.
   - **RAM (e.g., 6264):** Stores temporary data and the stack.
4. **External I/O (8255 PPI):** Provides programmable parallel I/O ports.
5. **External Timer (8253/8254):** Provides timing and counting functions.
6. **External UART (8251):** Provides serial communication.
7. **External Interrupt Controller (8259):** Manages multiple hardware interrupts.
8. **Address Decoding Logic:** Gates like 74LS138 decode the high-order address bits to select specific memory or I/O chips.
9. **Clock Generator:** 8284 clock generator provides CLK and ready signals to the CPU.
10. **Power and Reset Circuit:** Provides +5V, GND, and a reset signal to initialize the CPU.

**Comparative Table (Already provided in Section 2.2 — student should reproduce it here; this takes 1 mark from the 7.)**

**Part (b) — 8051 Pin Diagram (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram:** *(The 40-pin DIP layout from Section 4.2 should be reproduced here; the Mermaid diagram should be redrawn by hand or printed in the answer sheet.)*

**Explanation of Key Pin Groups (4 marks):**

1. **Power Pins (2 pins):**
   - Pin 40: VCC = +5V
   - Pin 20: VSS = GND (0V)

2. **Oscillator Pins (2 pins):**
   - Pin 18: XTAL2 — output of the in-chip oscillator amplifier.
   - Pin 19: XTAL1 — input to the in-chip oscillator amplifier.
   - A quartz crystal connected between XTAL1 and XTAL2, with two 30 pF capacitors to ground, generates the system clock.

3. **Reset Pin (1 pin):**
   - Pin 9: RST — A HIGH pulse of at least 2 machine cycles on this pin resets the 8051. Internally, a small pull-down resistor holds it LOW.

4. **I/O Port Pins (32 pins):**
   - **Port 1 (Pins 1–8):** 8-bit bidirectional, with internal pull-ups. Has no alternate functions. Pure general-purpose I/O.
   - **Port 3 (Pins 10–17):** 8-bit bidirectional, with internal pull-ups. Each pin has an alternate function (see below).
   - **Port 2 (Pins 21–28):** 8-bit bidirectional, with internal pull-ups. Acts as the **high-byte address bus (A8–A15)** when external memory is accessed.
   - **Port 0 (Pins 32–39):** 8-bit bidirectional, **open-drain (no internal pull-up)**. Functions as the **multiplexed low-byte address (A0–A7) and data bus (D0–D7)**.

5. **Control Pins (3 pins):**
   - Pin 29: PSEN
   - Pin 30: ALE/PROG
   - Pin 31: EA/VPP
   *(Already explained in Question 2 of Part A.)*

**Alternate Functions of Port 3 (3 marks):**

| P3 Pin | Pin No. | Alternate Name | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | 10 | RxD | Serial Input (Receive Data) |
| P3.1 | 11 | TxD | Serial Output (Transmit Data) |
| P3.2 | 12 | INT0 | External Interrupt 0 input |
| P3.3 | 13 | INT1 | External Interrupt 1 input |
| P3.4 | 14 | T0 | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | 15 | T1 | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | 16 | WR | External Data Memory Write (active LOW) |
| P3.7 | 17 | RD | External Data Memory Read (active LOW) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 1, Choice 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **difference between Von Neumann and Harvard architecture**. State which one is used in **8051** and justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an 8051 system using an **11.0592 MHz crystal**, calculate: **(i)** the **machine cycle frequency**, **(ii)** the **time taken for one machine cycle**, **(iii)** the **time taken to execute the instruction `MOV A, #55H`**, and **(iv)** the **maximum external program memory size** the 8051 can address. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B**

**Part (a) — Von Neumann vs. Harvard (7 marks)**

**Von Neumann Architecture (3 marks):**
- Proposed by **John von Neumann in 1945**.
- Uses a **single memory** to store both **instructions and data**.
- Uses a **single bus** (the *data/address bus*) to transfer both instructions and operands.
- Because the bus is shared, the CPU can either *fetch an instruction* OR *fetch/write data* in any given cycle — **never both simultaneously**. This is called the **Von Neumann bottleneck**.
- **Example processors:** 8085, 8086, ARM7 (in some configurations), most general-purpose CPUs.

**Harvard Architecture (4 marks):**
- Based on the **Harvard Mark I relay-based computer (1944)**.
- Uses **physically separate memories and separate buses** for **instructions and data**.
- Allows the CPU to **simultaneously fetch an instruction** (via the instruction bus) **and read/write data** (via the data bus). This eliminates the Von Neumann bottleneck and increases throughput.
- **Disadvantage:** More complex hardware (two buses, two memories, more pins for I/O).
- **Example processors:** 8051 (modified Harvard), PIC microcontrollers, DSPs, modern ARM Cortex-M.

**8051 Architecture Type (2 marks):**
The **8051 uses a MODIFIED HARVARD ARCHITECTURE**.

**Justification:**
- The 8051 has **separate program memory (64 KB code space)** and **separate data memory (64 KB data space)**.
- Internally, it has **separate buses** for instruction fetch (via PC) and data read/write (via R0–R7, DPTR, SP).
- However, the **external bus is unified** — Port 0 is multiplexed for both low-byte address and data, and Port 2 carries the high-byte address.
- When accessing **external code memory**, **PSEN** is used.
- When accessing **external data memory**, **RD** and **WR** are used.
- This selective use of control signals (PSEN for code, RD/WR for data) is what makes it a *modified* Harvard rather than a pure Harvard.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$

**(i) Machine Cycle Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12}
$$

$$
f_{mc} = 0.9216 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Time for One Machine Cycle (2 marks):**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{1}{f_{mc}} = \frac{1}{0.9216 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{mc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085\ \mu s
$$

**(iii) Time to Execute `MOV A, #55H` (2 marks):**

The instruction `MOV A, #55H` is a **1-byte, 1-machine-cycle** instruction in the 8051 (opcode: `74H`).

$$
T_{exec} = 1 \times T_{mc} = 1 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 1.085\ \mu s
$$

**(iv) Maximum External Program Memory (1 mark):**

The 8051 has a **16-bit Program Counter**, so:

$$
\text{Max Program Memory} = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

> **Conversion logic recap:** The "÷12" is a defining feature of the *original Intel 8051* (MCS-51 family). Modern derivatives like the **8052, AT89C52, AT89S52** retain the same architecture but have more RAM (256 B) and more ROM (8 KB).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing PSEN and RD:** PSEN is for *program memory (ROM)* read, RD is for *data memory (RAM)* read. Drawing a memory interfacing diagram with PSEN connected to a RAM chip or RD connected to a ROM chip will cost you 2 marks outright.
> 2. **Forgetting Port 0's open-drain nature:** If you draw Port 0 used as output without showing the 10KΩ pull-up resistors to VCC, the examiner will mark the port structure diagram incomplete.
> 3. **Wrong EA logic:** Writing "EA = 1 means use external ROM" is a common wrong answer. **EA = 1** means "use *internal* ROM first, then external." **EA = 0** means "use *only* external ROM" (this is the configuration for the 8031, which has no internal ROM).
> 4. **Calculation errors in machine cycle:** Writing $T_{mc} = 1/f_{osc}$ instead of $12/f_{osc}$ will lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up Harvard and Von Neumann:** Writing "8051 uses Von Neumann" will lose 2 marks. The 8051 uses **Modified Harvard**.
> 6. **Forgetting the P3 alternate functions in the pin diagram question:** If the question asks to "list the pin numbers and alternate functions of Port 3," a student who only draws the pin diagram without listing the alternate functions loses 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> Use this section as a **rapid-revision checklist** the night before the exam.

- [x] **Microprocessor = CPU only; Microcontroller = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on one chip.**
- [x] **Microprocessor uses Von Neumann (typically); Microcontroller uses Harvard / Modified Harvard.**
- [x] **Microprocessor has external memory and I/O; Microcontroller has internal.**
- [x] **Microprocessor cost and power are HIGH; Microcontroller cost and power are LOW.**
- [x] **8051 has 40 pins, packaged in DIP.**
- [x] **4 ports: P0 (32–39, open-drain), P1 (1–8, pull-up), P2 (21–28, pull-up), P3 (10–17, pull-up + alt functions).**
- [x] **Pin 9 = RST (active HIGH, ≥ 2 machine cycles).**
- [x] **Pin 18 = XTAL2, Pin 19 = XTAL1 (crystal oscillator).**
- [x] **Pin 20 = GND, Pin 40 = VCC (+5V).**
- [x] **Pin 29 = PSEN (Program Store ENable, active LOW, for external code memory).**
- [x] **Pin 30 = ALE (Address Latch Enable, latches A0–A7 from P0) / PROG (programming pulse).**
- [x] **Pin 31 = EA (External Access: 1 = internal ROM, 0 = external ROM) / VPP (+12V programming voltage).**
- [x] **P3.0 = RxD, P3.1 = TxD, P3.2 = INT0, P3.3 = INT1, P3.4 = T0, P3.5 = T1, P3.6 = WR, P3.7 = RD.**
- [x] **8051 internal ROM = 4 KB, internal RAM = 128 B.**
- [x] **8051 addressable space = 64 KB program + 64 KB data = 128 KB total.**
- [x] **8051 is an 8-bit microcontroller with a 16-bit PC and 16-bit DPTR.**
- [x] **Machine cycle frequency = $f_{osc} / 12$.**
- [x] **Machine cycle time = $12 / f_{osc}$.**
- [x] **11.0592 MHz crystal is the industry-standard choice for error-free UART baud rates.**
- [x] **8051 uses MODIFIED HARVARD architecture — separate code/data address spaces, unified external bus, PSEN for code, RD/WR for data.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 8051 Microcontroller — Formal Definition

The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit Harvard architecture** Single-Chip Microcontroller (SCM) introduced by Intel in 1980. It integrates a CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O ports, timers, serial port, and interrupt logic onto a single silicon die. KTU 2024 syllabus uses the **MCS-51 family (8051)** as the foundational reference architecture for teaching embedded systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PCCPT302 course exclusively focuses on the **8-bit 8051 family** architecture. You must distinguish it from the Von Neumann architecture used in generic CPUs (e.g., 8086).

### Architectural Specifications at a Glance

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Data Bus Width | **8-bit** |
| Address Bus Width (External) | **16-bit** |
| Maximum External Memory | **64 KB** Code + **64 KB** Data |
| On-chip RAM | **128 Bytes** (Standard 8051) / **256 Bytes** (8052) |
| On-chip ROM | **4 KB** (Standard 8051) |
| I/O Ports | **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines** |
| Timers/Counters | **2 × 16-bit** |
| Interrupt Sources | **5** (Standard 8051) |
| Operating Frequency | **12 MHz** crystal (1 µs machine cycle) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a **microcontroller as a smart restaurant kitchen**:
- **CPU** = The Head Chef — executes instructions (recipes).
- **ROM (Program Memory)** = The Recipe Book — permanently stored, never modified.
- **RAM (Data Memory)** = The Prep Counter — temporary workspace where ingredients are chopped/mixed.
- **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** = Specialized Tools (ovens, mixers, timers) — each with a specific purpose.
- **I/O Ports** = The serving windows (Pass-through to customers/devices).
- **Instruction Set** = The vocabulary of cooking techniques the chef knows.
- **Addressing Modes** = The way the chef refers to ingredients ("the second bowl", "the bowl on the right", "the bowl that contains salt").

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harvard Architecture?** In a restaurant, recipes (program) and ingredients (data) are stored in *separate* locations. The chef can read a recipe and grab an ingredient **simultaneously** — this is why Harvard is faster than Von Neumann for embedded control.

## 1.3 Block-Level Architecture Overview

The 8051 contains the following functional blocks integrated on-chip:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — 8-bit ALU, Accumulator (A), B Register, Program Counter (PC), Stack Pointer (SP), Program Status Word (PSW).
2. **Oscillator and Clock Circuit** — Requires external crystal (typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**).
3. **Program Memory (ROM)** — 4 KB on-chip, expandable to 64 KB externally.
4. **Data Memory (RAM)** — 128 bytes on-chip (00H–7FH), expandable to 64 KB externally.
5. **Four 8-bit I/O Ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3 (each with bidirectional capability).
6. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — T0, T1.
7. **Full-Duplex Serial Port** — UART.
8. **Interrupt Control Logic** — 5 sources, 2 priority levels.
9. **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — Located in upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (80H–FFH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Alert (3-Mark Favorite):** *"List the features of 8051."* Memorize the table in §1.1 — it is a recurring KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CPU Architecture and Register Organization

The 8051 CPU contains the following registers used for program execution:

### 2.1.1 General Purpose Registers
- **Accumulator (A / ACC)** — 8-bit; the most-used register for arithmetic, logic, and data transfer operations. Used implicitly in many opcodes.
- **B Register** — 8-bit; used explicitly in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions; otherwise available as a scratch register.

### 2.1.2 Pointer Registers
- **Data Pointer (DPTR = DPH + DPL)** — 16-bit register used to access external data memory (XRAM) and look-up tables in program memory (via `MOVC`).
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** — 8-bit register pointing to the top of the stack in internal RAM. **Initialized to 07H** on reset. Stack grows **upward**.

### 2.1.3 Program Counter (PC)
- **16-bit** register; always points to the **next instruction** to be fetched.
- **Initialized to 0000H** on reset (this is the program start address).

### 2.1.4 Program Status Word (PSW)
The PSW is an **8-bit SFR at address D0H** that contains flag bits reflecting the result of the last arithmetic/logic operation.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | CY | D7H | Carry Flag — set on unsigned overflow |
| PSW.6 | AC | D6H | Auxiliary Carry — for BCD arithmetic |
| PSW.5 | F0 | D5H | User-defined Flag 0 |
| PSW.4 | RS1 | D4H | Register Bank Select 1 |
| PSW.3 | RS0 | D3H | Register Bank Select 0 |
| PSW.2 | OV | D2H | Overflow Flag — signed overflow |
| PSW.1 | — | D1H | Reserved |
| PSW.0 | P | D0H | Parity Flag — set if odd number of 1s in A |

> [!NOTE]
> **RS1:RS0 Bank Selection Logic:**
> - **00** → Bank 0 (00H–07H)
> - **01** → Bank 1 (08H–0FH)
> - **10** → Bank 2 (10H–17H)
> - **11** → Bank 3 (18H–1FH)

## 2.2 Register Banks (R0–R7)

The lower **32 bytes (00H–1FH)** of internal RAM are organized as **4 banks of 8 registers each** (R0–R7). Only one bank is active at a time, selected by RS1:RS0 bits in PSW.

## 2.3 Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH)

The **16 bytes from 20H to 2FH** are **bit-addressable**, providing **128 individually addressable bits** (bit 00H to 7FH). This allows single-bit manipulation using instructions like `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL`, `ORL`.

## 2.4 Special Function Registers (SFRs) — The Heart of 8051

SFRs occupy the **upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses 80H–FFH)**. They control on-chip peripherals and CPU functions.

### Major SFRs Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | 80H | Yes | Port 0 latch |
| **SP** | 81H | No | Stack Pointer |
| **DPL** | 82H | No | Data Pointer Low |
| **DPH** | 83H | No | Data Pointer High |
| **PCON** | 87H | No | Power Control |
| **TCON** | 88H | Yes | Timer Control |
| **TMOD** | 89H | No | Timer Mode |
| **TL0** | 8AH | No | Timer 0 Low |
| **TL1** | 8BH | No | Timer 1 Low |
| **TH0** | 8CH | No | Timer 0 High |
| **TH1** | 8DH | No | Timer 1 High |
| **P1** | 90H | Yes | Port 1 latch |
| **SCON** | 98H | Yes | Serial Control |
| **SBUF** | 99H | No | Serial Data Buffer |
| **P2** | A0H | Yes | Port 2 latch |
| **IE** | A8H | Yes | Interrupt Enable |
| **P3** | B0H | Yes | Port 3 latch |
| **IP** | B8H | Yes | Interrupt Priority |
| **PSW** | D0H | Yes | Program Status Word |
| **A (ACC)** | E0H | Yes | Accumulator |
| **B** | F0H | Yes | B Register |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Map Rule:** SFR addresses 80H–FFH **overlap** with the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM, but they are accessed via **different addressing mechanisms** (direct addressing → SFRs; indirect with @R0/@R1 → upper RAM in 8052).

## 2.5 I/O Port Structure

Each of the 4 ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) is an **8-bit bidirectional** latch with internal pull-ups (except P0).

### 2.5.1 Port 0 (P0) — Pins 32–39
- **Dual function:** Acts as **lower address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** when accessing external memory.
- **No internal pull-up resistors** — requires **external 10 kΩ pull-ups** when used as a general I/O port.
- Open-drain output.

### 2.5.2 Port 1 (P1) — Pins 1–8
- **General purpose I/O only** (no alternate functions in standard 8051).
- Has **internal pull-up resistors**.

### 2.5.3 Port 2 (P2) — Pins 21–28
- **Dual function:** Provides the **high byte of the address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- Has **internal pull-up resistors**.

### 2.5.4 Port 3 (P3) — Pins 10–17
- **Multi-functional pin** with alternate uses:

| Pin | Alternate Function |
|---|---|
| P3.0 | RxD (Serial Input) |
| P3.1 | TxD (Serial Output) |
| P3.2 | INT0 (External Interrupt 0) |
| P3.3 | INT1 (External Interrupt 1) |
| P3.4 | T0 (Timer 0 Input) |
| P3.5 | T1 (Timer 1 Input) |
| P3.6 | WR (External Write Strobe) |
| P3.7 | RD (External Read Strobe) |

## 2.6 Memory Organization

The 8051 has a **Harvard architecture** with **separate address spaces** for code and data.

### 2.6.1 Program Memory (Code Space)
- **Total: 64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH)
- On-chip ROM: **4 KB** (0000H–0FFFH in standard 8051)
- External code memory: Up to **64 KB** accessed via **PSEN** signal.
- Special addresses:
  - **0000H** → Reset vector
  - **0003H** → External Interrupt 0 (INT0)
  - **000BH** → Timer 0 Overflow
  - **0013H** → External Interrupt 1 (INT1)
  - **001BH** → Timer 1 Overflow
  - **0023H** → Serial Port Interrupt

### 2.6.2 Internal Data Memory (Lower 128 bytes: 00H–7FH)
| Address Range | Function |
|---|---|
| 00H–1FH | 4 Register Banks (R0–R7 × 4) |
| 20H–2FH | Bit-Addressable Area (128 bits) |
| 30H–7FH | General Purpose RAM (Scratch Pad) |

### 2.6.3 External Data Memory (XDATA)
- **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) accessed using **MOVX** instructions.
- Addressed via **16-bit DPTR** or **R0/R1** (with P2 providing upper address in some modes).

## 2.7 Addressing Modes — Complete Reference

| Mode | Syntax | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | `#data` | `MOV A, #25H` | Constant embedded in instruction |
| **Register** | `Rn` | `MOV A, R0` | One of R0–R7 |
| **Direct** | `direct` | `MOV A, 30H` | Direct internal RAM/SFR address |
| **Register Indirect** | `@Ri` | `MOV A, @R0` | Address held in R0/R1 (lower 128 bytes) |
| **Register Indirect (16-bit)** | `@DPTR` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | Address in DPTR (for XDATA) |
| **Indexed** | `@A+DPTR` / `@A+PC` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | For look-up tables in code memory |
| **Implied/Implicit** | — | `CLR A`, `INC A` | Operation on A or specified register |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack Addressing** is also a hidden form of register indirect addressing via SP. Instructions `PUSH` and `POP` use this mode.

## 2.8 Instruction Set Categories

The 8051 has **111 distinct instructions** grouped into:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions** — `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`, `XCHD`
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** — `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `DA A`
3. **Logical Instructions** — `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, `SWAP`
4. **Boolean (Bit) Instructions** — `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C`, `ORL C`, `MOV C`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`
5. **Program Branching** — `LJMP`, `SJMP`, `AJMP`, `JMP @A+DPTR`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`
6. **Pseudo Instructions** (Assembler Directives) — `ORG`, `END`, `DB`, `DW`, `EQU`, `BIT`

### KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | At 12 MHz → 1 µs |
| Instruction Cycles | 1, 2, or 4 machine cycles | 1 µs, 2 µs, or 4 µs at 12 MHz |
| External Memory | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | 64 KB code + 64 KB data |
| Total ROM (4 KB) | $4 \times 1024 = 4096$ bytes | 0000H–0FFFH |
| Total RAM (128 B) | $2^7 = 128$ bytes | 00H–7FH |
| Bit-Addressable | $16 \times 8 = 128$ bits | 20H–2FH |
| Register Banks | 4 banks $\times$ 8 = **32** bytes | 00H–1FH |
| Baud Rate (Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Serial port |
| Timer Overflow | $\text{Delay} = (65536 - \text{Initial}) \times T_{MC}$ | For 16-bit mode |
| Multiplication Result | $\text{Result} = A \times B$ | B:A (high:low) |
| Division Quotient | $\text{A} = \text{A} / \text{B}$ | Quotient in A, remainder in B |
| Parity Bit (P) | $P = A_0 \oplus A_1 \oplus \ldots \oplus A_7$ | Set if odd number of 1s in A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The 8051's Harvard architecture + bit-addressable I/O + integrated peripherals make it ideal for **real-time control** in washing machines, automotive ECUs, industrial PLCs, and IoT sensor nodes. Its deterministic 1-cycle instruction timing is critical for **time-sensitive control loops**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Assembly Language Programs

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 instruction format is purely **mnemonic-based**. Each line typically follows the pattern:
> **[Label:] Opcode [Operand1 [, Operand2]] [; Comment]**

## 3.1 Program 1: Addition of Two 8-bit Numbers

**Problem:** Add the contents of internal RAM location **30H** and **31H**. Store the result (low byte + carry if any) in **32H** and **33H** respectively.

### Algorithm (Exhaustive Steps)
1. Load the value at 30H into the accumulator.
2. Add the value at 31H to the accumulator (with carry using `ADD` or without using `ADD`; we will use `ADD` for unsigned addition without prior carry).
3. Store the sum in 32H.
4. If a carry was generated, load 01H into 33H; else load 00H.
5. End the program.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        MOV     DPTR, #START    ; Not used; placeholder
        ORG     0030H
START:  MOV     A, 30H          ; Step 1: A ← [30H]
        ADD     A, 31H          ; Step 2: A ← A + [31H] (CY set if overflow)
        MOV     32H, A          ; Step 3: Store sum in 32H
        JNC     NO_CARRY        ; Step 4a: If CY = 0, jump
        MOV     33H, #01H       ; Step 4b: Else store carry
        SJMP    END_PRG
NO_CARRY:
        MOV     33H, #00H
END_PRG: SJMP    END_PRG         ; Step 5: Infinite loop to halt
        END
```

### Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation
**Sample inputs:** `30H = A5H`, `31H = 5AH` (Both are 8-bit values)
- Step 1: `A ← A5H`
- Step 2: `A5H + 5AH = FFH`. Since `FFH < 100H`, **CY = 0**.
- Step 3: `[32H] ← FFH`
- Step 4: Since CY = 0, `[33H] ← 00H`
- **Result:** `[32H] = FFH`, `[33H] = 00H` → Sum = 255 (correct)

**Another test:** `30H = FFH`, `31H = 02H`
- `FFH + 02H = 101H` → Result: `A = 01H`, `CY = 1`
- `[32H] = 01H`, `[33H] = 01H` → Sum = 257 (correct, carry stored)

## 3.2 Program 2: Subtraction of Two 8-bit Numbers

**Problem:** Subtract internal RAM location **31H** from **30H**. Store the difference in **32H** and borrow status in **33H**.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, 30H          ; A ← minuend
        SUBB    A, 31H          ; A ← A − [31H] − CY
        MOV     32H, A          ; Store difference
        JNC     NO_BORROW       ; If no borrow (CY=0)
        MOV     33H, #01H       ; Else set borrow
        SJMP    DONE
NO_BORROW:
        MOV     33H, #00H
DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** `SUBB` subtracts **borrow (CY) too**. Always clear CY before subtraction using `CLR C` if you want pure subtraction:
> ```assembly
>         CLR     C
>         MOV     A, 30H
>         SUBB    A, 31H
> ```

### Numerical Example: `30H = 50H`, `31H = 80H`
- `50H − 80H = −30H` (in 2's complement = `D0H` in 8-bit)
- `CY = 1` (borrow occurred)
- Result: `[32H] = D0H` (signed: −48), `[33H] = 01H` (borrow flag)

## 3.3 Program 3: Multiplication of Two 8-bit Numbers

**Problem:** Multiply values in internal RAM **30H** and **31H**. The 16-bit result is stored in **B:A** automatically by the `MUL AB` instruction.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, 30H          ; A ← multiplicand
        MOV     B, 31H          ; B ← multiplier
        MUL     AB              ; B:A ← A × B
        MOV     32H, A          ; Store low byte
        MOV     33H, B          ; Store high byte
DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

### Numerical Example: `30H = 0AH` (10), `31H = 0CH` (12)
- `MUL AB`: `A = 10`, `B = 12` → Result = 120 = `78H`
- Low byte: `A = 78H`, High byte: `B = 00H`
- **Stored:** `[32H] = 78H`, `[33H] = 00H`

**Example with overflow:** `30H = FFH` (255), `31H = FFH` (255)
- `255 × 255 = 65025 = FE01H`
- `A = 01H` (low), `B = FEH` (high)
- **Stored:** `[32H] = 01H`, `[33H] = FEH` ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **`MUL AB` rule:** If the result > 255 (i.e., high byte B ≠ 0), the **OV flag is set**.

## 3.4 Program 4: Division of Two 8-bit Numbers

**Problem:** Divide value at **30H** by value at **31H**. Store quotient in **32H** and remainder in **33H**.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, 30H          ; Dividend
        MOV     B, 31H          ; Divisor
        DIV     AB              ; A ← quotient, B ← remainder
        MOV     32H, A          ; Store quotient
        MOV     33H, B          ; Store remainder
DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

### Numerical Example: `30H = 0FFH` (255), `31H = 0AH` (10)
- `255 ÷ 10 = 25` remainder `5`
- `A = 19H` (25), `B = 05H` (5)
- **Stored:** `[32H] = 19H`, `[33H] = 05H` ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`DIV AB` special case:** If `B = 00H` (divide by zero), the result `A` is **undefined** and the **OV flag is set**.

## 3.5 Program 5: Block Data Transfer (Internal RAM → Internal RAM)

**Problem:** Transfer a block of **N bytes** starting at internal RAM location **30H** to a destination block starting at **60H**. Assume the count is stored in **20H**.

### Algorithm
1. Load source pointer in R0.
2. Load destination pointer in R1.
3. Load counter in R2.
4. Loop: Move `[R0]` to `A`, then `A` to `[R1]`. Increment both pointers, decrement counter.
5. Continue until counter = 0.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; R0 ← source start address
        MOV     R1, #60H        ; R1 ← destination start address
        MOV     R2, 20H         ; R2 ← count
AGAIN:  MOV     A, @R0          ; A ← [source]
        MOV     @R1, A          ; [destination] ← A
        INC     R0              ; Source pointer++
        INC     R1              ; Dest pointer++
        DJNZ    R2, AGAIN       ; Decrement R2; if R2 ≠ 0, jump to AGAIN
DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

### Numerical Example
**Source block (30H–33H):** `11H, 22H, 33H, 44H`  
**Count at 20H:** `04H`  
**Destination block (60H–63H):** Initially `00H, 00H, 00H, 00H`

#### Exhaustive Trace
| Cycle | R0 | R1 | R2 | A | [R0]→[R1] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | 30H | 60H | 04H | — | — |
| 1 | 30H | 60H | 04H | 11H | 60H ← 11H |
| 2 | 31H | 61H | 03H | 22H | 61H ← 22H |
| 3 | 32H | 62H | 02H | 33H | 62H ← 33H |
| 4 | 33H | 63H | 01H | 44H | 63H ← 44H |
| 5 | 34H | 64H | 00H | — | DJNZ: R2=0, exit loop |

**Final destination (60H–63H):** `11H, 22H, 33H, 44H` ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Why R0 and R1 for pointers?** Because only **R0 and R1** can be used for **register indirect addressing** of internal RAM. Other registers (R2–R7) cannot.

## 3.6 Program 6: Block Data Transfer (Code Memory Look-up Table)

**Problem:** Read 5 bytes from code memory (program ROM) starting at address **0200H** and copy to internal RAM starting at **30H**.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0200H    ; DPTR ← source in code memory
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; R0 ← destination in RAM
        MOV     R2, #05         ; Counter
LOOP:   CLR     A
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR      ; A ← [DPTR] (read from code memory)
        MOV     @R0, A          ; Store in RAM
        INC     DPTR            ; Source pointer++
        INC     R0              ; Dest pointer++
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
DONE:   SJMP    DONE

        ORG     0200H           ; Data in code memory
        DB      11H, 22H, 33H, 44H, 55H
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`MOVC` (Move Code):** Used specifically to read from **program memory (ROM)**. Always uses either `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` indexed addressing.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls

| Operation | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Addition w/o carry | Using `ADD` is fine; using `ADDC` requires pre-clearing CY | `CLR C` then `ADDC` for multi-precision |
| Subtraction | Forgetting `SUBB` includes CY | Use `SUBB` only after `CLR C` for first byte |
| Multiply | Expecting 8-bit result | Result is 16-bit: B:A |
| Divide | Checking A instead of B for remainder | Quotient → A, **Remainder → B** |
| Block transfer | Using R2–R7 for pointers | Only R0/R1 valid for `@Ri` addressing |
| MOVC | Using immediate operand | Must use indexed: `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Internal Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[CPU ALU 8-bit] --> A1[Accumulator A]
    A0 --> A2[B Register]
    A0 --> A3[PSW Flags]
    A0 --> A4[Temp Registers]
    B0[Program Counter PC 16-bit] --> A0
    B1[DPTR 16-bit] --> A0
    B2[Stack Pointer SP 8-bit] --> A0
    C0[Oscillator Circuit] --> C1[Clock Generator]
    C1 --> A0
    D0[Program Memory 4KB ROM] --> D1[0000H to 0FFFH]
    D0 --> A0
    E0[Internal Data RAM 128B] --> E1[Register Banks 00H-1FH]
    E0 --> E2[Bit Addressable 20H-2FH]
    E0 --> E3[General Purpose 30H-7FH]
    F0[SFR Area 80H-FFH] --> F1[Port Latches P0 P1 P2 P3]
    F0 --> F2[Timer Control TCON TMOD]
    F0 --> F3[Serial Port SCON SBUF]
    F0 --> F4[Interrupt IE IP]
    G0[Timer 0 16-bit] --> G1[TH0 TL0]
    H0[Timer 1 16-bit] --> H1[TH1 TL1]
    I0[Serial Port UART] --> A0
    J0[External Bus Interface] --> J1[Address Bus 16-bit]
    J0 --> J2[Data Bus 8-bit]
    J0 --> J3[Control Signals PSEN ALE EA RD WR]
    K0[I/O Port 0 P0] --> A0
    K1[I/O Port 1 P1] --> A0
    K2[I/O Port 2 P2] --> A0
    K3[I/O Port 3 P3] --> A0
```

## 4.2 Memory Organization Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PROG["PROGRAM MEMORY 64KB Total"]
        P1[0000H - Reset Vector]
        P2[0003H - INT0 ISR]
        P3[000BH - T0 ISR]
        P4[0013H - INT1 ISR]
        P5[001BH - T1 ISR]
        P6[0023H - Serial ISR]
        P7[On-chip ROM 4KB 0000H-0FFFH]
        P8[External ROM 1000H-FFFFH]
    end

    subgraph DATA["DATA MEMORY 64KB External plus 128B Internal"]
        subgraph IRAM["Internal RAM 128B 00H-7FH"]
            R0[Reg Banks 00H-1FH]
            R1[Bit Addr 20H-2FH]
            R2[General 30H-7FH]
        end
        XDATA[External XDATA 64KB 0000H-FFFFH via MOVX]
    end

    subgraph SFR["SFR Area 80H-FFH"]
        S1[80H P0]
        S2[81H SP]
        S3[82H-83H DPTR]
        S4[88H TCON]
        S5[89H TMOD]
        S6[90H P1]
        S7[98H SCON]
        S8[A0H P2]
        S9[A8H IE]
        S10[B0H P3]
        S11[D0H PSW]
        S12[E0H A]
        S13[F0H B]
    end
```

## 4.3 I/O Port Pin Structure (Port 1 Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[CPU Data Bus D0-D7] --> B0[Port Latch D-Type FlipFlop]
    B0 --> C0[Internal Pull-up Resistor]
    C0 --> D0[Output Driver]
    D0 --> E0[Pin P1.x]
    E0 --> F0[Input Buffer]
    F0 --> G0[Read Pin Logic]
    B0 --> G0
    B0 --> H0[Read Latch Logic]
    I0[Instruction Decoder] --> B0
    E0 --> J0[External Device]
```

## 4.4 Execution Flow of `MOV A, 30H` Instruction

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Fetch opcode from code memory] --> S1[Decode: MOV direct to A]
    S1 --> S2[Fetch second byte: address 30H]
    S2 --> S3[Read internal RAM at 30H]
    S3 --> S4[Place data on internal bus]
    S4 --> S5[Load into Accumulator A]
    S5 --> S6[Update parity flag P in PSW]
    S6 --> S7[Increment PC by 2]
    S7 --> S8[Fetch next instruction]
```

## 4.5 Program Flow — Block Data Transfer (from §3.5)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[START] --> A1[Initialize R0 source ptr]
    A1 --> A2[Initialize R1 dest ptr]
    A2 --> A3[Load count in R2]
    A3 --> A4[R2 = 0]
    A4 -->|Yes| A5[END]
    A4 -->|No| B1[Read source byte via R0]
    B1 --> B2[Write to dest via R1]
    B2 --> B3[Increment R0]
    B3 --> B4[Increment R1]
    B4 --> B5[DJNZ R2: decrement and check]
    B5 --> A4
```

## 4.6 Addressing Modes Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[8051 Addressing Mode Selection] --> M1[Immediate Mode]
    ROOT --> M2[Register Mode]
    ROOT --> M3[Direct Mode]
    ROOT --> M4[Register Indirect Mode]
    ROOT --> M5[Indexed Mode]
    ROOT --> M6[Implied Mode]

    M1 --> M1A[MOV A #25H]
    M2 --> M2A[MOV A R0]
    M3 --> M3A[MOV A 30H]
    M4 --> M4A[MOV A @R0]
    M5 --> M5A[MOVC A @A plus DPTR]
    M6 --> M6A[CLR A or INC DPTR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why P0 needs external pull-up:** Unlike P1, P2, P3, the P0 output transistors are **open-drain** (no internal pull-up). When outputting a logic '1', the pin floats and must be pulled high by an external resistor. This is a common exam question worth 3 marks.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the Harvard architecture of 8051 with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
The 8051 uses the **Harvard architecture** in which the program memory (code) and data memory are **physically and logically separate** with **independent address buses and data buses**. This allows **simultaneous fetching of an instruction (from code memory via PC)** and **accessing of data (from data memory via DPTR or R0/R1)** in a single machine cycle, increasing throughput. The code memory is accessed using **PSEN** (Program Store Enable), while data memory uses **RD** and **WR** signals. The total program memory is **64 KB** and data memory is **64 KB** external + **128 bytes** internal.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any six Special Function Registers of 8051 with their addresses and functions.**

**Model Answer:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| ACC (A) | E0H | Accumulator — primary data register |
| B | F0H | Used in MUL and DIV instructions |
| PSW | D0H | Contains flags (CY, AC, OV, P) and bank select bits |
| SP | 81H | Stack Pointer — points to top of stack |
| DPTR (DPL+DPH) | 82H/83H | 16-bit Data Pointer for external memory |
| TCON | 88H | Timer/Counter control register |

(Any six are acceptable with the table format.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of 8051 microcontroller. List its salient features.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 microcontroller consists of the following functional blocks (refer to §4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **8-bit CPU** with ALU, Accumulator, B register, PSW, and temporary registers.
2. **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** — initialized to 0000H on reset.
3. **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)** — for accessing external data memory.
4. **4 KB on-chip ROM** for program storage (expandable to 64 KB).
5. **128 bytes on-chip RAM** for data storage.
6. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3).
7. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1).
8. **Full-duplex UART** for serial communication.
9. **Interrupt controller** with 5 sources and 2 priority levels.
10. **Oscillator and clock generator** (external crystal).

**Salient Features (any 8 needed for full marks):**
- 8-bit data bus, 16-bit address bus.
- 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM on-chip.
- 32 programmable I/O lines.
- 64 KB external code + 64 KB external data memory.
- 2 × 16-bit timers/counters.
- 5 interrupt sources, 2 priority levels.
- Full-duplex serial port.
- Bit-addressable I/O and RAM.
- Boolean (bit) processor for single-bit operations.
- 111 instructions, 1–4 machine cycles each.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the PSW register of 8051 in detail. How is the register bank selected using PSW?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an 8-bit Special Function Register located at address **D0H**. It is bit-addressable and contains the following bits:

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | CY | D7H | **Carry Flag** — set on addition overflow or borrow in subtraction |
| 6 | AC | D6H | **Auxiliary Carry** — set if carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (for BCD) |
| 5 | F0 | D5H | **User Flag 0** — general purpose |
| 4 | RS1 | D4H | **Register Bank Select 1** |
| 3 | RS0 | D3H | **Register Bank Select 0** |
| 2 | OV | D2H | **Overflow Flag** — set on signed overflow |
| 1 | — | D1H | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | P | D0H | **Parity Flag** — set if odd number of 1s in A |

**Register Bank Selection using RS1:RS0:**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H (default after reset) |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

For example, to use Bank 2, we execute:
```assembly
        SETB    PSW.4         ; RS1 = 1
        CLR     PSW.3         ; RS0 = 0
```

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — Internal Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the various addressing modes of 8051 with suitable examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 supports **5 distinct addressing modes** (plus implied), which determine how the operand is accessed:

**1. Immediate Addressing**
- The operand (8- or 16-bit) is part of the instruction itself.
- Example: `MOV A, #25H` — loads A with 25H.
- Example: `MOV DPTR, #2000H` — loads DPTR with 16-bit constant.

**2. Register Addressing**
- Operands are in one of the eight working registers R0–R7 (currently selected bank).
- Example: `MOV A, R3` — copies R3 to A.
- Example: `ADD A, R5` — adds R5 to A.

**3. Direct Addressing**
- The instruction contains the 8-bit address of the operand in internal RAM (00H–7FH) or SFR (80H–FFH).
- Example: `MOV A, 30H` — loads A with content of RAM location 30H.
- Example: `MOV P1, A` — writes A to Port 1 SFR (90H).

**4. Register Indirect Addressing**
- The address of the operand is held in R0 or R1 (for internal RAM) or DPTR (for external data memory).
- Example: `MOV A, @R0` — loads A with content of RAM pointed to by R0.
- Example: `MOVX A, @DPTR` — reads external memory at DPTR.
- Example: `MOVX @R0, A` — writes A to external memory (P2 supplies upper address, R0 supplies lower).

**5. Indexed Addressing**
- Used for look-up tables in code memory. Effective address = base + A.
- Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — reads code memory at (DPTR + A).
- Example: `MOVC A, @A+PC` — reads code memory at (PC + A + 1).

**6. Implied (Inherent) Addressing**
- No explicit operand; the instruction operates on a specific register (usually A or DPTR).
- Example: `CLR A` — clears A.
- Example: `INC DPTR` — increments 16-bit DPTR.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an 8051 ALP to multiply two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 30H and 31H. Store the 16-bit result in locations 32H (low byte) and 33H (high byte).**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm:**
1. Load the first operand (multiplicand) into the Accumulator.
2. Load the second operand (multiplier) into the B register.
3. Execute the `MUL AB` instruction — the result is automatically placed in B:A (16-bit).
4. Store the low byte (A) into 32H and the high byte (B) into 33H.

**Assembly Language Program:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        MOV     A, 30H          ; A ← multiplicand
        MOV     B, 31H          ; B ← multiplier
        MUL     AB              ; B:A ← A × B (16-bit result)
        MOV     32H, A          ; Store low byte in 32H
        MOV     33H, B          ; Store high byte in 33H
DONE:   SJMP    DONE            ; Stop (infinite loop)
        END                     ; End of program
```

**Step-by-Step Trace Example:**
Assume `[30H] = 0AH` (10 decimal) and `[31H] = 0CH` (12 decimal).

| Step | Instruction | A | B | [32H] | [33H] | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | 0AH | — | — | — | A loaded with multiplicand |
| 2 | `MOV B, 31H` | 0AH | 0CH | — | — | B loaded with multiplier |
| 3 | `MUL AB` | 78H | 00H | — | — | 10×12 = 120 = 78H; B=0, A=78H |
| 4 | `MOV 32H, A` | 78H | 00H | 78H | — | Low byte saved |
| 5 | `MOV 33H, B` | 78H | 00H | 78H | 00H | High byte saved |

**Final Result:** `[32H] = 78H`, `[33H] = 00H` → Decimal 120 ✓

**Another Example with overflow:** `[30H] = FFH` (255), `[31H] = FFH` (255)
- `MUL AB` → `B = FEH`, `A = 01H` (255×255 = 65025 = FE01H)
- `[32H] = 01H`, `[33H] = FEH` ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (commonly lose marks):**
> 1. **Wrong register used for multiplication** — Students sometimes write `MUL A, 30H` which is **invalid syntax**. Only `MUL AB` is correct.
> 2. **Forgetting that result is 16-bit** — Storing only A in one location loses the high byte and earns 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Confusing quotient/remainder in division** — Quotient goes to A, **remainder to B** (not the other way).
> 4. **Using SUBB without clearing CY** — Always precede the first `SUBB` with `CLR C` for single-byte subtraction.
> 5. **Block transfer pointer register error** — Only R0 and R1 can be used with `@Ri`; using R2–R7 is a syntax error.
> 6. **Forgetting `#` in immediate operands** — `MOV A, 25H` means *direct* addressing; `MOV A, #25H` is *immediate*. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU answer sheets.
> 7. **Stack pointer not initialized** — SP starts at 07H; for safe stack usage, initialize with `MOV SP, #60H` or similar.
> 8. **Missing `END` directive** — Without `END`, the assembler doesn't know where program code stops.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick Revision Checklist

- ✅ **8051 is 8-bit Harvard architecture**, 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs machine cycle.
- ✅ **4 KB on-chip ROM** (0000H–0FFFH), **128 B on-chip RAM** (00H–7FH), **64 KB external code/data each**.
- ✅ **4 I/O ports (P0–P3) = 32 I/O lines**. P0 has **no internal pull-up** (needs external 10kΩ).
- ✅ **SFRs occupy 80H–FFH**; key ones: A (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), DPTR (82H-83H), P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP.
- ✅ **Internal RAM layout:** 00H–1FH (Register Banks), 20H–2FH (Bit-addressable, 128 bits), 30H–7FH (Scratch).
- ✅ **PSW bits:** CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, –, P. RS1:RS0 selects one of 4 register banks.
- ✅ **5 Addressing Modes:** Immediate (#), Register (Rn), Direct (addr), Register Indirect (@R0/@R1/@DPTR), Indexed (@A+DPTR/@A+PC).
- ✅ **111 instructions** organized into Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, Branching, Pseudo.
- ✅ **MUL AB** → 16-bit result in B:A. **DIV AB** → Quotient in A, Remainder in B.
- ✅ **Block transfer** uses R0 (source ptr) + R1 (dest ptr) + R2 (counter) + `DJNZ`.
- ✅ **MOVC** is for code memory (ROM); **MOVX** is for external data memory (XDATA); **MOV** is for internal RAM/SFR.
- ✅ **Stack grows upward**; SP is 8-bit pointing to **top occupied location** (post-increment on PUSH).
- ✅ **Reset vector is 0000H**; all interrupts have fixed vectors (0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H).
- ✅ **SUBB subtracts carry too**; always precede first SUBB with `CLR C`.
- ✅ **Only R0 and R1** can be used for register indirect addressing of internal RAM.
- ✅ **Parity flag (P) = 1** if Accumulator has an **odd** number of 1s.

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Exam Strategy

| Question Type | Focus Area | Time Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| 3-mark (Part A) | Definitions, features, register lists, addressing modes table | 5 min |
| 14-mark (Part B) — (a) | Architecture diagram + features OR PSW + bank selection | 15 min |
| 14-mark (Part B) — (b) | Full assembly program with algorithm + trace | 20 min |

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always write the **algorithm first**, then the **code**, then the **numerical trace** for assembly programs. KTU examiners award partial marks for correct algorithm even if the code has minor syntax errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The 8051 Programming Model

The **8051 Microcontroller** is an 8-bit Harvard-architecture single-chip microcontroller introduced by Intel, featuring a dedicated program memory (ROM) and data memory (RAM) accessed via separate buses. The programming model defines the set of registers, memory spaces, and instruction formats that the programmer manipulates to control hardware peripherals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCPT302 / M2):**
> The 8051 programming model consists of an **8-bit Accumulator (A)**, a **B register**, an **8-bit Program Status Word (PSW)**, a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)**, a **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)**, four **8-bit General Purpose Register Banks (R0–R7 × 4 banks)**, and the **Stack Pointer (SP)**. The Special Function Registers (SFRs) occupy the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses **0x80 to 0xFF**).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Workshop Manager"

Think of the 8051 as a **workshop manager** running a small factory:

- **Accumulator (A)** → The manager's *primary clipboard*: every arithmetic operation passes through it.
- **B Register** → A *secondary scratchpad* used only in multiplication/division.
- **R0–R7 (Register Banks)** → Four *drawer stacks* at the manager's desk. The manager picks one bank at a time (controlled by the RS0/RS1 bits in PSW), so he can switch contexts instantly without losing previous work.
- **PC (Program Counter)** → The manager's *instruction pointer* — pointing to the next instruction on the rulebook.
- **DPTR** → A *two-finger pointer* used to address external data (like fetching items from a far warehouse).
- **SP (Stack Pointer)** → A *stacking tray* — pushes/pops temporary notes during subroutine calls.
- **PSW** → The manager's *status report* (carry-overflow, zero result, current drawer bank, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 is the canonical vehicle for KTU PCCPT302. Mastery of its assembly programming is mandatory — questions frequently ask for *handwritten assembly code*, *delay subroutine design*, and *timer/serial register configuration*.

---

## 1.3 8051 Internal Memory Map

| Address Range | Region | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0x00 – 0x1F | Register Banks (32 B) | 4 banks × 8 registers (R0–R7) |
| 0x20 – 0x2F | Bit-Addressable RAM | 16 bytes → 128 individually-addressable bits |
| 0x30 – 0x7F | General Purpose RAM | 64 bytes of scratchpad storage |
| 0x80 – 0xFF | Special Function Registers (SFR) | TCON, TMOD, SCON, SBUF, IE, IP, P0–P3, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> The SFR area is **not** general-purpose RAM; writing to unused addresses is undefined behavior.

---

## 1.4 8051 Peripherals — Functional Overview

The 8051 integrates four key programmable peripherals on-chip:

1. **Four 8-bit I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** — bidirectional quasi-bidirectional pins.
2. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0, T1)** — for timing, event counting, baud rate generation.
3. **Full-Duplex UART (Serial Port)** — asynchronous or synchronous serial communication.
4. **Interrupt Controller** — 5 sources (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial); covered in detail in Module 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Each peripheral is controlled by a specific SFR:** `TMOD`/`TCON` (Timers), `SCON`/`SBUF` (Serial), `PCON` (Power/Baud), `IE`/`IP` (Interrupts).
