# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Addressing Modes of the 8051

The 8051 supports **five** primary addressing modes — *a guaranteed KTU question in some form every semester*.

| Mode | Syntax Example | Operation | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Immediate** | `MOV A, #25H` | Loads the literal constant 0x25 | Initializing constants |
| **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | Copies R0 into A (no memory access) | Fast register ops |
| **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | Reads internal RAM location 0x30 | Accessing variables |
| **Register-Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | A ← [[R0]] (R0 holds an address) | Pointer-based loops |
| **Indexed** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Code lookup using DPTR base | Table lookups / branch tables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A common 7-mark question asks the student to "Explain the different addressing modes of 8051 with examples." A model answer must include at least one valid instruction per mode.

---

## 2.2 Instruction Set Categories

The 8051 has **111 base instructions** (≈ 255 when including addressing-mode variants):

- **Data Transfer** — `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`, `XCHD`
- **Arithmetic** — `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`, `DA A`
- **Logical** — `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `RL`, `RR`, `RLC`, `RRC`, `SWAP`
- **Boolean (Bit)** — `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`, `MOV C,bit`
- **Program Branch** — `LJMP`, `SJMP`, `AJMP`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`

> [!TIP]
> **Bit-addressable instructions** are a unique 8051 feature. PSW has 8 bit-addressable bits, and the 16 bytes at 0x20–0x2F provide **128 individually addressable bits** with addresses 0x00–0x7F in bit-space.

---

## 2.3 Timers/Counters — TMOD and TCON

### 2.3.1 The TMOD Register (Address 0x89, NOT bit-addressable)

$$\text{TMOD} = \underbrace{\text{GATE}}_{\text{T1}} \underbrace{\text{C}/\bar{\text{T}}}_{\text{T1}} \underbrace{\text{M1}}_{\text{T1}} \underbrace{\text{M0}}_{\text{T1}} \; \underbrace{\text{GATE}}_{\text{T0}} \underbrace{\text{C}/\bar{\text{T}}}_{\text{T0}} \underbrace{\text{M1}}_{\text{T0}} \underbrace{\text{M0}}_{\text{T0}}$$

| Bit | Function | Value Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GATE** | Software/Hardware control | 1 → INTx pin enables timer; 0 → TRx alone enables |
| **C/$\bar{\text{T}}$** | Counter or Timer | 1 → External event counter; 0 → Internal oscillator |
| **M1, M0** | Mode select | 00→Mode 0 (13-bit); 01→Mode 1 (16-bit); 10→Mode 2 (auto-reload 8-bit); 11→Mode 3 (split timer) |

### 2.3.2 The TCON Register (Address 0x88, bit-addressable)

| Bit | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | TF1 | Timer 1 Overflow Flag (set by hardware, cleared by software) |
| 6 | TR1 | Timer 1 Run Control (1 = start) |
| 5 | TF0 | Timer 0 Overflow Flag |
| 4 | TR0 | Timer 0 Run Control |
| 3 | IE1 | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag |
| 2 | IT1 | External Interrupt 1 Type (1=edge, 0=level) |
| 1 | IE0 | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag |
| 0 | IT0 | External Interrupt 0 Type |

---

## 2.4 Timer Operating Modes

| Mode | Width | Behavior | Reload Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mode 0** | 13-bit | Legacy 8048 compatibility; uses 8 lower bits of TH and lower 5 of TL | Software |
| **Mode 1** | 16-bit | Full 16-bit count; rolls over 0x0000 → 0xFFFF | Software |
| **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload | TL counts 0x00–0xFF; on overflow, TL ← TH | Hardware from TH |
| **Mode 3** | Split | T0 becomes two 8-bit timers; T1 halts (often used for baud rate) | Software |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mode 3 is NOT available to Timer 1.** In Mode 3, T0 splits into TL0 (controlled by TR0, flags TF0) and TH0 (controlled by TR1, flags TF1). Timer 1 is effectively disabled — except it can be set to Mode 2 as the *baud rate generator* for the serial port.

---

## 2.5 Serial Communication — SCON and SBUF

### 2.5.1 The SCON Register (Address 0x98, bit-addressable)

$$\text{SCON} = \text{SM0}\;\text{SM1}\;\text{SM2}\;\text{REN}\;\text{TB8}\;\text{RB8}\;\text{TI}\;\text{RI}$$

| Bit | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 |
| 6 | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (modes 2 & 3) |
| 4 | REN | Receive Enable (1 → start receiving) |
| 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (modes 2 & 3) |
| 2 | RB8 | 9th received bit (modes 2 & 3) |
| 1 | TI | Transmit Interrupt Flag (set after last bit sent) |
| 0 | RI | Receive Interrupt Flag (set after byte received) |

### 2.5.2 Serial Mode Selection (SM0, SM1)

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ (fixed) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 8-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1 overflow) |
| 1 | 0 | 2 | 9-bit UART | $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{32}$ or $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{64}$ |
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 9-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1 overflow) |

### 2.5.3 The SBUF Register (Address 0x99)

Physically two registers sharing one address: writing to SBUF loads the **transmit buffer**, reading SBUF reads the **receive buffer**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU board problems on Timers/Counters and Serial Communication.

### Timer Frequency & Delay Formulas

$$\boxed{f_{\text{timer}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{timer}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{timer}}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Timer count cycles} = 2^{n} - \text{initial value}}$$

where $n$ = mode width (13, 16, or 8).

$$\boxed{\text{Delay} = \text{Timer count cycles} \times T_{\text{timer}}}$$

For Mode 1 (16-bit):

$$\text{Delay} = (65536 - X) \times \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$$

### Baud Rate Formulas

For **Serial Mode 1 / Mode 3** (Timer 1 in auto-reload, Mode 2):

$$\boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}}$$

Solving for TH1:

$$\boxed{\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}}$$

For **Serial Mode 2**:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{64} \times f_{\text{osc}}$$

| Serial Mode | Baud Rate Expression | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mode 0 | $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | Shift-register expansion (e.g., 74373) |
| Mode 1 / 3 | $\frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | PC communication at 9600, 19200 |
| Mode 2 | $\frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{64}$ | High-speed internal baud |

> [!WARNING]
> **Boundary Note:** In the formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols (if any) represent absolute value. In the markdown table syntax, use the typographic representation $\text{TH1}$ for clarity — **never use the literal `|` pipe** inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the table parser.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Timers** are the heartbeat of every embedded system: scheduling tasks (RTOS tick), generating PWM, debouncing buttons, measuring pulse width, and providing precise software delays.
- **Serial Communication** forms the backbone of device-to-device communication: GPS modules, GSM modems, Bluetooth (HC-05), and PC debug terminals all speak UART — a sub-feature of the 8051's serial port.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Timer Delay Calculation (Mode 1)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 3/7 marker):**
> Calculate the value to be loaded into the Timer 0 (TH0, TL0) registers to generate a delay of **10 ms** using Timer 0 in **Mode 1** on an 8051 system with crystal frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592$ MHz. Use Mode 1, software control, internal oscillator, and verify with the flag-clear convention.

### Step 1 — Compute the Timer Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to derive the internal machine cycle clock:

$$f_{\text{machine}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 921600 \text{ Hz}$$

$$T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{machine}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \;\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Required Count for 10 ms

The total number of timer increments needed to span the desired delay:

$$N_{\text{required}} = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{\text{machine}}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 9216 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Initial 16-bit Timer Value

In **Mode 1**, the timer is 16-bit, so the total range is $2^{16} = 65536$. The initial value to preload is:

$$X = 2^{16} - N_{\text{required}} = 65536 - 9216 = 56320$$

Convert to hexadecimal:

$$56320 = 0\text{xDC00}$$

### Step 4 — Split into TH0 and TL0

$$\text{TH0} = 0\text{xDC}, \qquad \text{TL0} = 0\text{x00}$$

### Step 5 — Verification (Reverse Calculation)

$$N = 65536 - 56320 = 9216$$

$$\text{Delay} = 9216 \times 1.085\;\mu\text{s} = 9.99936 \;\text{ms} \approx 10\;\text{ms} \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **11.0592 MHz crystal is preferred** over 12 MHz in serial communication because it yields *exact* integer baud rates (9600, 19200). For 12 MHz crystals, delays will be slightly off-integer but the formulas still apply.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Baud Rate Computation for Serial Mode 1

**Problem Statement:**
> An 8051 system with $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592$ MHz must communicate with a PC at **9600 baud** using Serial Mode 1. Compute the **TH1 reload value** assuming SMOD = 0, and verify the resulting baud rate.

### Step 1 — Apply the Serial Mode 1 Baud Rate Formula

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

Substitute $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}$, SMOD = 0, Baud = 9600:

$$9600 = \frac{2^{0} \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 2 — Isolate the (256 − TH1) Term

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{2^{0} \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot 9600}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3686400}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = 3$$

### Step 3 — Solve for TH1

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0\text{xFD}$$

### Step 4 — Verification

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1 \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot (256 - 253)} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1152} = 9600 \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** When students forget to set `PCON.7` (SMOD), they get exactly half the intended baud rate. **Always state the SMOD value** in your answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Timer 1 in Mode 2 as Baud-Rate Generator

In standard 8051 serial-port programming, Timer 1 is configured in **Mode 2 (auto-reload 8-bit)** purely as a baud-rate generator. This avoids the manual reload burden in the ISR.

**Configuration block (model assembly):**

```asm
MOV  TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload), C/T=0
MOV  TH1,  #0FDH    ; Reload value for 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz
MOV  SCON, #50H     ; Serial Mode 1, REN enabled
MOV  PCON, #00H     ; SMOD = 0
SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1
```

### Cross-Reference: How TH1 = 0xFD maps to N = 3 machine cycles

$$(256 - \text{TH1}) = (256 - 253) = 3 \text{ timer ticks per baud}$$

$$T_{\text{baud}} = 3 \times 1.085\;\mu\text{s} = 3.255\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{3.255\;\mu\text{s}} \approx 30720 \text{ (timer overflows/sec)} \times \text{divisor}$$

More precisely, applying the canonical formula with the 16x clock divider implicit in Mode 1:

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$= \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1152} = 9600 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Timer Mode Comparison for KTU Viva

| Criterion | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Counter width | 13-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit (TL) | T0 split into two 8-bit |
| Roll-over value | 0x1FFF | 0xFFFF | 0xFF | 0xFF (each half) |
| Auto-reload? | No | No | Yes (from TH) | No |
| Typical use | Legacy | Long delays / general | **Baud-rate generator** | Special (parallel tasks) |
| Overflow frequency for TH = 0x00 | $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot 8192}$ | $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot 65536}$ | $\frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot 256}$ | Same as Mode 2 for each half |
