# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** (Part A: 3 marks, Part B: 14 marks with sub-parts a and b at 7 marks each). Every solution is provided in full.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List the different addressing modes of the 8051 with one example instruction for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 8051 supports five addressing modes:
1. **Immediate** — operand is a constant. *Example:* `MOV A, #25H` → Loads 0x25 into A.
2. **Register** — operand is in a register R0–R7. *Example:* `MOV A, R0`.
3. **Direct** — operand is an internal RAM/SFR address. *Example:* `MOV A, 30H`.
4. **Register-Indirect** — address of operand is held in R0/R1/DPTR. *Example:* `MOV A, @R0`.
5. **Indexed** — used for code/data table lookup via DPTR or PC. *Example:* `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.

> [!TIP]
> Award full 3 marks only if the student provides *all five* modes with **at least one valid instruction** and a brief one-line meaning. Missing one mode costs ~0.5 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the function of the `TMOD` register. How is Timer 0 configured in Mode 1 for a software-controlled internal timer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`TMOD` (0x89) is a non-bit-addressable SFR that configures the operating mode of **both Timer 0 and Timer 1**.

For Timer 0 in **Mode 1** as a software-controlled internal timer:
- **M1 = 0, M0 = 1** → Mode 1 (16-bit counter)
- **C/$\bar{\text{T}}$ = 0** → Use internal oscillator (timer mode)
- **GATE = 0** → Software control (TR0 alone starts/stops the timer)

Therefore the lower nibble of TMOD = **0001 = 0x1**, and the configuration is:
```asm
MOV TMOD, #01H   ; Timer 0, Mode 1, SW-controlled, internal timer
```

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: each Part B question contains sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each, with a choice. Below are **two alternative 14-mark questions** simulating internal choice.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Draw the TMOD register format and explain each bit in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With an 8051 system running at $f_{\text{osc}} = 12$ MHz, write an assembly program to generate a **square wave of 2 kHz** on P1.0 using Timer 0 in **Mode 1**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

`TMOD` is at SFR address 0x89; it is **not bit-addressable**, so the entire byte must be written in one MOV instruction.

$$\text{TMOD} = \text{T1} \text{ fields} \;|\; \text{T0 fields} = [\text{GATE}][\text{C/}\bar{\text{T}}][\text{M1}][\text{M0}][\text{GATE}][\text{C/}\bar{\text{T}}][\text{M1}][\text{M0}]$$

| Bit Position (D7→D0) | Symbol | Function | Effect of 0 | Effect of 1 |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | GATE (T1) | Hardware gating | TR1 only | TR1 AND INT1 pin |
| 6 | C/$\bar{\text{T}}$ (T1) | Counter/Timer select | Timer | Counter (T1 pin) |
| 5 | M1 (T1) | Mode high | (see mode table) | |
| 4 | M0 (T1) | Mode low | | |
| 3 | GATE (T0) | Hardware gating | TR0 only | TR0 AND INT0 pin |
| 2 | C/$\bar{\text{T}}$ (T0) | Counter/Timer select | Timer | Counter (T0 pin) |
| 1 | M1 (T0) | Mode high | | |
| 0 | M0 (T0) | Mode low | | |

**Mode Encoding (M1, M0):**
- (0, 0) → Mode 0, 13-bit timer
- (0, 1) → Mode 1, 16-bit timer
- (1, 0) → Mode 2, auto-reload 8-bit
- (1, 1) → Mode 3, split timer

(7 marks: 2 for register layout, 3 for bit-wise function, 2 for mode-encoding table.)

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Calculate the required half-period delay.**
Square wave of 2 kHz has period $T = 1/2000 = 500\;\mu\text{s}$.
Half-period (high or low) = $250\;\mu\text{s}$.

**Step 2 — Compute machine cycle period.**

$$T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\;\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Required count for 250 µs.**

$$N = 250\;\mu\text{s} / 1\;\mu\text{s} = 250 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Initial Timer Value (Mode 1, 16-bit).**

$$X = 65536 - 250 = 65286 = 0\text{xFF06}$$

$$\text{TH0} = 0\text{xFF}, \quad \text{TL0} = 0\text{x06}$$

**Step 5 — Assembly Program.**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, SW-controlled
AGAIN:  MOV TH0, #0FFH
        MOV TL0, #006H
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE       ; Wait for overflow
        CLR TR0
        CLR TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
        CPL P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0 (square wave)
        SJMP AGAIN
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if the student forgets to (a) clear TF0 manually (in Mode 1, TF0 is NOT auto-cleared), (b) reload TH0/TL0 in *every* half-cycle (a common mistake — leaving them in for the second half gives a much longer delay), or (c) include `CLR TR0` before reloading values.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the different operating modes of the 8051 serial port with a focus on SCON register bits SM0, SM1, REN, TI, and RI. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the assembly program to send the byte **0x55** serially at **4800 baud** in Serial Mode 1, with $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592$ MHz and SMOD = 0. Compute TH1 and justify its value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

The **SCON** register (0x98) is bit-addressable and has the format:

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | **SM0** | Serial mode bit 0 |
| 6 | **SM1** | Serial mode bit 1 |
| 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor enable (modes 2/3) |
| 4 | **REN** | Receive Enable (1 → enable; start reception) |
| 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (modes 2/3) |
| 2 | RB8 | 9th received bit (modes 2/3) |
| 1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag (set by HW after last bit shifted out) |
| 0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag (set by HW after last bit shifted in) |

**Mode Description:**

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | $f_{\text{osc}} / 12$ |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 8-bit UART (Variable) | Timer 1 overflow rate |
| 1 | 0 | 2 | 9-bit UART | $f_{\text{osc}} / 32$ or $f_{\text{osc}} / 64$ |
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 9-bit UART | Timer 1 overflow rate |

- **REN** is set by software to start receiving; cleared to disable reception.
- **TI** must be **cleared by software** inside the ISR (or polling loop) — the 8051 sets it on transmission completion.
- **RI** must also be **cleared by software** before exiting the receive routine.

(7 marks: 2 for SCON format, 2 for mode table, 1 each for REN/TI/RI explanation.)

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute TH1 using the baud rate formula.**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$4800 = \frac{1 \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot 4800} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1843200} = 6$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 6 = 250 = 0\text{xFA}$$

**Step 2 — Verification.**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 6} = 4800 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Assembly Program.**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV TH1,  #0FAH    ; 4800 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0
        MOV SCON, #50H     ; Mode 1, REN=1, TI=RI=0
        MOV PCON, #00H     ; SMOD = 0
        SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1 (baud clock)

        MOV SBUF, #55H     ; Transmit 0x55

WAIT:   JNB TI, WAIT        ; Wait until transmission complete
        CLR TI              ; Clear TI flag
HERE:   SJMP HERE           ; Done
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners **deduct 2–3 marks** if (a) the student uses `SMOD = 1` without explicitly setting `PCON.7 = 1` in the code, (b) the student writes `MOV SBUF, #055H` (3 hex digits — illegal) instead of `MOV SBUF, #55H`, or (c) the student fails to clear the **TI flag** after the transmission is complete, leading to an infinite loop on the next transmission.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Memorize every bullet. KTU examiners reward precision.**

### ✔ Programming Model Essentials
- 8051 has **8-bit ALU**; internal data bus is 8-bit; external address bus is 16-bit (64 KB program + 64 KB data).
- 4 register banks × 8 registers (R0–R7) located at 0x00–0x1F; selected by **RS0, RS1** in PSW.
- 16 bytes of **bit-addressable RAM** (0x20–0x2F) provide 128 individually addressable bits.
- **SFR space** (0x80–0xFF) overlaps the upper RAM; only defined SFR addresses are usable.

### ✔ Instruction Set & Addressing Modes
- **111 base instructions**; data transfer (`MOV`, `MOVC`, `MOVX`, `XCH`), arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`), logical (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `RL`, `RR`, `SWAP`), bit (`SETB`, `CLR`), and branches (`LJMP`, `SJMP`, `AJMP`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `JZ`, `JNZ`).
- **Five addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register-Indirect, Indexed.
- **Stack**: grows **upward** in 8051 (PUSH increments SP first); default stack starts at 0x07.

### ✔ Timers/Counters
- **Two timers** (T0, T1); configured by **TMOD** (mode + C/T + GATE) and **TCON** (TRx, TFx).
- **Mode 0**: 13-bit; **Mode 1**: 16-bit; **Mode 2**: 8-bit auto-reload (used for baud); **Mode 3**: T0 split into two 8-bit.
- $f_{\text{timer}} = f_{\text{osc}} / 12$; $T_{\text{timer}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$.
- Delay formula: $\text{Delay} = (2^{n} - X) \times 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$.
- **TFx is NOT auto-cleared in Modes 0/1** — software must clear it.

### ✔ Serial Communication
- **SCON** (0x98) controls mode, REN, TI, RI. **SBUF** (0x99) is a shared transmit/receive buffer.
- **Mode 1** (8-bit UART, variable baud) is the most common for PC communication.
- **Timer 1 in Mode 2** is the standard baud-rate generator; reload value TH1 is computed from the baud formula.
- **Baud formula**: $\text{Baud} = 2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{osc}} / [384 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})]$.
- **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred because it gives **exact** integer baud rates at standard values (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200).
- **TI and RI** must be cleared by software; otherwise the program hangs on the next operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** Always end a timer or serial configuration program with the flag-handling step (clear TFx, clear TI/RI) — this single line differentiates a 14-mark answer from a 10-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Structure, Programming, Interfacing of Peripherals - LED (ALP and Embedded C Programming), OLED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Structure, Programming, Interfacing of Peripherals - LED (ALP and Embedded C Programming), OLED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interrupt Structure – The Heartbeat of Real-Time Embedded Systems

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> **Interrupt** is an asynchronous external or internal event that temporarily halts the main program execution of the microcontroller, forcing the CPU to execute a dedicated routine known as the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. Upon completion of the ISR, the processor resumes the main program from the exact instruction where it was interrupted, provided the return address was saved on the stack.

The **Interrupt Structure** refers to the complete hardware-software architecture that governs how interrupts are generated, prioritized, acknowledged, serviced, and returned from. In the **8051 family** (the canonical KTU reference), this structure comprises:

- **TCON (Timer/Counter Control Register)** – holds IT0, IT1 (interrupt type), IE0, IE1 (interrupt edge flags), TR0, TR1 (run control).
- **IE (Interrupt Enable Register)** – master switch EA + individual enables (EX0, EX1, ET0, ET1, ES).
- **IP (Interrupt Priority Register)** – assigns high/low priority (PX0, PX1, PT0, PT1, PS).
- **Hardware vector table** at code addresses 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT**: The 8051 has **5 interrupt sources** with a **fixed 2-level priority** structure. Default (natural) priority order: **External 0 → Timer 0 → External 1 → Timer 1 → Serial Port**.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are cooking dinner (the **main program**). Suddenly, the doorbell rings (an **interrupt**). You pause cooking, save the recipe step mentally (the **stack push**), answer the door, and once done, you resume exactly from where you left off. If a phone call (a **higher priority interrupt**) comes while answering the door, you may pause the door-answering, take the urgent call, and then return. This is precisely how an embedded CPU handles real-world asynchronous events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping for KTU Examinations**:
> - **Polling** → CPU keeps asking "Is there an event?" (wasteful, blocking).
> - **Interrupt** → Hardware notifies CPU only when an event occurs (efficient, event-driven).
> - **ISR** → The "door-answering" routine.
> - **Vector Address** → The "address on the door" telling CPU where the ISR lives.

## 1.2 LED Interfacing – The "Hello World" of Embedded Systems

### Formal Definition

**Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a **p-n junction semiconductor** device that emits photons (light) when forward-biased, following the principle of **electroluminescence**. The typical forward voltage ($V_F$) of a standard LED is **1.8 V to 3.2 V** (color-dependent) with a nominal forward current ($I_F$) of **10 mA to 20 mA**.

An LED is interfaced to a microcontroller through a **current-limiting resistor** $R$ computed via:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$$

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of a microcontroller pin as a **water tap (GPIO)**. The LED is a **bulb** that needs a controlled amount of current to glow safely. The current-limiting resistor is the **flow restrictor** that prevents the bulb from burning out. The microcontroller can turn the tap **ON (Logic 1)** or **OFF (Logic 0)**, making the bulb blink.

## 1.3 OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode) – Modern Visual Output

### Formal Definition

**OLED** is a flat-panel display technology where an **organic compound film** sandwiched between two electrodes (anode and cathode) emits light in response to an electric current. Unlike LCD, OLED is **self-emissive** (no backlight required), offering higher contrast ratios, faster response times, and wider viewing angles.

For microcontroller interfacing, common OLED modules are **SSD1306-based 0.96" displays** (128×64 pixels) communicating via **I²C** (2-wire) or **SPI** (4-wire).

> [!NOTE]
> **SSD1306 OLED** is the de facto KTU-referenced display because it is inexpensive, I²C-friendly, and widely supported in embedded C libraries.

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of OLED as a **digital canvas** made of millions of **tiny individually-controlled bulbs** (pixels). Each pixel is a small LED that can glow independently. Sending a stream of bytes (data frames) over I²C is like sending an artist a painting instruction list — "this pixel ON, this pixel OFF" — to form a complete picture.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Coverage Statement (Module 2)**: The KTU Module 2 demands understanding of (a) 8051 Interrupt Structure including IE and IP registers, (b) ALP and Embedded C programs for LED blinking, LED patterns, and switch-LED combinations, and (c) OLED interfacing with I²C protocol fundamentals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Interrupt Structure – Architectural Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 The Interrupt Enable (IE) Register – Byte-Addressable at 0xA8

The **IE register** is the master control for enabling or disabling individual interrupt sources. It is **bit-addressable**, making it exam-friendly.

| Bit | Symbol | Function | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **EA** | Global Enable (must be 1 for any interrupt) | 0xAF |
| 6 | – | Reserved | 0xAE |
| 5 | – | Reserved | 0xAD |
| 4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port interrupt | 0xAC |
| 3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt | 0xAB |
| 2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 (P3.3) | 0xAA |
| 1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt | 0xA9 |
| 0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 (P3.2) | 0xA8 |

**Configuration Example**: To enable only External Interrupt 0:

$$IE = 0x81 \quad \text{(i.e., } EA=1, EX0=1, \text{ all others 0)}$$

### 2.1.2 The Interrupt Priority (IP) Register – Byte-Addressable at 0xB8

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | – | Reserved |
| 6 | – | Reserved |
| 5 | – | Reserved |
| 4 | **PS** | Serial Port priority |
| 3 | **PT1** | Timer 1 priority |
| 2 | **PX1** | External 1 priority |
| 1 | **PT0** | Timer 0 priority |
| 0 | **PX0** | External 0 priority |

> **Logic '1' = HIGH priority**; **Logic '0' = LOW priority**.

### 2.1.3 The TCON Register – Trigger Source Control

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | **IE1** | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag (set by hardware) |
| 2 | **IT1** | Interrupt 1 Type: 1 = Edge-triggered, 0 = Level-triggered |
| 1 | **IE0** | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag |
| 0 | **IT0** | Interrupt 0 Type: 1 = Edge-triggered, 0 = Level-triggered |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU FAVORITE QUESTION**: Difference between **Level-Triggered** and **Edge-Triggered** interrupts.
> - **Level-Triggered (ITx = 0)**: Interrupt request persists as long as pin is LOW. CPU checks pin state.
> - **Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1)**: Interrupt generated only on a **falling (HIGH-to-LOW) transition**. Hardware latches the event in IEx flag.

### 2.1.4 Interrupt Vector Addresses (Fixed by Hardware)

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Priority (Default) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | – | 0x0000 | Highest |
| External 0 | IE0 | 0x0003 | 1 |
| Timer 0 | TF0 | 0x000B | 2 |
| External 1 | IE1 | 0x0013 | 3 |
| Timer 1 | TF1 | 0x001B | 4 |
| Serial Port | TI/RI | 0x0023 | 5 (Lowest) |

## 2.2 LED Interfacing – Hardware and Software Theory

### 2.2.1 Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation

Given $V_{CC} = 5V$, $V_F = 2V$, $I_F = 10 mA$:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 - 2}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 300 \Omega$$

The **closest standard E12 value** is **330 Ω**, universally adopted in KTU lab kits.

### 2.2.2 Sourcing vs. Sinking Current

| Mode | Microcontroller Pin | LED Connection | Logic Level to Turn ON |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Sourcing** | Pin drives current **OUT** | Anode to Pin, Cathode to GND via R | Logic **HIGH (1)** |
| **Current Sinking** | Pin absorbs current **IN** | Cathode to Pin, Anode to Vcc via R | Logic **LOW (0)** |

> **KTU Tip**: 8051 GPIO pins can source ~10 mA per pin. Sinking is more reliable — most KTU practical exams prefer **active-low LED configuration**.

## 2.3 OLED Interfacing (SSD1306) – I²C Fundamentals

### 2.3.1 I²C Bus Architecture

OLED modules use the **Inter-Integrated Circuit (I²C)** bus — a **2-wire serial protocol** invented by Philips:

- **SDA (Serial Data)** → Bidirectional data line (P3.0 on 8051 if bit-banged).
- **SCL (Serial Clock)** → Clock line generated by master (P3.1 if bit-banged).

> The 8051 does **NOT** have a dedicated I²C hardware controller; therefore, **GPIO bit-banging** is the standard KTU pedagogical approach.

### 2.3.2 SSD1306 Default I²C Address

$$0x3D \quad \text{(sometimes } 0x3C\text{)}$$

The address is **7-bit** = `0111101` = `0x3D`, with R/W bit appended → write address = `0x7A`, read = `0x7B`.

### 2.3.3 Display RAM (GDDRAM) Layout

- **128 columns × 64 rows** = **8192 pixels** = **1024 bytes** of GDDRAM.
- Each byte controls **8 vertical pixels** (page mode).
- Organized into **8 pages** (Page 0 to Page 7), each controlling 8 rows.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Register | Unit | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED Series Resistor | $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$ | Ω | $V_{CC} = 5V$, $I_F = 10$–$20 mA$ |
| 2 | LED Power Dissipation | $P_{LED} = V_F \times I_F$ | mW | Typical: 20–60 mW |
| 3 | 8051 Crystal Frequency | $f_{OSC}$ | Hz | Common: 11.0592 MHz (serial-friendly) |
| 4 | Machine Cycle | $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$ | seconds | For 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 μs |
| 5 | Timer Delay | $T_{delay} = (65536 - X) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | X = initial timer value |
| 6 | Timer Initial Value | $X = 65536 - (T_{delay}/T_{MC})$ | integer | For 50 ms delay at 11.0592 MHz → X = 46085 (0xB3FD) |
| 7 | I²C Bit Rate (Standard Mode) | 100 kbps | bps | Fast Mode: 400 kbps |
| 8 | OLED GDDRAM Size | $128 \times 64 / 8$ | bytes | = **1024 bytes** |
| 9 | Interrupt Vector Size | 8 bytes each | bytes | LJMP used to extend ISR region |
| 10 | Baud Rate (Timer 1) | $BR = (2^{SMOD}/32) \times (f_{OSC}/(12 \times 32 \times (256 - TH1)))$ | bps | SMOD in PCON |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZATION PRIORITY (KTU Exams)**: The 3 most-frequently tested formulas are **(1) LED Resistor**, **(5) Timer Delay**, and **(10) Baud Rate**. Master these in the order presented.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Interrupt-driven keypad scan in dashboards.
- **Medical Devices**: OLED display showing patient vitals in real time.
- **Industrial IoT**: LED indicators on PLCs; OLED HMIs for machine status.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Washing machine status LEDs; smartwatch OLED screens.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Programming, and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Timer Delay Calculation for LED Blinking

> **Problem**: An 8051 system with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 MHz$ must produce a **50 ms** delay using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Compute the initial timer value $X$.

### Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle Period

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \mu s$$

> **Logic**: The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to produce one machine cycle. At 11.0592 MHz, each machine cycle is approximately 1.085 μs.

### Step 2 — Count the Number of Timer Increments Needed

$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46082.95 \approx 46083 \text{ counts}$$

> **Logic**: The 16-bit Timer 0 increments once per machine cycle. To span 50 ms, it must increment 46083 times.

### Step 3 — Compute the Initial Timer Value

$$X = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - 46083 = 19453$$

> **Logic**: The 16-bit timer overflows (sets TF0) after counting from $X$ to $2^{16}$. So $X$ is the starting value.

### Step 4 — Convert $X$ to TH0 and TL0 Bytes

$$\begin{aligned}
TH0 &= \text{High Byte} = \lfloor 19453 / 256 \rfloor = 75 \;\; (= 0x4B) \\
TL0 &= \text{Low Byte} = 19453 \mod 256 = 253 \;\; (= 0xFD)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic**: The 16-bit timer value is split into two 8-bit registers. The high byte is integer division; the low byte is the remainder.

### Step 5 — Final Verification

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Counts} &= 2^{16} - 19453 = 46083 \\
\text{Delay} &= 46083 \times 1.085 \mu s = 50.0 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap**: Students often use **12 MHz** instead of 11.0592 MHz. At 12 MHz, $T_{MC} = 1 \mu s$ exactly, making the math simpler. Always **state your assumed $f_{OSC}$** explicitly in the answer.

---

## 3.2 Program 1 — LED Blinking in Assembly Language (ALP)

> **Objective**: Blink an LED connected to **P1.0** with a 50 ms ON/OFF cycle.

```assembly
        ORG 0000H            ; Reset vector
        LJMP MAIN            ; Jump to main program

        ORG 0030H            ; Main program starts here
MAIN:   ACALL DELAY_50MS     ; Call delay subroutine
        CPL P1.0             ; Complement P1.0 (toggle LED)
        SJMP MAIN            ; Infinite loop

;-------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: DELAY_50MS using Timer 0, Mode 1
;-------------------------------------------------
DELAY_50MS:
        MOV TMOD, #01H       ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV TH0, #0x4B       ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #0xFD       ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0             ; Start Timer 0
WAIT:   JNB TF0, WAIT        ; Loop until TF0 = 1 (overflow)
        CLR TR0              ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR TF0              ; Clear overflow flag
        RET                  ; Return from subroutine

        END
```

### Line-by-Line Explanation

| Line | Code | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ORG 0000H` | Assembler directive: place next instruction at reset vector 0x0000. |
| 2 | `LJMP MAIN` | Long jump to skip the ISR vector area (0x0003–0x002A). |
| 3 | `ORG 0030H` | Place main program at 0x0030 to avoid interrupt vector clashes. |
| 4 | `ACALL DELAY_50MS` | Call 50 ms delay; ACALL is 2-byte call (range 2 KB). |
| 5 | `CPL P1.0` | **Complement** P1.0 — toggles between 0 and 1, blinking the LED. |
| 6 | `SJMP MAIN` | **Short Jump** — infinite loop (2-byte instruction). |
| 7–14 | Delay Block | Standard 8051 timer delay using polled TF0 flag. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite**: This exact 50 ms delay program appears in almost every university examination. **Memorize TH0/TL0 values**: For 50 ms at 11.0592 MHz, use **0x4BFD**.

---

## 3.3 Program 2 — LED Blinking in Embedded C (Keil C51 / SDCC)

> **Objective**: Same as Program 1, but written in **Embedded C** using **Keil µVision** or **SDCC** compiler.

```c
#include <reg51.h>   // 8051 SFR definitions

sbit LED = P1^0;     // Define P1.0 as 'LED'

void delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4B;     // High byte for 50 ms
    TL0  = 0xFD;     // Low byte for 50 ms
    TR0  = 1;        // Start Timer 0
    while(TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow flag
    TR0  = 0;        // Stop Timer 0
    TF0  = 0;        // Clear overflow flag
}

void main(void) {
    LED = 0;         // Initialize LED OFF (active-high assumed)
    while(1) {
        LED = ~LED;  // Toggle LED
        delay_50ms();// 50 ms delay
    }
}
```

### Explanation Matrix

| Construct | Meaning | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| `#include <reg51.h>` | Header with SFR/PORT definitions | Compiler-specific for 8051 |
| `sbit LED = P1^0;` | Defines a single bit-addressable variable | Cleaner, readable code |
| `TMOD = 0x01;` | Sets Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 1 is canonical for delays |
| `while(TF0 == 0);` | Polled wait loop | Equivalent to `JNB TF0, $` in ALP |
| `LED = ~LED;` | Bitwise NOT — toggles the pin | Cleaner than `LED = !LED;` in some cases |
| `while(1)` | Infinite loop | Mandatory in main() for embedded systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compiler-Specific Note**: For **SDCC** (free compiler), use `#include <8051.h>` instead of `<reg51.h>`. For **Keil**, the conventional header is `<reg51.h>`.

---

## 3.4 Program 3 — LED Pattern Generation (Chaser Light)

> **Objective**: Generate a running-light (chaser) pattern on Port P1: 8 LEDs blink one-by-one, then restart.

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV A, #01H     ; Initial pattern: 00000001
        MOV P1, A       ; Send to Port 1
        ACALL DELAY
        RL A            ; Rotate left through carry
        SJMP MAIN

DELAY:  MOV R0, #0FFH
        MOV R1, #0FFH
LOOP1:  DJNZ R0, LOOP1
        DJNZ R1, LOOP1
        RET
        END
```

> **Embedded C equivalent**:

```c
#include <reg51.h>
void delay(void) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i=0; i<500; i++)
        for(j=0; j<500; j++);
}
void main(void) {
    unsigned char pattern = 0x01;
    while(1) {
        P1 = pattern;    // Output pattern
        delay();
        pattern <<= 1;  // Shift left
        if(pattern == 0)
            pattern = 0x01;  // Restart from LSB
    }
}
```

---

## 3.5 Program 4 — Switch-Controlled LED (Polled Input)

> **Objective**: When a switch on P3.2 is pressed, the LED on P1.0 toggles. Demonstrates **polled I/O**.

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   SETB P1.0       ; LED initially ON (active-low optional)
LOOP:   JB P3.2, LOOP   ; If switch NOT pressed, wait
        ACALL DELAY     ; Debounce delay
        JB P3.2, LOOP   ; Check again (debounce)
        CPL P1.0        ; Toggle LED
WAIT_RELEASE:
        JNB P3.2, WAIT_RELEASE  ; Wait until switch is released
        SJMP LOOP

DELAY:  MOV R0, #0FFH
        MOV R1, #0FFH
LOOP1:  DJNZ R0, LOOP1
        DJNZ R1, LOOP1
        RET
        END
```

> **Embedded C equivalent**:

```c
#include <reg51.h>
sbit LED = P1^0;
sbit SW  = P3^2;
void delay(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    for(i=0; i<30000; i++);
}
void main(void) {
    LED = 0;
    while(1) {
        if(SW == 0) {        // Active-low switch
            delay();          // Debounce
            if(SW == 0) {
                LED = ~LED;   // Toggle
                while(SW == 0); // Wait for release
            }
        }
    }
}
```

---

## 3.6 Program 5 — Interrupt-Driven LED (External Interrupt 0)

> **Objective**: Use **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** on P3.2 to toggle an LED on P1.7. Demonstrates **event-driven** architecture.

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN

        ORG 0003H              ; External 0 ISR Vector
        LJMP EXT0_ISR

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV IE, #81H          ; EA=1, EX0=1
        SETB IT0              ; Edge-triggered (falling edge)
        SETB PX0              ; High priority (optional)
        HERE: SJMP HERE        ; Idle loop — CPU does nothing

EXT0_ISR:
        CPL P1.7              ; Toggle LED
        RETI                  ; Return from interrupt

        END
```

> **Embedded C equivalent**:

```c
#include <reg51.h>
sbit LED = P1^7;

void ext0_isr(void) interrupt 0 {
    LED = ~LED;   // Toggle LED
}

void main(void) {
    IT0 = 1;      // Edge-triggered
    EX0 = 1;      // Enable External 0
    EA  = 1;      // Global enable
    while(1);     // Idle — wait for interrupt
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keil C51 Interrupt Syntax**: The keyword `interrupt N` designates the ISR, where `N` is the **interrupt number** (0 = INT0, 1 = T0, 2 = INT1, 3 = T1, 4 = Serial). The compiler auto-generates the vector `LJMP` and the `RETI` instruction.

---

## 3.7 Program 6 — OLED Display Initialization (I²C Bit-Banged)

> **Objective**: Initialize an **SSD1306-based 0.96" OLED** and display a static character "K" (representing KTU).

```c
#include <reg51.h>
#define SDA P1_0    // Software I2C SDA
#define SCL P1_1    // Software I2C SCL

void i2c_start(void);
void i2c_stop(void);
void i2c_write(unsigned char dat);
void oled_cmd(unsigned char cmd);
void oled_init(void);
void oled_data(unsigned char dat);

void delay_us(unsigned int us) {
    unsigned int i;
    for(i=0; i<us; i++);
}

void i2c_start(void) {
    SDA = 1; SCL = 1; delay_us(5);
    SDA = 0; delay_us(5);
    SCL = 0;
}

void i2c_stop(void) {
    SDA = 0; SCL = 0; delay_us(5);
    SCL = 1; delay_us(5);
    SDA = 1;
}

void i2c_write(unsigned char dat) {
    unsigned char i;
    for(i=0; i<8; i++) {
        SDA = (dat & 0x80) ? 1 : 0;
        SCL = 1; delay_us(2);
        SCL = 0; delay_us(2);
        dat <<= 1;
    }
    SDA = 1;          // ACK bit release
    SCL = 1; delay_us(2);
    SCL = 0;
}

void oled_cmd(unsigned char cmd) {
    i2c_start();
    i2c_write(0x3D << 1);   // Slave address + Write (0)
    i2c_write(0x00);         // Control byte: Co=0, D/C#=0 (Command)
    i2c_write(cmd);
    i2c_stop();
}

void oled_data(unsigned char dat) {
    i2c_start();
    i2c_write(0x3D << 1);
    i2c_write(0x40);         // Control byte: D/C#=1 (Data)
    i2c_write(dat);
    i2c_stop();
}

void oled_init(void) {
    oled_cmd(0xAE);    // Display OFF
    oled_cmd(0xA8);    // Set multiplex ratio
    oled_cmd(0x3F);    // 64 MUX
    oled_cmd(0xD3);    // Set display offset
    oled_cmd(0x00);
    oled_cmd(0x40);    // Set start line = 0
    oled_cmd(0xA1);    // Segment remap
    oled_cmd(0xC8);    // COM output scan direction
    oled_cmd(0xDA);    // Set COM pins
    oled_cmd(0x12);
    oled_cmd(0x81);    // Set contrast
    oled_cmd(0x7F);
    oled_cmd(0xA4);    // Display from RAM
    oled_cmd(0xA6);    // Normal display
    oled_cmd(0xD5);    // Set oscillator
    oled_cmd(0x80);
    oled_cmd(0x8D);    // Enable charge pump
    oled_cmd(0x14);
    oled_cmd(0xAF);    // Display ON
}

void main(void) {
    oled_init();
    // Display letter "K" (5x7 font, simplified)
    oled_data(0x7C); oled_data(0x08); oled_data(0x08);
    oled_data(0x14); oled_data(0x22);
    while(1);
}
```

### OLED Command Analysis Matrix

| Command Byte | Function | Why Important |
|---|---|---|
| `0xAE` / `0xAF` | Display OFF / ON | Must turn ON after init |
| `0xA8 0x3F` | Set MUX ratio to 64 | Required for 128×64 display |
| `0xA1` | Segment remap (column 127 → SEG0) | Mirror horizontally |
| `0xC8` | COM scan direction reverse | Mirror vertically |
| `0x8D 0x14` | Enable internal charge pump | Powers OLED without external Vcc |
| `0x81 0x7F` | Contrast = 127/255 | Adjust brightness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Interrupt Handling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Execution] --> B{Interrupt Request}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{Global EA = 1?}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D{Source Enable EX/ET/ES = 1?}
    D -- No --> A
    D -- Yes --> E{Priority Check}
    E --> F{Equal Priority?}
    F -- No --> G[Service Higher Priority]
    F -- Yes --> H[Service by Vector Order]
    G --> I[Complete Current Instruction]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Push PC on Stack]
    J --> K[Load Vector Address]
    K --> L[Execute ISR]
    L --> M[RETI Instruction]
    M --> N[Pop PC from Stack]
    N --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The flow demonstrates that an interrupt request is only serviced if (1) the global EA bit is set, (2) the specific source is enabled, and (3) the priority is resolved. The RETI instruction is essential — it signals to the hardware that the ISR is complete and that interrupts may again be accepted.

## 4.2 LED Interfacing Circuit (8051 Port 1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[8051 P1.0] --> R[330 Ohm Resistor]
    R --> L[LED Anode A]
    L --> K[LED Cathode K]
    K --> G((GND))
    
    VCC[Vcc 5V] -. Pull-up .- P1_1[8051 P1.1 Switch]
    P1_1 --> SW[Push-to-Ground Switch]
    SW --> G
```

> **Reading the diagram**: A 330 Ω current-limiting resistor sits between the 8051 pin and the LED anode. The cathode is grounded. When P1.0 outputs HIGH, current flows and the LED glows. A switch on P1.1 (with pull-up) demonstrates polled input.

## 4.3 OLED Module (SSD1306) I²C Connection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[8051] -- SDA P1.0 --> OLED[SSD1306 OLED]
    M -- SCL P1.1 --> OLED
    VCC[+5V] --> OLED
    GND[0V] --> OLED
    
    R1[Pull-up 4.7k] --> SDA
    R2[Pull-up 4.7k] --> SCL
    VCC -. 5V .-> R1
    VCC -. 5V .-> R2
```

> **Reading the diagram**: I²C requires **pull-up resistors** on both SDA and SCL lines (typically **4.7 kΩ** to Vcc). Without them, the bus will not function. The 8051 acts as the **master** and drives both lines.

## 4.4 Interrupt Vector Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[0x0000 Reset Vector] --> E0[0x0003 External 0 ISR]
    E0 --> T0[0x000B Timer 0 ISR]
    T0 --> E1[0x0013 External 1 ISR]
    E1 --> T1[0x001B Timer 1 ISR]
    T1 --> S[0x0023 Serial Port ISR]
    S --> User[0x002A User Program Space]
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Each interrupt vector occupies **8 bytes** of code memory. In practice, this is **insufficient** for most ISRs, so the canonical technique is to place an `LJMP` instruction at the vector, redirecting to a full ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

## 4.5 Functional Block Diagram — Embedded LED Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Input Peripherals] --> MCU
    subgraph MCU[8051 Microcontroller]
        CPU[CPU ALU Registers] --> MEM[Code Data SFR]
        MEM --> TMR[Timers Counters]
        MEM --> INT[Interrupt Controller]
    end
    Output[LED Array + OLED Display] --> MCU
    Trigger[Switch or Sensor] --> MCU
    Trigger -- Hardware Signal --> INT
    INT -- Event Signal --> CPU
    CPU -- Control Logic --> Output
```

> **Reading the diagram**: This high-level block diagram illustrates the integration of all components covered in Module 2. A switch or sensor triggers an interrupt; the interrupt controller notifies the CPU; the CPU executes the ISR; the ISR updates the LED or OLED output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Explain the difference between level-triggered and edge-triggered interrupts in 8051. How is it configured?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
>
> In 8051, the **TCON** register bits **IT0 (bit 0)** and **IT1 (bit 2)** control the trigger mode for External Interrupts 0 and 1.
>
> | Mode | Configuration | Behavior |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Level-Triggered** | `ITx = 0` | Interrupt is requested as long as the pin is held LOW. CPU must service it before the pin returns HIGH, else the request is lost. |
> | **Edge-Triggered** | `ITx = 1` | Interrupt is requested on the **falling edge** (HIGH-to-LOW transition). The hardware latches the event in the **IEx** flag, which must be cleared by software in the ISR. |
>
> **Configuration Example** (Edge-triggered External 0): `SETB IT0;` followed by `SETB EX0; SETB EA;` (3 distinct lines for full marks).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: An LED with $V_F = 2V$ and $I_F = 15 mA$ is to be connected to a 5V 8051 pin. Calculate the series resistor value.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks)**:
>
> Using the current-limiting formula:
>
> $$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{5 - 2}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200 \Omega$$
>
> The nearest standard E12 resistor is **220 Ω**, universally available.
>
> **Power dissipation across R**: $P_R = I_F^2 \times R = (0.015)^2 \times 220 = 0.0495 W \approx 50 mW$, so a **¼ W (250 mW) resistor** is sufficient.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme)**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice**. The structure is two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks.

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Adapted]**

**Q: (a)** Draw and explain the **Interrupt Structure of 8051** including the **IE** and **IP** registers. List the vector addresses of all interrupts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an **8051 ALP** to toggle an LED connected to **P1.0** continuously with a 50 ms delay, assuming a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. Show the calculation of the timer reload value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The 8051 has **5 interrupt sources** and a **2-level priority** structure managed by three SFRs:

**1. IE Register (0xA8)** — Master + Individual Enable:

| EA | – | – | ES | ET1 | EX1 | ET0 | EX0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

(Example: enables only External 0 with global enable = 0x81)

**2. IP Register (0xB8)** — Priority:

| – | – | – | PS | PT1 | PX1 | PT0 | PX0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

(Sets External 0 to high priority)

**3. Vector Addresses**:

| Interrupt | Vector |
|---|---|
| Reset | 0x0000 |
| External 0 | 0x0003 |
| Timer 0 | 0x000B |
| External 1 | 0x0013 |
| Timer 1 | 0x001B |
| Serial | 0x0023 |

> **7-mark allocation**: 2 marks IE, 2 marks IP, 2 marks vector table, 1 mark default priority note.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Timer Calculation**:

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \mu s$$

$$N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46083 \text{ counts}$$

$$X = 65536 - 46083 = 19453 = 0x4BFD$$

$$TH0 = 0x4B, \quad TL0 = 0xFD$$

**Step 2 — Assembly Program**:

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   CLR P1.0          ; LED OFF initially
HERE:   SETB P1.0         ; LED ON
        ACALL DELAY
        CLR P1.0          ; LED OFF
        ACALL DELAY
        SJMP HERE

DELAY:  MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV TH0, #0x4B
        MOV TL0, #0xFD
        SETB TR0          ; Start timer
WAIT:   JNB TF0, WAIT
        CLR TR0
        CLR TF0
        RET
        END
```

> **7-mark allocation**: 2 marks timer math, 3 marks program, 1 mark comment, 1 mark END directive.

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **OLED display interfacing with 8051** using I²C protocol. Discuss the **SSD1306 controller**, its commands, and the **GDDRAM** structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an **Embedded C** program for 8051 (Keil) to initialize an **SSD1306 OLED** and display the character **"K"**. Use software I²C on P1.0 (SDA) and P1.1 (SCL). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**OLED Module Overview**:
The 0.96" OLED uses the **SSD1306 controller** with a **128×64 pixel** resolution. It communicates via **I²C** (or SPI) with default address **0x3D** (7-bit).

**I²C Bus**:
- **SDA**: Bidirectional data on P1.0
- **SCL**: Master-generated clock on P1.1
- Both lines require **4.7 kΩ pull-up** resistors to Vcc.

**GDDRAM Organization**:
- 128 columns × 64 rows = 8192 pixels
- Divided into **8 pages** (Page 0 to Page 7)
- Each page = 8 rows × 128 columns = 128 bytes
- Total: **1024 bytes** of display RAM

**Key SSD1306 Commands**:
- `0xAE` / `0xAF`: Display OFF / ON
- `0xA8`: Set multiplex ratio (0x3F for 64 rows)
- `0xA1`: Segment remap
- `0xC8`: COM scan direction reverse
- `0x8D 0x14`: Enable charge pump regulator
- `0x81`: Set contrast

**Control Bytes**:
- `0x00`: Following bytes are commands
- `0x40`: Following bytes are display data

#### Model Solution for (b)

```c
#include <reg51.h>
sbit SDA = P1^0;
sbit SCL = P1^1;

void delay() { unsigned int i; for(i=0; i<1000; i++); }

void i2c_start(void) {
    SDA = 1; SCL = 1; delay();
    SDA = 0; delay(); SCL = 0;
}
void i2c_stop(void) {
    SDA = 0; SCL = 0; delay();
    SCL = 1; delay(); SDA = 1;
}
void i2c_write(unsigned char d) {
    unsigned char i;
    for(i=0; i<8; i++) {
        SDA = (d & 0x80) ? 1 : 0;
        SCL = 1; delay(); SCL = 0; delay();
        d <<= 1;
    }
    SDA = 1; SCL = 1; delay(); SCL = 0;
}
void oled_cmd(unsigned char c) {
    i2c_start();
    i2c_write(0x3D << 1);
    i2c_write(0x00);
    i2c_write(c);
    i2c_stop();
}
void oled_data(unsigned char d) {
    i2c_start();
    i2c_write(0x3D << 1);
    i2c_write(0x40);
    i2c_write(d);
    i2c_stop();
}
void oled_init(void) {
    oled_cmd(0xAE);
    oled_cmd(0xA8); oled_cmd(0x3F);
    oled_cmd(0xD3); oled_cmd(0x00);
    oled_cmd(0x40);
    oled_cmd(0xA1); oled_cmd(0xC8);
    oled_cmd(0xDA); oled_cmd(0x12);
    oled_cmd(0x81); oled_cmd(0x7F);
    oled_cmd(0xA4); oled_cmd(0xA6);
    oled_cmd(0x8D); oled_cmd(0x14);
    oled_cmd(0xAF);
}
void main(void) {
    oled_init();
    // 'K' in 5x7 font approximation
    oled_data(0x7F); oled_data(0x08);
    oled_data(0x14); oled_data(0x22);
    oled_data(0x41);
    while(1);
}
```

> **7-mark allocation**: 2 marks I²C functions, 2 marks OLED cmd/data, 2 marks init sequence, 1 mark main display.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**:
> 1. **Forgetting `EA = 1`** — No interrupt will fire without the global enable. Most common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Missing `CLR TF0`** — Timer interrupt will be triggered continuously, causing an infinite interrupt storm.
> 3. **Wrong I²C address shift** — Students write `0x3D` directly without `<< 1`, causing the device to never acknowledge.
> 4. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** — This is a fatal error. `RETI` tells the interrupt hardware that the ISR has completed; `RET` does not.
> 5. **Forgetting pull-up resistors** — In OLED interfacing, no pull-ups means no I²C communication. Examiners deduct 2 marks if schematic omits them.
> 6. **Incorrect timer reload order** — Always load **TH0 first, then TL0**, then **SETB TR0**. The opposite order can cause a one-cycle error.
> 7. **Vector overflow** — Placing ISR code directly at vector 0x0003 may overwrite code at 0x000B. Always `LJMP` to a safe location.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Interrupts, LED, OLED**

- ✅ 8051 has **5 interrupts** with **fixed vector addresses** 0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023.
- ✅ **IE register** at 0xA8 controls enables; **EA = 1** is mandatory for any interrupt.
- ✅ **IP register** at 0xB8 controls 2-level priority; default is natural polling order.
- ✅ **TCON bits IT0/IT1** = 1 selects edge-triggered; 0 selects level-triggered.
- ✅ LED resistor: $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$ → Standard value is **330 Ω** for typical 5V/10 mA.
- ✅ LED can be **active-high** (anode to pin) or **active-low** (cathode to pin) — KTU prefers active-high.
- ✅ **Timer 0 Mode 1 (16-bit)** reload for 50 ms at 11.0592 MHz: **TH0 = 0x4B, TL0 = 0xFD**.
- ✅ Embedded C ISR syntax: `void name(void) interrupt N { ... }` (Keil-specific).
- ✅ **RETI** is mandatory; never use **RET** in ISRs.
- ✅ OLED SSD1306 uses **I²C (2-wire)** — SDA + SCL — with address **0x3D**.
- ✅ Pull-up resistors (**4.7 kΩ**) are **mandatory** on both SDA and SCL lines.
- ✅ GDDRAM: 1024 bytes (128×64 ÷ 8), organized in **8 pages**.
- ✅ OLED init must end with `0xAF` (Display ON) and include charge-pump enable `0x8D 0x14`.
- ✅ **ALP vs Embedded C trade-off**: ALP gives finer hardware control; C gives portability and readability.
- ✅ Debounce delay (~20–50 ms) is essential for switch-based polled inputs to avoid false triggering.

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra**: *"Know the three SFRs (TCON, IE, IP), the five vectors, the 50 ms timer values (0x4BFD), the 330 Ω resistor rule, and the I²C address 0x3D with pull-ups. You are ready for Module 2!"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SENSOR (KTU Definition):** A sensor is a physical device, module, or subsystem that detects physical, chemical, or biological quantities (such as temperature, pressure, light, motion, humidity, or chemical composition) and converts them into an electrical signal (analog or digital) that can be read, processed, stored, or displayed by an embedded system or microcontroller.

In the context of **Embedded Systems**, a sensor is the *input transducer* that bridges the gap between the **physical world** (analog, continuous-time domain) and the **digital world** of the microcontroller (discrete, binary domain). It forms the first block in any data acquisition chain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an Embedded System as a tiny artificial "robot body."**

| Human Body | Embedded System Equivalent |
|------------|---------------------------|
| Eyes (detect light) | LDR / Photodiode / Camera Sensor |
| Skin (detect heat/pressure) | LM35 (temperature) / FSR (force) |
| Ears (detect sound) | Microphone / Ultrasonic Sensor |
| Nerves (carry signal) | Signal conditioning + ADC |
| Brain (process) | Microcontroller (ARM/AVR/PIC) |
| Muscles (act) | Actuators / Motors |

**Just like a human cannot "feel" the world without nerve endings, a microcontroller cannot "sense" the world without a sensor.** The sensor is the "nerve ending" of the embedded system.

## 1.3 Role of Sensors in the Embedded Data Acquisition Chain

The complete signal path in any modern IoT or smart embedded device follows the same generic pipeline:

```
Physical Quantity  →  Sensor  →  Signal Conditioning  →  ADC  →  Microcontroller  →  Decision
   (e.g., 45°C)        (LM35)      (Amplify/Filter)        (12-bit)    (ARM Cortex-M)        (Fan ON)
```

## 1.4 Standard Sensor Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measurement to the true value (typically expressed as ± % of Full-Scale).
- **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement under identical conditions.
- **Sensitivity:** Ratio of output change (ΔY) to input change (ΔX). Unit: V/unit of measurand.
- **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change in input. For digital sensors, limited by ADC bits.
- **Range (Span):** Minimum to maximum input the sensor can measure.
- **Response Time:** Time taken to reach 90% (or 63.2% — time constant τ) of final output.
- **Linearity:** Maximum deviation of the actual transfer curve from an ideal straight line.
- **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input during increasing vs. decreasing sweeps.
- **Drift:** Slow change of output over time, even when input is constant.

> [!NOTE]
> The **2024 KTU syllabus** specifically emphasizes **sensors, transducers, signal conditioning, and ADC interfacing** for microcontrollers. These metrics are repeatedly tested in Part A (3-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensors (Hierarchical Breakdown)

### A. Based on Power Requirement
- **Active Sensors:** Generate their own electrical output (no external excitation needed).
  - Examples: Piezoelectric accelerometer, thermocouple, photovoltaic (solar) cell.
- **Passive Sensors:** Require external excitation (voltage/current source) to operate.
  - Examples: Thermistor, RTD, LDR, strain gauge.

### B. Based on Output Signal Type
- **Analog Sensors:** Continuous output voltage/current proportional to measurand.
  - Examples: LM35 (10 mV/°C), thermistor, potentiometric angle sensor.
- **Digital Sensors:** Output is discrete pulses, frequency, or digital protocol (I²C/SPI).
  - Examples: DHT11, DS18B20 (1-Wire), MPU6050 (I²C).

### C. Based on Measurand (Most Common KTU Classification)
| Measurand | Common Sensor | Output Type |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Temperature | LM35, DS18B20, Thermistor, RTD | Analog / Digital |
| Light | LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor | Analog (resistance/voltage) |
| Pressure | MPX series, BMP180 | Analog / I²C |
| Proximity | IR sensor, Ultrasonic (HC-SR04) | Digital / Time |
| Position/Angle | Rotary encoder, Potentiometer | Digital / Analog |
| Acceleration | ADXL345, MPU6050 | I²C/SPI Digital |
| Gas | MQ-2, MQ-135 | Analog (resistance change) |
| Humidity | DHT22, SHT31 | Digital |
| Magnetic | Hall Effect (A3144), Reed switch | Digital |

## 2.2 Operating Principle of Representative Sensors

### 2.2.1 LM35 Precision Temperature Sensor
- **Principle:** Band-gap voltage reference IC. Output voltage is linearly proportional to Celsius temperature.
- **Sensitivity:** **10 mV/°C** (linear, zero offset at 0°C).
- **Supply:** 4 V to 30 V; Output range: 0 V to 1.5 V for 0°C to 150°C.
- **Output equation:**
  
  $$V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T_{Celsius}$$

### 2.2.2 Thermistor (NTC type)
- **Principle:** Negative Temperature Coefficient — resistance decreases as temperature rises.
- **Steinhart-Hart equation (precise model):**
  
  $$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C \left[\ln(R)\right]^3$$

  Where T is in Kelvin, R in ohms, and A, B, C are calibration constants.

- **Simplified β-equation (most common):**
  
  $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

  Where R₀ is resistance at reference temperature T₀ (usually 25°C = 298.15 K), and β is the material constant (typically 3000–5000 K).

### 2.2.3 LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)
- **Principle:** Photoconductivity — resistance drops as light intensity (lux) increases.
- **Typical values:** ~1 MΩ in dark, ~few hundred Ω in bright light.

### 2.2.4 HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Sensor
- **Principle:** Time-of-Flight of ultrasonic burst (40 kHz).
- **Distance equation:**
  
  $$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

  Where v_sound ≈ 343 m/s (at 20°C), t_echo is the measured echo pulse width, and division by 2 accounts for the round trip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **v_sound is temperature-dependent:** v = 331 + 0.6 × T(°C) m/s. KTU problems often test this.

### 2.2.5 Strain Gauge
- **Principle:** Resistance changes when the gauge is stretched/compressed.
  
  $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \epsilon$$

  Where **G** is the Gauge Factor (typically ~2.0 for metallic gauges), and ε is the strain (ΔL/L).

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning — The "Nervous System" Between Sensor and MCU

Raw sensor output is rarely directly acceptable by a microcontroller. It must be conditioned:

1. **Amplification:** Small signals (mV range from thermocouple) need Op-Amp amplification (e.g., INA128, LM358).
2. **Filtering:** Remove 50/60 Hz mains noise using low-pass filters (RC, active).
3. **Linearization:** Convert non-linear sensor response (thermistor) to linear (using lookup table or Steinhart-Hart).
4. **Level Shifting:** Match voltage levels (e.g., 0–3.3 V for ARM Cortex-M, 0–5 V for Arduino).
5. **Isolation:** Optocoupler or transformer isolation in noisy industrial environments.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | V per unit of measurand |
| LM35 Output | $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$ | T in °C |
| Thermistor (β-model) | $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | T in Kelvin |
| Thermocouple Seebeck | $V = S_{TC} \cdot \Delta T$ | S_TC in µV/°C |
| Ultrasonic Distance | $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Round-trip compensation |
| Speed of Sound (air) | $v = 331 + 0.6 T$ | T in °C, v in m/s |
| Strain Gauge | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \epsilon$ | G ≈ 2 for metal |
| ADC Resolution | $V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | n = ADC bits |
| ADC Conversion | $D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$ | Decimal output code |
| % Full Scale Error | $\%FS = \frac{V_{meas} - V_{true}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | — |
| Time Constant (1st order) | $V(t) = V_{final}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | τ = RC |
| Wheatstone Bridge Balance | $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$ | Balanced → V_out = 0 |
| Bridge Output (strain) | $V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \epsilon}{4}$ | Quarter-bridge config |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Crash detection (accelerometer), tire pressure (TPMS), O2 (lambda) sensor.
- **Healthcare:** Pulse oximeter, ECG electrodes, blood-glucose biosensors.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance (vibration + temperature on motors).
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture, NPK sensors, weather stations.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones pack 10+ sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer, proximity, ambient light, barometer, fingerprint).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 examiner focus:** "Application-based" questions. Always link the sensor to a real embedded application. Memorize at least **2 application examples** for each of the 4–5 sensors discussed in class.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: LM35 Output to Temperature Conversion (with ADC)

**Problem:** An LM35 is connected to a 10-bit ADC of an ARM Cortex-M0+ microcontroller with V_ref = 3.3 V. The ADC gives a digital output of **512**. Find the temperature.

### Step 1: Recall ADC resolution equation
The voltage corresponding to one LSB (Least Significant Bit) is:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} = 3.226 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

### Step 3: Convert digital code to voltage

$$V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB}$$

$$V_{in} = 512 \times 3.226 \text{ mV} = 1651.71 \text{ mV} = 1.652 \text{ V}$$

### Step 4: Apply LM35 transfer equation (10 mV/°C)

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$T = \frac{1651.71 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = 165.17 \text{°C}$$

### Step 5: Check feasibility
LM35 standard range is 0°C to 150°C (basic version). 165°C exceeds the basic range — would need the **LM35DZ (0–100°C)** or **LM35CZ (0–110°C)** version. For a valid answer, this is a useful **exam pitfall** to point out.

> [!NOTE]
> Always verify whether the computed temperature lies within the sensor's valid operating range. KTU examiners often include this as a sub-part.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ultrasonic Distance Computation with Temperature Compensation

**Problem:** HC-SR04 reports an echo pulse width of **t = 5.85 ms**. Ambient temperature is **T = 35°C**. Compute the distance to the object.

### Step 1: Temperature-compensated speed of sound

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times T(°C)$$

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 35 = 331 + 21 = 352 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2: Apply time-of-flight distance formula

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute values (unit conversion: t in seconds)

$$d = \frac{352 \text{ m/s} \times 5.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{2.0592}{2} = 1.0296 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Express in cm for practical use

$$d \approx 102.96 \text{ cm} \approx 1.03 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **½** in the denominator is critical: the ultrasonic wave travels *to* the object and *back*, so total path = 2 × d.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermistor Resistance at Unknown Temperature (β-Model)

**Problem:** An NTC thermistor has R₀ = 10 kΩ at T₀ = 25°C (298.15 K) and β = 3950 K. Find its resistance at T = 80°C (353.15 K).

### Step 1: State the β-equation

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

### Step 2: Compute the reciprocal temperatures

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{353.15} = 2.832 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Compute the difference

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = (2.832 - 3.354) \times 10^{-3} = -0.522 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4: Multiply by β

$$\beta \cdot \Delta\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = 3950 \times (-0.522 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.062$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate

$$e^{-2.062} = 0.1271$$

### Step 6: Compute final resistance

$$R_T = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times 0.1271 = 1.271 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Sanity check:** As temperature increases (80°C > 25°C), NTC resistance should *decrease*. ✓ (1.27 kΩ < 10 kΩ). Correct.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Common Applications

| Application | Recommended Sensor | Justification (KTU-grade reasoning) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial furnace (>500°C) | K-type Thermocouple | Wide range, rugged, high T |
| Medical digital thermometer | DS18B20 | ±0.5°C accuracy, 1-Wire digital |
| Room thermostat | LM35 + comparator | Linear, cheap, easy MCU interface |
| Robot obstacle avoidance | HC-SR04 (ultrasonic) | Works in dark, range 2–400 cm |
| Line-follower robot | IR Reflective (TCRT5000) | Fast response, low cost, digital output |
| Smartphone screen rotation | ADXL345 (3-axis) | I²C digital, low power, embedded in IMUs |
| Air quality monitor | MQ-135 | Detects NH₃, NOx, CO₂, alcohol |
| Smart irrigation | Soil moisture capacitive sensor | Corrosion-resistant vs. resistive type |
| Wearable heart-rate | MAX30102 (PPG) | Optical, integrated red/IR LEDs |
| BLDC motor commutation | Hall-effect sensor (A3144) | Digital pulses for rotor position |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Sensors] --> B0[Active]
    A0 --> C0[Passive]
    A0 --> D0[Analog Output]
    A0 --> E0[Digital Output]
    B0 --> B1[Thermocouple]
    B0 --> B2[Piezoelectric]
    B0 --> B3[Photovoltaic]
    C0 --> C1[Thermistor]
    C0 --> C2[LDR]
    C0 --> C3[Strain Gauge]
    D0 --> D1[LM35]
    D0 --> D2[Potentiometer]
    D0 --> D3[Photodiode]
    E0 --> E1[DS18B20]
    E0 --> E2[MPU6050]
    E0 --> E3[HC SR04]
```

## 4.2 Complete Sensor-to-Microcontroller Signal Conditioning Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    PQ[Physical Quantity] --> SEN[Sensor]
    SEN --> SC1[Amplification Op Amp]
    SC1 --> SC2[Low Pass Filter]
    SC2 --> SC3[Level Shifter]
    SC3 --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller ARM]
    MCU --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing]
    DSP --> ACT[Actuator or Display]
    MCU -. Feedback .-> SC1
```

## 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge for Strain Gauge Interfacing

```mermaid
graph TD
    VEX[V excitation] --> N1[Node A]
    VEX --> N2[Node C]
    N1 --> R1[R1 Fixed]
    N1 --> RG[Rg Strain Gauge]
    N2 --> R2[R2 Fixed]
    N2 --> R3[R3 Fixed]
    R1 --> NB[Node B]
    RG --> NB
    R2 --> ND[Node D]
    R3 --> ND
    NB --> OA1[Op Amp INA128]
    ND --> OA1
    OA1 --> OUT[Vout to ADC]
```

## 4.4 Interfacing LM35 to a Microcontroller (Voltage Divider-Free)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC[+5V] --> LM35[LM35 TO Package]
    LM35 --> VOUT[Vout Pin]
    VOUT --> ADCIN[ADC Channel 0 Pin]
    GND[GND] --> LM35
    ADCIN --> SAMC[SAM Cortex M0 ADC]
    SAMC --> LCD[16x2 LCD Display]
    SAMC --> UART[UART to PC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always mention the **ADC reference voltage (V_ref)** when drawing any sensor-to-MCU diagram. The reference is what makes the digital conversion meaningful. This single point differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** Define a sensor. Differentiate between **active** and **passive** sensors with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
A sensor is a device that converts a physical quantity into a measurable electrical signal. An **active sensor** generates its own electrical output from the energy it measures (e.g., a **thermocouple** generates a voltage from a temperature gradient — Seebeck effect), and hence requires **no external excitation**. A **passive sensor** requires external power to operate and modulates this excitation based on the measurand (e.g., an **LDR** whose resistance changes with light, used in a voltage-divider circuit with V_cc). The key distinction is the **source of output energy**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is the significance of **signal conditioning** in an embedded sensor system? List any two conditioning techniques.

**Model Answer:**
Raw sensor output is usually too weak, noisy, or non-linear to be directly fed to a microcontroller ADC. **Signal conditioning** processes the raw signal into a clean, scaled, linear, MCU-compatible form. It improves **accuracy**, **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)**, and **resolution**.

Two key techniques:
1. **Amplification** — using instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., INA128) to boost mV-level signals (e.g., thermocouple) to the 0–3.3 V range of an ADC.
2. **Filtering** — using a low-pass RC filter to remove high-frequency noise and 50 Hz mains interference.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of the **LM35** temperature sensor. With a neat block diagram, describe how it is interfaced to an ARM Cortex microcontroller, including the role of the **ADC** and the **reference voltage**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **LM35** is a precision centigrade temperature sensor IC whose output voltage is **linearly proportional to Celsius temperature**, with a scale factor of **10 mV/°C**. It uses the **band-gap reference** principle of a silicon transistor (the base-emitter voltage V_BE has a known temperature coefficient of –2 mV/°C, which is cancelled by an on-chip delta-V_BE amplifier producing +10 mV/°C). It does not require any external calibration or trimming.

**Block diagram of interfacing:**

```
   +5V ──► LM35 (V+ pin)       LM35 (V_out) ──► PA0 (ADC Channel)
   GND ──► LM35 (GND pin)
                               
   ARM Cortex-M0 MCU
   ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  ADC0 ──► Calibration ──► Display  │
   │  V_ref = 3.3V, Resolution = 12-bit │
   └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Role of ADC:** The LM35 produces an analog voltage (e.g., 250 mV at 25°C). The **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** inside the ARM Cortex-M0 samples this voltage and converts it into a **12-bit digital code** (D), where:

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{12} - 1)$$

The MCU then computes temperature as:

$$T = \frac{D \times V_{ref}}{(2^{12} - 1) \times 10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

**Role of V_ref:** The reference voltage sets the **maximum input range** of the ADC. For V_ref = 3.3 V, the ADC can digitize 0–3.3 V, corresponding to 0–330°C for LM35. Choosing V_ref = 1.0 V (internal band-gap) gives 0–100°C with 4× better resolution (1.0 V / 4095 ≈ 244 µV/LSB ≈ 0.024°C).

**(b)** A thermistor has R₀ = 10 kΩ at 25°C and β = 3950 K. Calculate its resistance at 60°C. Discuss why a **voltage divider** is commonly used to interface an NTC thermistor with a microcontroller. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Using the β-equation:

$$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15 \text{ K}} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{(60 + 273.15)} = \frac{1}{333.15} = 3.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = (3.002 - 3.354) \times 10^{-3} = -0.352 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-0.352 \times 10^{-3}) = -1.39$$

$$R_{60} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times e^{-1.39} = 10 \times 0.249 = 2.49 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Why a voltage divider?** The NTC thermistor is a **resistive** sensor — its resistance varies with temperature, but this change is *not directly readable* by a microcontroller. By forming a **voltage divider** with a fixed reference resistor (R_ref = 10 kΩ matched to R₀), the resistance change is converted into a **voltage change**:

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{therm}}{R_{ref} + R_{therm}}$$

This voltage is fed to the **ADC** of the MCU. The divider also serves a second purpose: it **linearizes** the inherently exponential NTC curve over a small operating range, making the ADC reading easier to interpret.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. **Temperature must be in Kelvin** in the β-equation. Using °C directly is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks.
> 2. Many students forget the **factor of 2** in the ultrasonic distance equation — read the question carefully for "round trip" vs. "one-way."
> 3. The NTC voltage divider is **non-linear** at extreme temperatures. For precise work, use a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration, not a single-ended divider.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **data acquisition system** in a microcontroller-based embedded system. Identify the role of the **sensor**, **signal conditioner**, **multiplexer**, **ADC**, and **microcontroller**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is the front-end of any embedded measurement system. Its job is to convert **multiple real-world analog signals** into digital data that a microcontroller can process.

**Block Diagram:**

```
                ┌──────┐    ┌──────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌──────┐    ┌────────────┐
   S1 ─────────►│      │    │      │    │         │    │      │    │            │
                │      ├───►│      │    │         │    │      │    │            │
   S2 ─────────►│ MUX  │    │ Sig  │    │   ADC   ├───►│ MCU  │───►│  Memory /  │
                │      ├───►│ Cond ├───►│         │    │      │    │  Display   │
   S3 ─────────►│      │    │      │    │         │    │      │    │            │
                │      │    │      │    │         │    │      │    │            │
   S4 ─────────►│      │    │      │    │         │    │      │    │            │
                └──────┘    └──────┘    └─────────┘    └──────┘    └────────────┘
```

**Role of each block:**

1. **Sensors (S1–S4):** Convert physical quantities (temperature, light, pressure) into analog voltage/current signals.
2. **Multiplexer (MUX):** A **74HC4051**-type analog MUX allows multiple sensor channels to share a **single ADC**, reducing cost and pin count on the MCU. Channels are selected via digital address lines from the MCU.
3. **Signal Conditioner:** Performs **amplification** (Op-Amp), **filtering** (RC low-pass), **isolation**, and **level shifting** to make the raw signal ADC-ready.
4. **ADC:** Converts the conditioned analog voltage into an **n-bit digital code**. With V_ref and resolution n, the smallest detectable voltage step is V_ref / (2ⁿ – 1).
5. **Microcontroller (MCU):** Reads the ADC result, runs the application algorithm (e.g., temperature control, decision logic), drives the display or actuator, and may communicate via UART/I²C/SPI.

**(b)** An **HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor** is used to measure the water level in a tank. The echo pulse width is **t = 8.7 ms** and the ambient temperature is **T = 42°C**. **(i)** Calculate the speed of sound. **(ii)** Compute the water level if the sensor is mounted at the **top** of the tank and the **total tank height is 3 m**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Speed of sound at 42°C:**

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \times T$$

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \times 42 = 331 + 25.2 = 356.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Compute round-trip distance:**

$$d_{round} = v \times t = 356.2 \times 8.7 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$d_{round} = 3.099 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) One-way distance (sensor to water surface):**

$$d = \frac{d_{round}}{2} = \frac{3.099}{2} = 1.549 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Water level (height of water in tank):**

$$\text{Level} = \text{Total height} - d$$

$$\text{Level} = 3 \text{ m} - 1.549 \text{ m} = 1.451 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve** the round-trip distance. Always read: "echo pulse" = round trip.
> 2. **Using v = 343 m/s** (a commonly memorized constant) without temperature compensation when the question gives the temperature. KTU **specifically** tests the temperature-compensated formula `v = 331 + 0.6T`.
> 3. **Mixing up** "level of water" vs. "distance from sensor." In a top-mounted setup, the **water level = tank height – measured distance**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Recall Bullet Sheet — Read this 1 hour before the exam:**

- 🔹 A **sensor** is an **input transducer**; an **actuator** is an **output transducer**. Do not confuse.
- 🔹 **Active vs. Passive:** Active = self-generating (thermocouple, piezoelectric). Passive = needs excitation (thermistor, LDR, strain gauge).
- 🔹 **Analog vs. Digital output:** Analog → needs ADC; Digital → may already be in I²C/SPI/UART format.
- 🔹 **LM35** sensitivity = **10 mV/°C**, linear from 0°C. V_out = 10 mV × T.
- 🔹 **Thermistor β-equation** uses **Kelvin** temperature: $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta (1/T - 1/T_0)}$.
- 🔹 **Ultrasonic distance:** $d = vt/2$. Speed of sound: $v = 331 + 0.6T$ (T in °C, v in m/s).
- 🔹 **Strain gauge:** $\Delta R/R = G \cdot \epsilon$ where $G \approx 2$ for metal. Used in **Wheatstone bridge** for measurable voltage.
- 🔹 **ADC resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$. Higher n → better resolution. Higher V_ref → larger range but coarser LSB.
- 🔹 **Signal conditioning** steps (in order): Amplify → Filter → Linearize → Level-shift → Isolate.
- 🔹 **Wheatstone bridge balance:** $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$ → output = 0.
- 🔹 **Sensitivity** is the slope of the transfer function (ΔV_out / ΔX).
- 🔹 **Accuracy** ≠ **Precision.** Accuracy is closeness to true value; precision is repeatability.
- 🔹 For **digital sensors** (DS18B20, DHT22, MPU6050), the MCU uses **protocol decoding** (1-Wire, I²C, SPI) — no external ADC is needed.
- 🔹 **KTU-favorite sensors** for numerical problems: **LM35, thermistor, HC-SR04, strain gauge, LDR + voltage divider.**
- 🔹 Always mention **V_ref** when discussing ADC interfacing. It is the examiner's signal that you understand the conversion principle.
- 🔹 **Block diagram must include arrows** showing signal flow direction (sensor → conditioner → ADC → MCU → actuator).
- 🔹 Application examples to memorize: **LM35** → digital thermometer, fire alarm. **HC-SR04** → car reverse parking, water tank. **MPU6050** → drone flight controller, screen rotation. **MQ-2** → LPG leak detector.
- 🔹 **Industrial vs. medical vs. consumer** — be able to justify sensor choice based on **accuracy, range, cost, and interface type** for at least 2 applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Switch and Keyboard Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Switch and Keyboard Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — SIMPLE SWITCH AND KEYBOARD INTERFACING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Switch Interfacing** is the technique of connecting electromechanical or solid-state switching elements (tactile push-buttons, toggle switches, slide switches, DIP switches) to the digital Input/Output (I/O) pins of a microcontroller so that human or mechanical actuation can be sampled as a binary logic level (logic-0 or logic-1) by the CPU.

**Keyboard Interfacing** is the structured hardware and software methodology used to connect an organized array of keys (typically organized as a matrix of rows × columns, e.g., 4×4 or 4×3) to a limited number of microcontroller pins, employing techniques like *row scanning*, *column scanning*, *key debouncing*, and *key encoding* to identify which key has been pressed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCPT302 — Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *design, draw, and write C/assembly code* for interfacing simple switches (single and multiple), debounce them (both in hardware and software), and implement matrix keyboard scanning algorithms (both row-wise and column-wise).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting inside a closed room with **only 8 small windows** through which you can see the outside world. People outside want to tell you **"Yes"** or **"No"** by pressing one of 16 different buttons. You cannot afford 16 windows (microcontroller pins are limited and expensive). So you arrange the 16 buttons in a **4×4 grid** like an Excel sheet — 4 rows and 4 columns. You connect the 4 rows to 4 wires and the 4 columns to 4 wires (total = 8 wires = 8 windows).

To find out which button is pressed, you **send a low signal down row 1** and check whether any column wire reads low. If yes, you have found the key. If not, you move to row 2, and so on. This is exactly how **matrix keyboard scanning** works — you "illuminate" one row at a time and "listen" on the columns.

For a **single switch**, the analogy is even simpler: it is like a doorbell button. Press it → circuit closes → a logic level changes → the microcontroller "knows."

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pull-up resistor value:** Typically **10 kΩ** (range 4.7 kΩ to 100 kΩ) for CMOS/TTL logic levels.
- **Switch bounce duration:** Typically **1 ms to 20 ms** for mechanical switches; **0 ms** for solid-state switches.
- **Standard matrix keyboard sizes:** **4×4** (16 keys) and **4×3** (12 keys) are the most common.
- **Standard debounce delay:** **10 ms** to **20 ms** is the universally accepted safe value.
- **Logic levels (TTL):** Logic 0 = **0 V to 0.8 V**, Logic 1 = **2.0 V to 5.0 V**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Debouncing:**
> *Debouncing* is the process of eliminating the spurious, rapid ON/OFF transitions (mechanical bounce) that occur at the contacts of an electromechanical switch during the first few milliseconds after actuation.
