## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **List any six features of the AT89C52 microcontroller used on the trainer board.**

**Model Answer:**

The AT89C52 is an 8-bit CMOS microcontroller from the 8051 family used as the central CPU on the trainer board. Its six key features are:

1. **8 KB** of In-System Reprogrammable (ISP) Flash program memory.
2. **256 bytes** of on-chip RAM (128 bytes user + 128 bytes SFR).
3. **32 programmable I/O lines** organized as four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3).
4. **Two 16-bit** Timer/Counters (T0, T1) with four operating modes.
5. **Full-duplex UART** (serial port) on pins P3.0 (RXD) and P3.1 (TXD).
6. **6 interrupt sources** ($\overline{\text{INT0}}, \overline{\text{INT1}}, T0, T1, \text{Serial Port}, T2$) with a two-level priority structure.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark per feature pair = (0.5 + 0.5) × 6 — examiner flexibility]**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **What is the role of the 74LS138 chip on the 8051 trainer board? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

The **74LS138** is a **3-to-8 line decoder** that performs the **address-decoding** function on the trainer board. It accepts three high-order address lines (typically A15, A14, A13) as inputs and produces **eight active-LOW outputs** ($\overline{Y0}$–$\overline{Y7}$). Each output is used as a **chip-select** (CS) signal for a different peripheral (ROM, RAM, 8255, ADC, 8279, etc.), ensuring that **only one peripheral** is activated at any given instant when the 8051 issues a corresponding address. This avoids bus contention and provides each device with a unique 8 KB address window.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

---

#### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Board-Level Block Schematic

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **block-level schematic of a typical 8051 trainer board** showing the AT89C52 microcontroller, the address latch (74LS373), the address decoder (74LS138), program memory (2764 EPROM), data memory (6264 SRAM), 8255 PPI, MAX232, and ADC0808. Label all bus connections (Address, Data, Control) clearly.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **function of the 74LS373 and 74LS138** in the schematic drawn in part (a). Why is the 74LS373 required, and how does the 74LS138 generate the chip-select signal for the 8255 PPI when the 8051 issues the address **0x6003**?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Schematic:**
(Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes for the comprehensive block schematic. In the exam, the student must draw a clean rectangular block diagram with all the listed chips, the multiplexed bus line going to the 74LS373, the upper address lines going to the 74LS138, and the decoded outputs to each chip.)

**Part (b) — Functional Explanation:**

**74LS373 (Octal Transparent D-Type Latch):**
- Port 0 of the 8051 is **time-multiplexed**: it carries the lower address byte (A0–A7) during the first half of every memory cycle, and the data byte (D0–D7) during the second half.
- The 74LS373 is connected to Port 0, and its **Latch Enable (LE)** pin is driven by the 8051's **ALE** signal.
- When ALE is HIGH (first half-cycle), the latch is *transparent* and the address byte flows through to its outputs. When ALE goes LOW (second half-cycle), the latch *freezes* the address, so external memory/peripherals see a stable **A0–A7** while Port 0 is now carrying the **D0–D7** data byte.
- **Without the 74LS373, external memory would receive the data byte on its address lines during the second half — total malfunction.**

**74LS138 (3-to-8 Decoder):**
- It is connected to the high-order address lines **A15, A14, A13** (inputs C, B, A respectively).
- For the address **0x6003**, the high-order bits are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x6003 &= 0110\,0000\,0000\,0011_2 \\[4pt]
\text{Thus } A15 &= 0, \; A14 = 1, \; A13 = 1 \\[4pt]
CBA &= 011_2 = 3_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

- With CBA = 011, the decoder's output **$\overline{Y3}$** is pulled **LOW** (all other $\overline{Yi}$ remain HIGH).
- $\overline{Y3}$ is routed to the 8255's chip-select pin $\overline{\text{CS}}$ (active LOW), so the 8255 is selected.
- The 8255 then decodes the lower two address bits (A1, A0 = 11) to identify which of its four internal registers (PA, PB, PC, Control) is being accessed. In this case, A1A0 = 11 → **Control Register**.
- $\overline{\text{RD}}/\overline{\text{WR}}$ from the 8051 determines whether it is a read or a write operation.

> **[7 Marks Breakdown: (74LS373: 3 marks) + (74LS138: 2 marks) + (Address 0x6003 decoding: 2 marks)]**

---

#### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **interfacing of the ADC0808** to the AT89C52 microcontroller through the 8255 PPI. List the **features of the ADC0808** (at least six).
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A temperature sensor with an output range of **0–5 V** is connected to channel **IN2** of an ADC0808. The ADC uses a **+5 V reference** and a **640 kHz clock**. Calculate the **step size**, the **digital output for a sensor voltage of 2.7 V**, and the **total conversion time**. Write an 8051 C-program snippet that reads this channel using polling on the EOC pin.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Interfacing & Features:**

*(Block Diagram: refer to the ADC0808 connection to the 8255 in Section 4.1.)*

The sensor output goes to channel **IN2** of the ADC0808. The 8-bit digital output of the ADC0808 is read by the 8255 PPI's Port A. The 8051 communicates with the 8255 via the address bus (decoded by 74LS138) and the $\overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}$ control lines. The ADC0808's **EOC (End of Conversion)** pin is connected to one bit of Port C of the 8255 (e.g., PC.7) for status polling.

**Six Features of the ADC0808:**
1. **8-channel analog input multiplexer** (IN0–IN7).
2. **8-bit resolution** (256 quantization levels).
3. **Successive Approximation** type converter.
4. **Conversion time ≈ 100 µs** at 640 kHz clock.
5. **Latched 3-state outputs** for direct bus connection.
6. **Easy microprocessor interface** with on-chip address latch.
7. **Single +5 V supply** operation.
8. **Ratiometric** operation with reference voltage.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Step Size:**

$$
V_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{256} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{256} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV}
$$

**Digital Output for 2.7 V:**

$$
D = \dfrac{2.7 \text{ V}}{0.01953 \text{ V}} \approx 138.24 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 138 = 0x8A
$$

**Conversion Time:**

$$
t_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{8}{640 \times 10^{3}} \times 8 = 100 \text{ µs}
$$

**8051 C-Code Snippet (Polling on EOC via 8255 Port C):**

```c
#include <reg52.h>
#define ADC_CS  P1_0
#define ADC_ALE P1_1
#define ADC_OE  P1_2
#define ADC_EOC P3_2

unsigned char read_adc(unsigned char channel) {
    unsigned char result;
    P0 = (channel & 0x07);      // Select channel IN0-IN7
    ADC_ALE = 1;
    ADC_CS  = 0;
    ADC_ALE = 0;                 // Latch channel address
    ADC_CS  = 1;                 // Start conversion
    while(ADC_EOC == 0);         // Poll until EOC = 1
    ADC_OE = 0;                  // Enable output
    result = P0;                 // Read digital byte
    ADC_OE = 1;
    return result;
}
```

> **[7 Marks Breakdown: (Block diagram: 2) + (Six features: 1.5) + (Step size: 1) + (Digital output: 1) + (Conversion time: 0.5) + (C-snippet: 1)]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Board-Schematic Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 74LS373:** Many students draw the 8051 connected *directly* to external memory. The 8051's Port 0 is **multiplexed** — without the latch, the external memory receives **data on the address lines** and the system fails. This single omission can cost **2 full marks**.
>
> 2. **Mis-wiring PSEN:** The 2764 EPROM's $\overline{\text{OE}}$ must be driven by **$\overline{\text{PSEN}}$** (Program Store Enable), **not** by $\overline{\text{RD}}$. The 6264 SRAM's $\overline{\text{OE}}$ is driven by $\overline{\text{RD}}$. Confusing these two control signals is the **#1 hardware-design error**.
>
> 3. **Active-LOW Confusion on 74LS138:** The decoder outputs $\overline{Y0}$–$\overline{Y7}$ are **active-LOW**. Students often draw them as active-HIGH, leading to inverted logic in their address-decoding calculation. Get the overbar right.
>
> 4. **Skipping $\overline{\text{EA}}$:** The 8051's $\overline{\text{EA}}$ (External Access) pin must be tied to GND (= 0) for the 8051 to fetch code from external ROM after address 0x0FFF. Forgetting this means the 8051 will never execute code from the 2764 EPROM.
>
> 5. **Incorrect Baud-rate Calculation:** Using 12 MHz instead of 11.0592 MHz yields a non-integer TH1, which the 8051 cannot produce. The crystal is **always 11.0592 MHz** on KTU-standard trainer boards for serial-port work.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Board Composition:** CPU (89C52) + Latch (74LS373) + Decoder (74LS138) + Memory (2764 + 6264) + I/O (8255) + Serial (MAX232) + Analog (ADC0808 + DAC0808) + Driver (ULN2003) + KBD/Display (8279).

✅ **Bus Architecture:** 16-bit Address Bus (A0–A15), 8-bit Data Bus (D0–D7), Control Bus ($\overline{\text{PSEN}}, \overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}, \text{ALE}, \overline{\text{EA}}$).

✅ **Port 0 Rule:** Multiplexed → must be demultiplexed by **74LS373** using **ALE**.

✅ **Port 2 Rule:** Carries the upper address byte (A8–A15) directly — no latch needed.

✅ **Decoder Rule:** 74LS138 inputs are typically A15, A14, A13 → outputs $\overline{Y0}$–$\overline{Y7}$ give **8 chips** of **8 KB** each.

✅ **PSEN vs RD:** PSEN → program memory (ROM), RD → data memory (RAM) & I/O.

✅ **TH1 = 0xFD** for 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal — memorize this *cold*.

✅ **ADC0808** = 8 channels, 8-bit, 100 µs conversion, **EOC** pin for status.

✅ **MAX232** = charge-pump generates ±10 V from +5 V using 4 capacitors.

✅ **ULN2003** = 7 Darlington pairs, 500 mA per channel, built-in flyback diodes.

✅ **8255** = 24 I/O lines, three modes, BSR mode for Port C bit manipulation.

✅ **DAC0808** = current output → external op-amp → voltage output.

✅ **8279** = offloads keyboard scanning and display refresh from the CPU.

✅ **Crystal Frequency:** **11.0592 MHz** is the universal KTU choice for UART-friendly baud-rate generation.

✅ **Hardware Pin Caution:** $\overline{\text{EA}}$ → **GND** for external program memory access.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPIO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GPIO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** is a generic, software-configurable digital pin on a microcontroller whose behavior (direction, drive mode, pull configuration, and speed) can be dynamically controlled by the CPU through a set of memory-mapped Special Function Registers (SFRs). In ARM Cortex-M based microcontrollers (such as the NXP LPC2148 / STM32 families), each GPIO port exposes **up to 32 independently programmable pins** that can interface LEDs, switches, relays, sensors, and act as the physical bridge between the on-chip peripherals (UART, SPI, I²C, timers) and the external world.

### 1.1.1 Why GPIO is the "Swiss Army Knife" of Embedded Pins

Unlike fixed-function pins (e.g., a dedicated `VDD` or `RESET`), a GPIO pin is **multiplexed**. One physical pin can serve as:
1. A **digital input** (read a switch, button, sensor logic).
2. A **digital output** (drive an LED, relay coil, buzzer).
3. An **alternate function input/output** (route to UART TX/RX, SPI MOSI/MISO, I²C SDA/SCL, PWM, ADC trigger).
4. An **analog channel** (on specific pins, route to the ADC).

The decision of *which role* the pin plays is made entirely in software by writing to configuration registers — **no soldering iron or hardware rework required**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Power Socket" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a GPIO pin as a Universal Power Socket on your wall.**

| Wall Socket Feature | GPIO Equivalent | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Socket has 2/3 pins | Pin has multiple internal modes | Pin direction: Input or Output |
| You decide to plug a fan, light, or charger | You decide the pin's job at runtime | Software selects role via registers |
| Some sockets have a switch (ON/OFF) | Pin can be High (1) or Low (0) | Logic voltage levels |
| Some sockets have a child lock (safety) | Pull-up / Pull-down resistors | Default known state when floating |
| Some sockets are dimmable (controlled current) | Output drive strength / slew rate | OSPEEDR register setting |
| Some sockets are shared with a data line | Alternate function mode | AF0–AF4 multiplexing |
| The socket has no power until you flip the breaker | Pin is unconfigured until clock + register setup | Must enable GPIO clock first |

**Bottom line:** Just as a socket is useless until you plug something in, a GPIO pin is useless until software *configures its direction* and *drives or reads* its value.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key GPIO electrical parameters (must memorize for KTU 2024 exams):**
> - **V_IL (Input Low Voltage):** ≤ **0.3 × V_DD** (typically ≤ 0.8 V @ 3.3 V)
> - **V_IH (Input High Voltage):** ≥ **0.7 × V_DD** (typically ≥ 2.0 V @ 3.3 V)
> - **V_OL (Output Low Voltage):** ≤ **0.4 V** when sinking **8 mA** (TTL-compatible)
> - **V_OH (Output High Voltage):** ≥ **2.4 V** when sourcing **8 mA**
> - **Maximum source/sink current per pin (LPC2148):** **40 mA** (absolute max)
> - **Maximum total current per port (8 pins):** **100 mA** (thermal limit)
> - **Pin toggle speed (OSPEEDR = 11 on STM32):** up to **50 MHz** (High Speed) / **100 MHz** (Very High Speed)
> - **Pull-up / Pull-down resistance (STM32):** ≈ **40 kΩ** (weak internal)

---

## 1.4 GPIO in the KTU 2024 Scheme — Where it Fits

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mapping for PCCPT302 Module 4 (GPIO):**
> - **CO4 (Mapped):** *Design and develop embedded systems using ARM microcontrollers.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** *Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4)* — Students must *configure* registers, *interpret* schematic logic, and *analyze* timing/drivability.
> - **Prerequisite for:** External Interrupts, Timer capture/compare, ADC trigger logic, and on-chip peripheral routing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GPIO Hardware Architecture — Inside One Pin

Each GPIO pin is backed by a chain of controllable hardware blocks. To "use" a pin, you must walk through this chain in order:

### 2.1.1 The 5-Stage Configuration Pipeline

1. **Stage 1 — Clock Gating (Power Domain Enable)**
   - On ARM Cortex-M3 chips (LPC2148 / STM32), every peripheral sits behind a clock gate.
   - **LPC2148:** `PCONP` (Power Control for Peripherals) register — bit `PCGPIO` must be set to 1.
   - **STM32:** `RCC->AHB1ENR` — set the corresponding bit (e.g., `RCC_AHB1ENR_GPIOAEN`).
   - *Why?* Power saving. Unused pins draw **0 µA** when clock is off.

2. **Stage 2 — Pin Mode Selection (Direction + Function)**
   - **LPC2148:** `PINSEL0..PINSEL2` (2 bits per pin) — choose among `00`=GPIO, `01/10/11`=Alternate Functions 1/2/3.
   - **STM32:** `GPIOx->MODER` (2 bits per pin) — `00`=Input, `01`=Output, `10`=Alternate, `11`=Analog.

3. **Stage 3 — Output Type & Drive Characteristic**
   - **LPC2148:** Implicit in pin function; no separate register (uses CMOS push-pull by default).
   - **STM32:** `GPIOx->OTYPER` — `0`=Push-Pull, `1`=Open-Drain.
   - **STM32:** `GPIOx->OSPEEDR` (2 bits/pin) — `00`=Low (2 MHz), `01`=Medium (25 MHz), `10`=Fast (50 MHz), `11`=High (100 MHz).

4. **Stage 4 — Internal Resistor Selection (Pull-up / Pull-down)**
   - **LPC2148:** `PINMODE0..PINMODE2` — `00`=Pull-up, `01`=Repeater (latch), `10`=Tri-state (no pull), `11`=Pull-down.
   - **STM32:** `GPIOx->PUPDR` — `00`=No pull, `01`=Pull-up, `10`=Pull-down, `11`=Reserved.

5. **Stage 5 — Data Read / Write**
   - **Read:** `GPIOx->IDR` (Input Data Register) — read-only, 16/32-bit.
   - **Write:** `GPIOx->ODR` (Output Data Register) — read/write, 16/32-bit.
   - **Bit-Banding (Cortex-M3/M4):** Atomic bit manipulation at address `0x42000000 + (RegisterAddr − 0x40000000)×32 + bit×4`.
   - **LPC2148 shortcut:** `IOSET`, `IOCLR`, `IOPIN` (set-only, clear-only, full read/write).

---

## 2.2 Output Drive Topologies — Push-Pull vs Open-Drain

### 2.2.1 Push-Pull Output
- Uses **two transistors** (one to V_DD, one to GND). When output is `1`, the upper transistor conducts → pin is driven **high**. When `0`, the lower transistor conducts → pin is driven **low**.
- **Pros:** Active drive in both directions, sharp edges, full V_OH / V_OL swing.
- **Cons:** Cannot safely wire-OR multiple outputs (bus contention).
- **Use case:** LEDs, logic-level signalling to a single receiver.

### 2.2.2 Open-Drain Output
- Only the **lower transistor to GND** exists. Output `0` actively pulls low; output `1` **releases the pin** (high-impedance / floating).
- **External pull-up resistor (typically 4.7 kΩ)** is required to reach logic-high.
- **Pros:** Safe to wire-AND multiple outputs (e.g., I²C SDA/SCL bus, multi-button interrupt lines).
- **Cons:** Rise-time limited by RC time constant of pull-up × bus capacitance.
- **Use case:** I²C bus, open-collector interrupt lines, level shifting to higher voltage rails.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** *Push-Pull = "Push and Pull both ways" (strong drive).* *Open-Drain = "Open to high, drains to low" (needs external pull-up).*

---

## 2.3 Input Modes — Floating, Pull-up, Pull-down, Schmitt Trigger

A digital input pin samples the external voltage and digitizes it. To avoid undefined behavior on a **floating** pin (which can oscillate and draw CMOS shoot-through current), pull resistors bias the pin to a known state:

| Mode | Default State (External Pin Open) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Floating (No pull)** | Undefined / noisy | Only when external circuit drives a defined level (e.g., driven by another IC output). |
| **Pull-up (internal ≈ 40 kΩ)** | Logic `1` | Active-low switches (button to GND). |
| **Pull-down (internal ≈ 40 kΩ)** | Logic `0` | Active-high switches (button to V_DD). |
| **Repeater (LPC2148 only)** | Latches the last driven value | Bus keeper applications. |

All GPIO inputs pass through a **Schmitt Trigger** — a hysteresis comparator that rejects small noise on slowly rising/falling edges. The hysteresis band is typically:
$$\Delta V_{HYS} = V_{T+} - V_{T-} \approx 0.1 \times V_{DD}$$
At $V_{DD} = 3.3\text{ V}$, this gives ~**0.33 V** of noise immunity.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key GPIO equations and parameters for KTU 2024 examinations. Memorize the symbols, not just the formulas.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output High Voltage | $V_{OH} \geq 2.4$ | V | When sourcing $I_{OH} = 8$ mA |
| 2 | Output Low Voltage | $V_{OL} \leq 0.4$ | V | When sinking $I_{OL} = 8$ mA |
| 3 | Input High Threshold | $V_{IH} \geq 0.7 \cdot V_{DD}$ | V | TTL-compatible logic |
| 4 | Input Low Threshold | $V_{IL} \leq 0.3 \cdot V_{DD}$ | V | TTL-compatible logic |
| 5 | Schmitt Hysteresis | $V_{HYS} = V_{T+} - V_{T-} \approx 0.1 V_{DD}$ | V | Noise margin band |
| 6 | LED Current (series resistor) | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{F,LED}}{R_{S}}$ | A | $V_{F,LED} \approx 1.8$–$3.0$ V (red/green) |
| 7 | Pull-up RC Rise Time (Open-Drain) | $\tau = R_{PU} \cdot C_{bus}$ | s | $C_{bus}$ = total line capacitance |
| 8 | 10–90% Rise Time | $t_{r} \approx 2.2 \cdot R_{PU} C_{bus}$ | s | One-pole exponential approx. |
| 9 | Max Pin Current (LPC2148) | $I_{pin,max} = 40$ | mA | Absolute maximum per pin |
| 10 | Max Port Current (LPC2148) | $I_{port,max} = 100$ | mA | 8 pins simultaneously |
| 11 | Power Dissipated in Pin | $P_{pin} = V_{OL} \cdot I_{OL} \cdot N_{low} + V_{DD} \cdot I_{LEAK} \cdot N_{hi}$ | W | Used for thermal design |
| 12 | Maximum Toggle Frequency | $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot (t_{r} + t_{f})}$ | Hz | Limited by OSPEEDR / capacitive load |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **LED Matrix Displays** — Multiplex rows/columns by rapidly toggling GPIO with controlled current-limiting resistors.
2. **Keypad Scanning** — Drive rows as outputs, read columns as inputs; debounce in software.
3. **Relay & Solenoid Driver** — GPIO drives a transistor base; transistor switches the inductive load. **Flyback diode** mandatory across inductive loads.
4. **Seven-Segment Display** — 7 data lines + 1 digit select line per digit; time-multiplexed.
5. **Chip-to-Chip Interconnect** — When alternate-function pins are unavailable, *bit-banged* GPIO implements software SPI/I²C/UART.
6. **Wake-up Source** — On many MCUs, only GPIO pins can wake the chip from deep-sleep (`STOP`/`STANDBY` mode).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations — Three Classic KTU Numerical Patterns

### 3.1.1 Derivation 1: Sizing the Current-Limiting Resistor for an LED

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** *An LPC2148 GPIO pin (V_DD = 3.3 V) drives a red LED ($V_F = 2.0$ V) through a series resistor $R_S$. The LED is rated $I_F = 10$ mA. Calculate $R_S$ and verify that the GPIO pin can source the required current safely.*

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit topology.**
This is a simple series loop: $V_{DD} \rightarrow R_S \rightarrow \text{LED anode} \rightarrow \text{LED cathode} \rightarrow \text{GPIO pin}$ (when configured as output LOW). The current $I_F$ flows when the GPIO pin is LOW (current-sink configuration, *most common* in KTU questions).

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.**
$$V_{DD} - V_{R_S} - V_F - V_{OL} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor.**
$$V_{R_S} = V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.** Use $V_{OL,max} = 0.4$ V (worst-case from datasheet).
$$V_{R_S} = 3.3 - 2.0 - 0.4 = 0.9 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law to find $R_S$.**
$$R_S = \frac{V_{R_S}}{I_F} = \frac{0.9 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ mA}} = 90 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Choose the nearest standard E12 value.** $R_S = 100 \text{ }\Omega$ (safer) or $82 \text{ }\Omega$ (slightly brighter). The student must *justify* the choice.

**Step 7 — Recompute actual LED current with $R_S = 100 \text{ }\Omega$.**
$$I_{F,actual} = \frac{V_{R_S}}{R_S} = \frac{0.9}{100} = 9 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 8 — Check GPIO current budget.**
$I_{F,actual} = 9 \text{ mA} \ll 40 \text{ mA}$ (per-pin max). **Safe.** ✓
If 8 such LEDs are driven in parallel: $8 \times 9 = 72 \text{ mA} < 100 \text{ mA}$ (per-port max). **Still safe.** ✓

**Final Answer:** $R_S = 100 \text{ }\Omega$ (standard), LED current = 9 mA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always use the *worst-case* $V_{OL}$ from the datasheet (0.4 V), not the typical 0.1 V. Students who use $V_{OL}=0$ get inflated currents and lose 1 mark.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation 2: Open-Drain Rise Time for an I²C Bus

> **Problem Statement:** *An open-drain GPIO pin pulls up to 3.3 V through $R_{PU} = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$. The bus capacitance (line + all device pins) is $C_{bus} = 100$ pF. Calculate the 0% → 63% rise time ($\tau$) and the 10% → 90% rise time ($t_r$).*

**Step 1 — Recognize the equivalent circuit.**
When the open-drain NMOS turns OFF, the line is pulled high by $R_{PU}$ charging $C_{bus}$ toward $V_{DD}$. This is a first-order RC charging circuit.

**Step 2 — Write the governing differential equation.**
$$V_{DD} = i(t) \cdot R_{PU} + V_C(t), \quad i(t) = C_{bus} \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Combine and solve.**
$$V_{DD} = R_{PU} C_{bus} \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C$$
$$\frac{dV_C}{V_{DD} - V_C} = \frac{dt}{R_{PU} C_{bus}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $V_C(0) = 0$ to $V_C(t) = V_C$.**
$$\int_0^{V_C} \frac{dV'_C}{V_{DD} - V'_C} = \int_0^t \frac{dt'}{R_{PU} C_{bus}}$$
$$-\ln(V_{DD} - V_C) + \ln(V_{DD}) = \frac{t}{R_{PU} C_{bus}}$$
$$\ln\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_C}\right) = \frac{t}{R_{PU} C_{bus}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_C(t)$.**
$$V_C(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t / (R_{PU} C_{bus})}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Define the time constant.**
$$\tau = R_{PU} \cdot C_{bus} = 4.7 \times 10^3 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} = 470 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 7 — At $t = \tau$, $V_C = 0.632 V_{DD}$ — the 63% rise time.**

**Step 8 — Calculate the 10% → 90% rise time $t_r$.**
For $V_C = 0.1 V_{DD}$: $t_{10\%} = -\tau \ln(0.9) = 0.1054 \tau$
For $V_C = 0.9 V_{DD}$: $t_{90\%} = -\tau \ln(0.1) = 2.3026 \tau$

**Step 9 — Subtract.**
$$t_r = t_{90\%} - t_{10\%} = \tau (2.3026 - 0.1054) = 2.1972 \tau$$
$$t_r \approx 2.2 \cdot \tau = 2.2 \times 470 \text{ ns} \approx 1.03 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 10 — Translate to maximum bus clock frequency.**
For reliable I²C at 50% duty cycle:
$$f_{max} \approx \frac{1}{10 \cdot t_r} = \frac{1}{10 \times 1.03 \text{ }\mu\text{s}} \approx 97 \text{ kHz}$$
This comfortably supports **Standard Mode (100 kHz)** I²C. For Fast Mode (400 kHz), $R_{PU}$ must be reduced or $C_{bus}$ must be smaller.

**Final Answer:** $\tau = 470$ ns, $t_r \approx 1.03 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$, max reliable $f \approx 97$ kHz.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation 3: Power Dissipation in a GPIO Pin Driving a Resistive Load

> **Problem Statement:** *A GPIO pin sources current to a 1 kΩ load tied to GND, with $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V. Compute the power dissipated (a) in the load and (b) in the GPIO output stage (transistor) when the output is HIGH.*

**Step 1 — Compute load current when output is HIGH.**
$$I_{OH} = \frac{V_{OH} - 0}{R_{load}} \approx \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 3.3 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute power dissipated in the load.**
$$P_{load} = I_{OH}^2 \cdot R_{load} = (3.3 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 1000 = 10.89 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute power dissipated in the GPIO output transistor.**
The PMOS transistor drops $V_{DD} - V_{OH} \approx V_{DD} - V_{DD} = 0$ in the ideal case. In practice, the on-resistance $R_{DS,on}$ of the PMOS causes a small drop, typically $R_{DS,on} \approx 25 \text{ }\Omega$ at 3.3 V on a 5-V tolerant pin:
$$P_{MOS} = I_{OH}^2 \cdot R_{DS,on} = (3.3 \text{ mA})^2 \cdot 25 \text{ }\Omega = 0.27 \text{ }\mu\text{W} \approx 0$$

**Step 4 — Total power.**
$$P_{total} = P_{load} + P_{MOS} \approx 10.89 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 5 — Check the safe operating area (SOA).**
At 3.3 mA, the pin is well within its 40 mA limit. Power is negligible, no thermal concern.

**Final Answer:** $P_{load} \approx 10.89$ mW, $P_{MOS} \approx 0$ (PMOS drop negligible).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** A common trick question reverses the topology — a *current-sink* circuit. The roles of PMOS and NMOS swap, and the same derivation applies with $V_{OL}$ as the transistor drop.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — GPIO Decision Flowchart (KTU Viva)

| Decision Node | Question to Ask | Register Action (STM32) | Register Action (LPC2148) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1** | Is the peripheral clock enabled? | `RCC->AHB1ENR \|= (1<<0);` (Port A) | `PCONP \|= (1<<15);` (GPIO) |
| **Q2** | Pin function = GPIO or alternate? | `MODER` bits = `01` (output) / `00` (input) | `PINSEL0` bits = `00` (GPIO) |
| **Q3** | Push-pull or open-drain? | `OTYPER` bit = `0` or `1` | Implicit push-pull |
| **Q4** | Output speed? | `OSPEEDR` bits = `00/01/10/11` | N/A (fixed 1×) |
| **Q5** | Internal pull-up/down? | `PUPDR` bits = `00/01/10` | `PINMODE0` bits = `00/01/10/11` |
| **Q6** | To set pin HIGH? | `BSRR = (1<<5);` (atomic set) | `IOSET0 = (1<<5);` (set-only) |
| **Q7** | To set pin LOW? | `BSRR = (1<<(5+16));` (atomic clear) | `IOCLR0 = (1<<5);` (clear-only) |
| **Q8** | To read pin? | `if (IDR & (1<<5))` | `if ((IOPIN0 & (1<<5)) == 0)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Bit-Banding Confusion:** On Cortex-M3, writing `ODR |= (1<<5);` is a **read-modify-write** — not atomic. If an interrupt fires between the read and write, the pin state can corrupt. Use `BSRR` (STM32) or `IOSET/IOCLR` (LPC2148) for atomic updates.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GPIO Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["APB or AHB Bus from CPU Core"] --> B["Clock Gating and Power Control PCONP or RCC AHB1ENR"]
    B --> C["Pin Function Select PINSEL0 to PINSEL2 or MODER"]
    C --> D["Output Type Push Pull or Open Drain"]
    C --> E["Pull Up or Pull Down Resistor 40 kOhm typical"]
    D --> F["Output Drive Stage PMOS and NMOS Transistors"]
    E --> G["Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis Comparator"]
    F --> H["Physical Pad Pin 5 of Port 0"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Input Data Register IDR or IOPIN"]
    F --> J["Output Data Register ODR or IOSET and IOCLR"]
    J --> F
    K["Alternate Function Mux AF1 to AF4"] --> F
    K --> G
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The bus (left) carries configuration commands from the CPU.
- The clock gate must be opened first (otherwise the registers cannot be written).
- The pin function selector decides *which* of three internal paths reaches the pad: GPIO input, GPIO output, or alternate-function peripheral.
- The output stage and Schmitt trigger share the physical pad through a multiplexing structure.
- The IDR/ODR are the only two registers an application programmer routinely reads/writes.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — GPIO Output Configuration Flow (KTU Practical)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CPU as CPU Firmware
    participant CLK as Clock Control PCONP
    participant SEL as Pin Select PINSEL0
    participant DIR as Direction IODIR
    participant OD as Output Data IOSET or IOCLR
    participant PAD as Physical Pin Pad
    CPU->>CLK: Step 1 - Write 1 to PCGPIO bit in PCONP
    CLK-->>CPU: Step 2 - Ack GPIO peripheral is clocked
    CPU->>SEL: Step 3 - Clear PINSEL0 bits for pin 0_5
    SEL-->>CPU: Step 4 - Ack pin 0_5 is now GPIO mode
    CPU->>DIR: Step 5 - Set bit 5 in IODIR0 to make output
    DIR-->>CPU: Step 6 - Ack direction latch updated
    CPU->>OD: Step 7 - Write 1 to IOSET0 bit 5
    OD-->>PAD: Step 8 - Drive PMOS ON, pad voltage rises to VDD
    Note over PAD: LED ON, current flows through RS
    CPU->>OD: Step 9 - Write 1 to IOCLR0 bit 5
    OD-->>PAD: Step 10 - Drive NMOS ON, pad voltage falls to 0V
    Note over PAD: LED OFF
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — Push-Pull vs Open-Drain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUSHPULL["Push Pull Configuration"]
        PP1["VDD 3.3V"] -->|PMOS ON| PPM["Pin Output"]
        PPM -->|NMOS ON| PPN["GND 0V"]
        PPN -.->|NEVER both ON| PPM
    end
    subgraph OPDRAIN["Open Drain Configuration"]
        OD1["VDD 3.3V"] -->|via Ext R_PU 4.7k| ODM["Pin Output"]
        ODM -->|NMOS ON to GND| ODG["GND 0V"]
        OD1 -.->|No internal path| ODM
    end
    style PUSHPULL fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400
    style OPDRAIN fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
    style PP1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style PPN fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PPM fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style OD1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style ODG fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ODM fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the push-pull stage (left), the pin is *actively driven* to V_DD by the PMOS or to GND by the NMOS — never floating. In the open-drain stage (right), the only active path is to GND via the NMOS; the high-level is reached passively through the external pull-up resistor.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Pin Lifecycle in Embedded Firmware

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> Clocked: PCONP bit set
    Clocked --> FunctionSelected: PINSEL0 cleared for GPIO
    FunctionSelected --> Configured: IODIR or MODER set
    Configured --> OutputHigh: IOSET or BSRR set
    Configured --> OutputLow: IOCLR or BSRR reset
    OutputHigh --> OutputLow: IOCLR triggered
    OutputLow --> OutputHigh: IOSET triggered
    OutputHigh --> InputMode: Direction flipped to read
    OutputLow --> InputMode: Direction flipped to read
    Configured --> InputMode: IODIR cleared
    InputMode --> OutputHigh: Direction restored
    InputMode --> OutputLow: Direction restored
    Configured --> AlternateFunction: PINSEL set to non-zero
    AlternateFunction --> Configured: PINSEL cleared
    Configured --> Uninitialized: PCONP bit cleared power save
    Uninitialized --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Push-Pull and Open-Drain output configurations of a GPIO pin. State one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Push-Pull | Open-Drain |
|---|---|---|
| Active high drive | Yes (PMOS to V_DD) | No — only passive pull-up required |
| Active low drive | Yes (NMOS to GND) | Yes (NMOS to GND) |
| External resistor | Not required | Pull-up mandatory |
| Wire-AND on bus | Not allowed (contention) | Allowed (safe bus sharing) |
| Edge sharpness | Sharp (active edges) | Slow rise (RC of pull-up × C_bus) |
| **Preferred application** | Driving an LED, logic level to a single IC | I²C SDA/SCL bus, multi-source interrupt line (wired-OR) |

> **Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for table, 1 mark for application.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the purpose of pull-up and pull-down resistors in a GPIO input pin. What happens if neither is enabled on a floating input?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Purpose (2 marks):** Pull-up/pull-down resistors provide a *defined default logic level* when no external device is actively driving the pin. A pull-up biases the pin to logic `1` (V_DD); a pull-down biases it to logic `0` (GND). This prevents the input from floating at an indeterminate voltage when a connected switch, button, or sensor is in its open/inactive state.

2. **Floating-pin consequence (1 mark):** If a digital input pin is left floating with no internal or external pull resistor, the input voltage can hover anywhere between GND and V_DD, picking up stray noise capacitively. This causes:
   - Erratic, non-deterministic read values (0/1 random).
   - **Shoot-through current** in the input buffer's CMOS pair — both NMOS and PMOS partially ON simultaneously, drawing several mA of quiescent current per pin and *damaging the device over time*.
   - False interrupt triggers if the pin is configured as an external interrupt source.

> **Typical values:** Internal pull-up/down on STM32 ≈ **40 kΩ**. External pull-up for open-drain I²C bus ≈ **4.7 kΩ**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a GPIO pin in an ARM Cortex-M3 microcontroller (LPC2148). List and briefly describe any **four** configuration registers used to set up a GPIO pin as a digital output.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A GPIO pin on an LPC2148 (V_DD = 3.3 V) drives a green LED ($V_F$ = 2.2 V, $I_F$ = 12 mA) through a current-limiting resistor $R_S$. The LED is wired in current-sink mode (cathode to GPIO, anode to V_DD via $R_S$).
>   (i) Calculate the required value of $R_S$.
>   (ii) Find the nearest standard E12 resistor value and recompute the actual LED current.
>   (iii) Verify that the per-pin current limit (40 mA) and per-port limit (100 mA) are not violated if 6 such LEDs are multiplexed on the same port.

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture & Registers

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)*

**Configuration Registers (any 4):**

1. **`PCONP` (Power Control for Peripherals Register):** A 32-bit register where setting bit 15 (`PCGPIO`) enables the clock to the GPIO module. Until this is set, all GPIO register writes are ignored — the peripheral is in power-down state.

2. **`PINSEL0`, `PINSEL1`, `PINSEL2` (Pin Function Select Registers):** Each pin is allocated 2 bits in these registers. The encoding is: `00` = GPIO, `01`/=`10`/=`11` = Alternate Functions 1/2/3 respectively. For example, P0.0 uses bits [1:0] of PINSEL0; P0.5 uses bits [11:10] of PINSEL0.

3. **`IODIR0` / `IODIR1` (GPIO Direction Registers):** A `1` bit configures the corresponding pin as output; a `0` bit configures it as input. These are reset to `0` (all inputs) on power-up, ensuring no accidental outputs drive bus conflicts at startup.

4. **`PINMODE0`, `PINMODE1`, `PINMODE2` (Pin Mode Registers):** Configure the on-chip pull-up/pull-down/repeater/tri-state behavior of input pins. Encoding: `00` = on-chip pull-up enabled, `01` = repeater mode (latches last driven value), `10` = tri-state (no pull), `11` = on-chip pull-down enabled.

5. **(Optional)** **`IOSET0` / `IOCLR0` (Output Set / Clear Registers):** Set-only and clear-only registers that allow atomic pin manipulation. Writing `1` to bit *n* of `IOSET0` drives pin *n* HIGH; writing `1` to bit *n* of `IOCLR0` drives pin *n* LOW. Writing `0` is a no-op — so no read-modify-write race condition.

> **Marks Breakdown:** Diagram 3 marks, Register descriptions 4 marks (1 each).

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**(i) Calculate $R_S$.**

In current-sink mode, the GPIO pin pulls the LED cathode to LOW (≈ $V_{OL}$). The current path is V_DD → R_S → LED anode → LED cathode → GPIO NMOS → GND.

Apply KVL:
$$V_{DD} - I_F \cdot R_S - V_F - V_{OL} = 0$$

Solve for $R_S$ using worst-case $V_{OL} = 0.4$ V:
$$R_S = \frac{V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL}}{I_F} = \frac{3.3 - 2.2 - 0.4}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.7}{0.012} \approx 58.3 \text{ }\Omega$$

**(ii) Nearest E12 standard value.**

E12 series: 10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82 Ω. The nearest standard value to 58.3 Ω is **$R_S = 56 \text{ }\Omega$** (slightly brighter, safer for LED brightness) or **$R_S = 68 \text{ }\Omega$** (slightly dimmer, longer LED life).

Choosing $R_S = 56 \text{ }\Omega$:
$$I_{F,actual} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL}}{R_S} = \frac{0.7}{56} = 12.5 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Verify port and pin limits.**

- Per-pin current = 12.5 mA < 40 mA. ✓
- Per-port current (6 LEDs ON simultaneously) = $6 \times 12.5 = 75$ mA < 100 mA. ✓

**All limits satisfied.** Design is electrically safe.

> **Marks Breakdown:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Bit-Banding feature of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor. How does it enable atomic GPIO bit manipulation? Derive the bit-band alias address formula and compute the bit-band alias address for **bit 12 of GPIO Port 0 Output Set Register (IOSET0)** on LPC2148.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** An open-drain GPIO pin on an STM32F4 is connected to an I²C-like bus. The external pull-up resistor is $R_{PU} = 2.2$ kΩ, the bus capacitance is $C_{bus} = 150$ pF, and $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V. Calculate:
>   (i) The RC time constant $\tau$.
>   (ii) The 10% → 90% rise time $t_r$.
>   (iii) The maximum theoretical bus toggle frequency, and the maximum standard I²C mode (100 kHz / 400 kHz) supported by this hardware.

#### Model Solution — (a) Bit-Banding

**Concept (3 marks):** Bit-banding is a memory-mapping feature of the ARM Cortex-M3 (and M4) where a single bit in the "bit-band region" (the alias region starting at `0x22000000` for SRAM, `0x42000000` for peripherals) maps to a full **32-bit word** in the underlying bit-band alias. This allows atomic, single-instruction read-modify-write operations on individual bits without read-modify-write hazards.

**GPIO Application (1 mark):** Without bit-banding, setting GPIO bit 5 requires `GPIOB->ODR |= (1<<5);` which is a 3-step (read, OR, write) sequence — interrupt-unsafe. With bit-banding, writing to the alias address at `0x42xxxxxx` directly sets/clears the bit atomically in one bus cycle.

**Address Derivation (2 marks):** The bit-band alias address is given by:
$$A_{alias} = A_{band\_base} + (A_{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (bit\_number \times 4)$$

Where:
- $A_{band\_base} = 0x42000000$ for the peripheral bit-band region.
- $A_{byte\_offset} = A_{reg} - 0x40000000$ is the byte offset of the register from the peripheral base.
- *bit_number* is the bit position (0–7 for IOSET0 since it's byte-addressable here, or 0–31 for full-word registers).

**Numerical Computation (1 mark):**

For LPC2148, the IOSET0 register is at address `0xE0028004` (after PINSEL/IO0DIR).
$$A_{byte\_offset} = 0xE0028004 - 0x40000000 = 0xA0028004$$

$$A_{alias} = 0x42000000 + (0xA0028004 \times 32) + (12 \times 4)$$

$$A_{alias} = 0x42000000 + 0x14005000080 + 0x30$$

$$A_{alias} = 0x180050000B0$$

> **Note to examiner:** The exact address computation can be simplified by noting that for LPC2148, the GPIO registers fall outside the Cortex-M3 peripheral bit-band region (which maps `0x40000000`–`0x400FFFFF` to `0x42000000`–`0x43FFFFFF`). LPC2148 GPIO registers at `0xE0028xxx` are *not* bit-bandable in the strict sense — they use the legacy fast-GPIO port. Mention this nuance for full marks.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**(i) RC time constant (2 marks).**
$$\tau = R_{PU} \cdot C_{bus} = 2.2 \times 10^3 \cdot 150 \times 10^{-12} = 330 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) 10% → 90% rise time (3 marks).**

Using the one-pole exponential charging equation derived in Section 3.1.2:
$$V_C(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

For 10% point:
$$0.1 V_{DD} = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t_{10}/\tau}) \Rightarrow t_{10} = -\tau \ln(0.9) = 0.1054 \tau$$

For 90% point:
$$0.9 V_{DD} = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t_{90}/\tau}) \Rightarrow t_{90} = -\tau \ln(0.1) = 2.3026 \tau$$

$$t_r = t_{90} - t_{10} = 2.1972 \tau \approx 2.2 \tau$$
$$t_r = 2.2 \times 330 \text{ ns} = 726 \text{ ns}$$

**(iii) Maximum bus frequency (2 marks).**

For a symmetric bus cycle with 50% duty and 4 rise/fall transitions per period (worst case, since each bit has 2 edges), the maximum frequency is conservatively:
$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{4 \cdot t_r} = \frac{1}{4 \times 726 \text{ ns}} \approx 344 \text{ kHz}$$

More practical bound (one full period = 5× rise time for reliable settling):
$$f_{max,practical} = \frac{1}{5 \times 726 \text{ ns}} \approx 275 \text{ kHz}$$

**Conclusion:** This hardware **supports Standard Mode I²C (100 kHz) easily**, and is on the boundary of **Fast Mode (400 kHz)** — operation at 400 kHz would require reducing $R_{PU}$ to 1 kΩ or reducing $C_{bus}$ through better board design.

> **Marks Breakdown:** (i) 2, (ii) 3, (iii) 2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks on GPIO questions (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Clock Enable step (–1 to –2 marks):** On LPC2148, students write `IODIR0 = ...` without first setting `PCONP |= (1<<15)`. The write silently fails, the code does nothing, and the student gets zero credit for the "output" portion.
>
> 2. **Confusing PINSEL0/1/2 bit mapping (–1 mark):** Each pin's 2-bit field is at a *different* bit position in PINSEL0, PINSEL1, PINSEL2. Writing to the wrong bits leaves the pin in alternate-function mode by default (e.g., UART0 TX on P0.0).
>
> 3. **Using $V_{OL} = 0$ in LED current calculations (–1 mark):** Datasheet specifies $V_{OL,max} = 0.4$ V. Using 0 inflates $R_S$ and produces under-driven LEDs.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the flyback diode on relay/solenoid loads (–1 mark):** A GPIO pin driving a relay coil *without* a reverse-biased diode across the coil will see hundreds of volts of inductive kickback and latch-up.
>
> 5. **Confusing current-source vs current-sink direction (–1 mark):** LED anode-cathode orientation reverses whether the GPIO sources or sinks current. Get this wrong and the LED never lights.
>
> 6. **Not clearing MODER/PINSEL before setting up alternate function (–2 marks):** Pins default to *analog* mode on STM32 after reset — a stale `MODER` value can silently route the pin to ADC instead of GPIO.
>
> 7. **Skipping debounce for mechanical switches (–1 mark):** A GPIO read of a push-button yields multiple transitions per press. Students must include a software/hardware debounce (10–50 ms RC or counter-based).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GPIO — Final 60-Second Revision (Print This Section Before Exam):**

### ✅ Hardware Essentials
- [ ] GPIO = General Purpose Input/Output, software-configurable digital pin.
- [ ] LPC2148 has 2 ports (P0, P1) of 32 pins each; STM32 has ports A–I of 16 pins each.
- [ ] Always enable the peripheral clock **first** (`PCONP` for LPC2148, `RCC->AHBxENR` for STM32).

### ✅ Configuration Registers (LPC2148) — Memorize
- [ ] `PINSEL0/1/2` → function select (00 = GPIO).
- [ ] `IODIR0/1` → 1 = output, 0 = input.
- [ ] `IOSET0/1` → write 1 to set HIGH (atomic).
- [ ] `IOCLR0/1` → write 1 to set LOW (atomic).
- [ ] `IOPIN0/1` → read current pin state.
- [ ] `PINMODE0/1/2` → pull-up / pull-down / tri-state / repeater.

### ✅ Configuration Registers (STM32) — Memorize
- [ ] `MODER` → 00=In, 01=Out, 10=AF, 11=Analog.
- [ ] `OTYPER` → 0=Push-Pull, 1=Open-Drain.
- [ ] `OSPEEDR` → 00/01/10/11 = 2/25/50/100 MHz.
- [ ] `PUPDR` → 00=None, 01=Up, 10=Down.
- [ ] `IDR` → read input (read-only).
- [ ] `ODR` → write output (read-write).
- [ ] `BSRR` → atomic bit set (lower 16 bits) / clear (upper 16 bits).

### ✅ Output Topologies
- [ ] **Push-Pull:** active drive both ways, sharp edges, no external resistor.
- [ ] **Open-Drain:** only sinks to GND, requires external pull-up; allows wired-AND bus sharing.
- [ ] **I²C bus** is open-drain; **LED drive** is typically push-pull (or current-sink NMOS).

### ✅ Input Modes
- [ ] **Floating:** undefined state — avoid for unconnected pins.
- [ ] **Pull-up:** default HIGH (button to GND — most common).
- [ ] **Pull-down:** default LOW (button to V_DD).
- [ ] **Schmitt trigger** provides ~0.1·V_DD hysteresis noise immunity.

### ✅ Critical Electrical Limits (LPC2148)
- [ ] $I_{pin,max} = 40$ mA
- [ ] $I_{port,max} = 100$ mA
- [ ] $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V (typical)
- [ ] $V_{IL,max} = 0.8$ V, $V_{IH,min} = 2.0$ V
- [ ] $V_{OL,max} = 0.4$ V @ 8 mA sink
- [ ] $V_{OH,min} = 2.4$ V @ 8 mA source

### ✅ Design Rules to Apply in Numericals
- [ ] LED series resistor: $R_S = (V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL}) / I_F$ — always use worst-case $V_{OL} = 0.4$ V.
- [ ] Open-drain rise time: $t_r \approx 2.2 \cdot R_{PU} \cdot C_{bus}$.
- [ ] Max toggle frequency: $f_{max} \approx 1 / (4 \cdot t_r)$ (conservative).
- [ ] 8 LEDs at 10 mA = 80 mA per port — within 100 mA limit. 9+ LEDs = violation.

### ✅ Programming Sequence (Always Follow)
1. Enable clock → 2. Clear PINSEL/MODER to GPIO → 3. Set IODIR/MODER direction → 4. Configure pull (if input) → 5. Use IOSET/IOCLR (LPC) or BSRR (STM32) for atomic writes.

### ✅ Examiner Trivia
- [ ] "What is bit-banding?" → Atomic single-bit access via memory alias at `0x42000000` for peripherals.
- [ ] "Why use open-drain for I²C?" → Allows multiple masters to share the bus without contention.
- [ ] "Why debounce?" → Mechanical switch contacts bounce 1–10 ms; without debounce, one press reads as many.
- [ ] "What happens if MODER is not cleared after reset?" → STM32 pins default to **analog** mode; the GPIO output does not function.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Memory in Embedded Systems — Formal Definition

In the context of **microcontrollers and embedded systems**, **Memory** refers to the organized array of storage cells (latches, capacitors, or floating-gate transistors) that retain binary data (instructions and operands) which the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes during real-time operation.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCPT302, Module 4)**, the memory subsystem is studied across two fundamental axes:

- **Volatility axis** → Volatile (loses data on power-off) vs. Non-Volatile (retains data without power)
- **Access-pattern axis** → Random Access Memory (RAM) vs. Sequential Access Memory (SAM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 unit on *Memory* specifically focuses on the **classification, internal architecture, and interfacing characteristics** of semiconductor memories used inside microcontrollers such as the 8051, ARM Cortex-M, and AVR families. Questions on memory-mapped I/O, Harvard vs. Von-Neumann mapping, and address decoding carry recurring high weightage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Warehouse" Intuition

Imagine a microcontroller as a **bustling factory floor** and memory as its **warehouse system**:

- **RAM (Random Access Memory)** is the **active workbench** at the center of the floor. The worker (CPU) can instantly grab *any* tool from *any* location in equal time (random access). However, the moment the factory shuts down (power-off), the workbench is cleared — this is **volatility**.
- **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** is the **permanent instruction manual** bolted to the wall. Even when the factory is dark, the manual remains. Workers can *read* it freely but cannot *write* to it during normal operation.
- **Cache memory** is the **worker's apron pocket** — a tiny, ultra-fast stash for the *currently used* tools. It is faster than the workbench but much smaller.
- **Flash/EEPROM** is the **locker room** — persistent, rewritable, but requires a special procedure (erase-program cycle) to change contents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Memory is *not* a monolithic block. Embedded engineers must carefully partition code, data, and stack into the right memory technology to balance **speed, density, cost, and non-volatility**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Access time (tₐ):** typically **60 ns – 100 ns** for legacy 8051 external memory; **10 ns – 20 ns** for modern SRAM.
- **Cell density:** DRAM ≈ **6 transistors → 1 transistor + 1 capacitor** per bit; SRAM needs **6 transistors per bit**.
- **Endurance of Flash:** **10,000 – 100,000 erase-program cycles** per sector.
- **Data retention of Flash:** typically **10 – 20 years** at room temperature.
- **Memory organization unit:** **1 Byte = 8 bits**, addressed linearly from `0x0000` upward in the address space.
- **Standard address size for 8051:** **16-bit → 64 KB** external code + 64 KB external data (Harvard split).
- **Standard address size for ARM Cortex-M:** **32-bit → 4 GB** unified address space (Von-Neumann variant).

## 1.4 Top-Level Classification of Semiconductor Memory

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Taxonomy (KTU Module 4 focus)**

| Major Class | Volatile? | Typical Use in MCUs |
|---|---|---|
| **SRAM** | Yes | Internal register file, cache, scratchpad |
| **DRAM** | Yes | External bulk data (rare in MCUs) |
| **Mask ROM** | No | Factory-programmed firmware |
| **PROM / EPROM** | No | One-time / UV-erasable program memory |
| **EEPROM** | No | Configuration storage, calibration |
| **Flash (NOR/NAND)** | No | In-system programmable firmware, data logging |
| **FRAM** | No | High-endurance parameter storage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Hierarchy in a Modern Microcontroller

Memory is arranged in a strict **pyramid hierarchy** that trades **speed**, **cost-per-bit**, and **capacity**:

1. **CPU Registers** — fastest (sub-ns), smallest (a few hundred bytes), inside the ALU.
2. **Level-1 Cache (L1)** — single-cycle access, KB-sized, integrated on-die.
3. **Internal SRAM** — multi-cycle, several KB to MB, on-chip.
4. **External SRAM / DRAM** — bus-dependent, MB-sized, off-chip.
5. **Internal Flash** — non-volatile, tens to hundreds of KB, on-chip.
6. **External Flash / Storage** — GB-sized, slow, off-chip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a hierarchy?** A single flat memory is impossible to build: *fast memory is expensive per bit, dense memory is slow*. The hierarchy exploits **locality of reference** (temporal + spatial) to make the *average* access time close to the fastest level.

## 2.2 SRAM — Static Random Access Memory

- Built from **6 CMOS transistors per bit** (two cross-coupled inverters + 2 access transistors).
- Data is held as long as power is supplied — **no refresh required**.
- **Read operation:** precharge bit-lines → activate word-line → sense amplifier reads differential voltage.
- **Write operation:** drive bit-lines to the desired value → activate word-line → flip the cell.

**Advantages:** fastest volatile memory, low power in standby, deterministic timing.
**Disadvantages:** low density (6T per bit), high cost per bit, large cell area.

## 2.3 DRAM — Dynamic Random Access Memory

- Built from **1 access transistor + 1 storage capacitor** per bit.
- The capacitor leaks charge → **must be refreshed every ~64 ms**.
- Multiplexed address bus: row + column are sent sequentially using **RAS** and **CAS** strobes.

**Advantages:** highest density, lowest cost per bit.
**Disadvantages:** needs refresh circuitry, destructive read (sense amplifier destroys the charge — must rewrite), higher latency.

## 2.4 ROM Family

| ROM Type | Programming | Erasing | Reusability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mask ROM** | At fabrication (photolithographic masks) | Impossible | One-time |
| **PROM** | User-programmable via fuse-blowing | Impossible | One-time |
| **EPROM** | User-programmable (hot-electron injection) | UV light (~20 min) | Limited cycles |
| **EEPROM** | Electrically byte-programmable | Electrically byte-erasable | ~10⁶ cycles |
| **Flash (NOR)** | Electrically block-erasable | Electrically block-erasable | ~10⁵ cycles |

## 2.5 Flash Memory Internals (Floating-Gate MOSFET)

A flash cell is a **MOSFET with an extra floating gate** buried in the oxide:

- **Programming (writing 0):** *Hot-electron injection* or *Fowler-Nordheim tunneling* pushes electrons onto the floating gate → raises threshold voltage → cell reads as 0.
- **Erasing:** A high negative voltage on the control gate pulls electrons *off* the floating gate → lowers threshold voltage → cell reads as 1.
- **Reading:** Apply a small Vgs between the 1 and 0 thresholds; the sense amp decides based on drain current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOR Flash vs. NAND Flash** — KTU high-yield distinction:
> - **NOR:** random byte-access, used for *code execution* (execute-in-place, XIP).
> - **NAND:** block-access only, used for *mass data storage* (SD cards, SSDs).

## 2.6 Memory Organization & Address Decoding

A memory chip of capacity $N \times M$ has:
- $n$ address lines, where $N = 2^n$
- $M$ data lines (M-bit-wide word)

For an 8051 system with **16-bit external address bus**, the maximum directly addressable space is:

$$
\text{Max Addressable Space} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

For an ARM Cortex-M with **32-bit address bus**:

$$
\text{Max Addressable Space} = 2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}
$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addressable locations | $2^n$ where $n$ = address lines | locations | For byte-organized memory |
| Total memory size | $2^n \times w$ where $w$ = word width | bits | Multiply by 8 to get bytes |
| Memory capacity decode | $\overline{\text{CS}} = \overline{A_{n-1} \cdot A_{n-2} \cdots A_{k}}$ | Boolean | Decoding using upper address lines |
| Refresh interval (DRAM) | typically $t_{\text{REF}} \le 64$ | ms | Industry standard |
| Effective access time (2-level cache) | $T_{\text{eff}} = h_1 T_1 + (1-h_1)(h_2 T_2 + (1-h_2) T_m)$ | seconds | $h_1, h_2$ = hit ratios |
| Average access time with hierarchy | $T_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot t_i$ | seconds | $p_i$ = probability of access at level $i$ |
| Endurance limit | $E = N_{\text{cycles}} \times N_{\text{sectors}}$ | cycles | Total writes over device lifetime |
| 8051 external code space | $2^{16} = 65536$ | bytes | 64 KB |
| 8051 internal data RAM | 128 | bytes | 00H–7FH |
| 8051 SFR space | 128 | bytes | 80H–FFH (same physical address range, different access via `MOV` @R0/@R1 vs direct) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Memory selection directly impacts the **bill of materials (BOM) cost, boot time, deterministic response, and field upgradability** of any embedded product. An automotive ECU might pair **Flash** for firmware, **EEPROM** for odometer/calibration, and **battery-backed SRAM** for crash data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Addressable Memory from Address Lines

**Problem context:** A microcontroller has $n$ parallel address pins. Derive the maximum number of distinct memory locations it can address if the memory is **byte-organized**.

**Step 1:** Each address pin can be driven to one of two stable logic levels, **0** or **1**.

$$
\text{Number of combinations of } n \text{ pins} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots (n \text{ times}) = 2^n
$$

**Step 2:** Each unique combination corresponds to exactly **one addressable byte** in a byte-organized memory.

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{locations}} = 2^n}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to KB, MB, GB as required.

$$
\text{Capacity (bytes)} = 2^n, \quad \text{Capacity (KB)} = \frac{2^n}{1024}, \quad \text{Capacity (MB)} = \frac{2^n}{2^{20}}
$$

### Worked Example — 8051 (16-bit address)

Given: $n = 16$ address lines.

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}
$$

Convert to KB:

$$
\frac{2^{16}}{2^{10}} = 2^{6} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

### Worked Example — ARM Cortex-M4 (32-bit address)

Given: $n = 32$ address lines.

$$
N_{\text{locations}} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Access Time with 2-Level Memory Hierarchy

**Problem context:** A system has L1 cache with hit ratio $h_1$ and access time $t_1$, plus main memory with access time $t_m$. Compute the effective (average) access time $t_{\text{eff}}$.

**Step 1:** On each memory reference, the CPU looks up L1 first.

- Probability of L1 hit = $h_1$
- Probability of L1 miss = $(1 - h_1)$

**Step 2:** On L1 hit, time consumed = $t_1$.

**Step 3:** On L1 miss, the CPU pays $t_1$ *plus* $t_m$ (must access main memory).

**Step 4:** The total effective access time is the probability-weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= h_1 \cdot t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot (t_1 + t_m) \\
&= h_1 \cdot t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot t_m \\
&= t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot t_m
\end{aligned}
$$

This can be written compactly as:

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{eff}} = t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot t_m}
$$

### Worked Example — Embedded MCU with L1 cache

Given: $t_1 = 1$ ns, $t_m = 50$ ns, $h_1 = 0.95$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= 1 + (1 - 0.95) \times 50 \\
&= 1 + 0.05 \times 50 \\
&= 1 + 2.5 \\
&= 3.5 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $h_1$ drops to $0.80$ (cold cache after a context switch):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= 1 + (1 - 0.80) \times 50 \\
&= 1 + 0.20 \times 50 \\
&= 1 + 10 \\
&= 11 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** A 15% drop in hit ratio produces a 3× increase in effective access time — justifying the KTU emphasis on cache-aware programming.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: 3-Level Effective Access Time (L1, L2, Main)

**Step 1:** Let hit ratios be $h_1$ (L1) and $h_2$ (L2, given L1 miss).

**Step 2:** $t_{\text{eff}}$ equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= h_1 \cdot t_1 + (1 - h_1)\bigl[h_2 \cdot (t_1 + t_2) + (1 - h_2) \cdot (t_1 + t_m)\bigr] \\
&= t_1 + (1 - h_1)\bigl[h_2 \cdot t_2 + (1 - h_2) \cdot t_m\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{eff}} = t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot h_2 \cdot t_2 + (1 - h_1)(1 - h_2) \cdot t_m}
$$

### Worked Example — Cortex-M7 with L1 + L2

Given: $t_1 = 1$ ns, $t_2 = 5$ ns, $t_m = 50$ ns, $h_1 = 0.90$, $h_2 = 0.85$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= 1 + (1 - 0.90)(0.85)(5) + (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85)(50) \\
&= 1 + (0.10)(0.85)(5) + (0.10)(0.15)(50) \\
&= 1 + 0.425 + 0.75 \\
&= 2.175 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Address Decoding for Memory-Mapped Chip Select

**Problem context:** Design the chip-select (CS) logic for a **4 KB SRAM** mapped to the address range `0x2000`–`0x2FFF` in an 8051-style 16-bit address system.

**Step 1:** Express the address range in binary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x2000 &= 0010\,0000\,0000\,0000 \\
0x2FFF &= 0010\,1111\,1111\,1111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the **fixed (don't-care) bits** vs. **decoded (variable) bits**.

- Bit 15 = 0, Bit 14 = 0, Bit 13 = 1, Bit 12 = 0 → these four bits are *constant* across the range.
- Bits 11–0 vary → these are the **internal 4 KB** address space ($2^{12} = 4096$ bytes).

**Step 3:** Form the chip-select Boolean expression. The chip is *selected* only when $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12} = 0010$.

$$
\overline{\text{CS}} = \overline{\,\overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot A_{13} \cdot \overline{A_{12}}\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify with the boundary address 0x2FFF.

- $A_{15}=0, A_{14}=0, A_{13}=1, A_{12}=0$ → satisfies → CS = 0 → chip enabled. ✓

The remaining 12 lines ($A_{11}$ to $A_0$) feed the SRAM's internal address pins.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Memory for an Embedded Project

| Design Constraint | Recommended Memory | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Boot firmware, rarely updated | **Mask ROM** or **internal Flash** | Non-volatile, no field-upgrade needed |
| Field-upgradable firmware | **NOR Flash** (execute-in-place) | Byte-random-read, in-system reprogrammable |
| Calibration / config data (< 1 KB) | **EEPROM** | Byte-level erase-write, no sector management |
| High-endurance data logging | **FRAM** | 10¹⁰ cycles, no write-delay |
| Scratchpad / stack / heap | **SRAM** | Fast, no refresh overhead |
| Large frame buffer / mass storage | **DRAM / NAND Flash** | High density, low cost per bit |
| Real-time control loop (deterministic) | **SRAM + Locked Cache** | No refresh jitter, predictable latency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Subsystem Architecture in a Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU CORE"] --> REG["REGISTER FILE"]
    REG --> CACHE1["LEVEL 1 CACHE"]
    CACHE1 --> SRAM["INTERNAL SRAM"]
    SRAM --> MMU["ADDRESS DECODER AND BUS MATRIX"]
    MMU --> EXTROM["EXTERNAL ROM OR FLASH"]
    MMU --> EXTRAM["EXTERNAL SRAM OR DRAM"]
    MMU --> IO["MEMORY MAPPED PERIPHERALS"]
    FLASH["INTERNAL FLASH"] --> MMU
    EEPROM["EEPROM CONFIG BLOCK"] --> MMU
    BUSARB["BUS ARBITER"] --> MMU
    REFRESH["DRAM REFRESH CONTROLLER"] --> EXTRAM
    BUSARB --- CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram is a **functional architecture flow** showing how the CPU's request propagates through the memory hierarchy, down to the bus arbiter and refresh controller. It is *not* a literal pin-out but a clean functional map suitable for KTU descriptive answers.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Memory Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MEM["SEMICONDUCTOR MEMORY"] --> VOL["VOLATILE"]
    MEM --> NVOL["NON VOLATILE"]
    VOL --> SRAM1["SRAM"]
    VOL --> DRAM1["DRAM"]
    SRAM1 --> S6T["6T CELL"]
    SRAM1 --> S4T["4T CELL"]
    DRAM1 --> SDRAM["SYNCHRONOUS DRAM"]
    DRAM1 --> DDR["DDR VARIANTS"]
    NVOL --> ROM["ROM"]
    NVOL --> FLASHN["FLASH"]
    ROM --> MASKROM["MASK ROM"]
    ROM --> EPROM1["EPROM"]
    ROM --> EEPROM1["EEPROM"]
    FLASHN --> NORF["NOR FLASH XIP"]
    FLASHN --> NANDF["NAND FLASH STORAGE"]
    NORF --> EMBF["EMBEDDED FIRMWARE"]
    EEPROM1 --> CFG["CONFIG AND CALIBRATION"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Address Decoding Flow for 8051 External Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A15["A15"] --> AND["AND GATE INPUTS"]
    A14["A14"] --> AND
    A13["A13"] --> AND
    A12["A12"] --> AND
    AND --> NOT["INVERTER"]
    NOT --> CS1["CS PIN OF CHIP A 0x2000 TO 0x2FFF"]
    A11["A11 TO A0"] --> INTA["INTERNAL ADDRESS PINS OF SRAM"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Flash Cell Programming & Erase Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IDLE["IDLE CELL THRESHOLD V MID"] --> PROG["PROGRAMMING APPLY HIGH V ON DRAIN AND GATE"]
    PROG --> INJ["HOT ELECTRON INJECTION ELECTRONS TRAPPED ON FLOATING GATE"]
    INJ --> VHI["THRESHOLD RISES TO V HIGH CELL READS AS ZERO"]
    VHI --> ERS["ERASING REVERSE POLARITY TUNNELING"]
    ERS --> RMV["ELECTRONS PULLED OFF FLOATING GATE"]
    RMV --> VLO["THRESHOLD FALLS TO V LOW CELL READS AS ONE"]
    VLO --> IDLE
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — Effective Access-Time Computation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["CPU ISSUES MEMORY REFERENCE"] --> L1CHK["L1 CACHE LOOKUP"]
    L1CHK -->|"HIT h1"| RET1["RETURN DATA IN TIME T1"]
    L1CHK -->|"MISS 1 MINUS h1"| L2CHK["L2 CACHE LOOKUP"]
    L2CHK -->|"HIT h2"| RET2["RETURN DATA IN TIME T1 PLUS T2"]
    L2CHK -->|"MISS"| MM["ACCESS MAIN MEMORY TIME T1 PLUS TM"]
    MM --> RETM["RETURN DATA"]
    RET1 --> AVG["COMPUTE T EFF"]
    RET2 --> AVG
    RETM --> AVG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Data retention on power-off | **Lost** | **Retained** |
| Access speed | Faster (no erase) | Slower (erase-before-write) |
| Typical cell structure | SRAM/DRAM | Floating-gate transistor |
| Example | **SRAM** in 8051 internal data RAM | **Flash** for program storage |
| Use in MCUs | Scratchpad, stack, variables | Firmware, configuration |

*(1 mark – definition of each, 1 mark – example, 1 mark – tabulated contrast)*

### Q2. What is the role of the chip-select (CS) pin in memory interfacing? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CS (Chip Select)** pin — sometimes called CE (Chip Enable) — activates a specific memory chip on a shared bus. When CS = 0 (active low), the chip responds to read/write strobes; when CS = 1, the chip is in standby and its data lines are tri-stated (high-Z).

1. **Enables bus-sharing** — multiple chips can share the same data/address lines; only the selected one drives the bus. *(1 mark)*
2. **Forms the address-decoding boundary** — decoding the upper address bits feeds the CS line. *(1 mark)*
3. **Reduces power consumption** — unselected chips draw only standby current. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry **7 + 7 = 14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) and (b).

### Question A (14 Marks) — Memory Hierarchy & Effective Access Time `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **memory hierarchy** in a typical microcontroller-based system. Why is a hierarchy necessary? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A system has L1 cache with access time **2 ns** and main memory with access time **80 ns**. The L1 hit ratio is **92 %**. Compute the effective access time. If the hit ratio drops to **75 %** during a DMA burst, what is the new effective access time? Comment on the impact. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Memory hierarchy explanation (7 marks):**

The memory hierarchy in a microcontroller is arranged as a pyramid with the **fastest, smallest, costliest** memory at the top and the **slowest, largest, cheapest** at the bottom.

- **Level 1 – CPU Registers:** few hundred bytes, sub-ns access, inside the ALU.
- **Level 2 – Cache (L1/L2):** tens of KB, single-cycle.
- **Level 3 – Internal SRAM:** several KB to MB, on-chip.
- **Level 4 – Internal Flash:** tens to hundreds of KB, non-volatile, on-chip.
- **Level 5 – External Memory:** MB-to-GB, off-chip, connected via parallel bus.

**Why a hierarchy is necessary (3 marks):**

1. **Cost vs. speed trade-off** — fast memory is expensive *per bit*; dense memory is slow. A hierarchy balances both. *(1 mark)*
2. **Locality of reference** — programs exhibit *temporal* (re-using recent data) and *spatial* (accessing nearby addresses) locality, so caching recent items in fast memory makes the average access time nearly equal to the fastest level. *(1 mark)*
3. **Scalability** — the hierarchy lets the designer independently scale capacity (using dense Flash/DRAM) and performance (using fast SRAM/cache). *(1 mark)*

*(Block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid flow; 4 marks for the diagram and 3 marks for the "why" reasoning.)*

**(b) Effective access time calculation (7 marks):**

**Case 1 — Normal operation, h₁ = 0.92**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$
t_{\text{eff}} = t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot t_m
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= 2 + (1 - 0.92) \times 80 \\
&= 2 + 0.08 \times 80 \\
&= 2 + 6.4 \\
&= 8.4 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for setup, 2 marks for substitution and result.)*

**Case 2 — DMA burst, h₁ = 0.75**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= 2 + (1 - 0.75) \times 80 \\
&= 2 + 0.25 \times 80 \\
&= 2 + 20 \\
&= 22 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for substitution and result.)*

**Comment (1 mark):** A drop of 17 % in hit ratio causes the effective access time to rise by a factor of ≈ **2.6×** (from 8.4 ns to 22 ns). This proves that cache locality has a *non-linear* impact on real-time performance — a critical concern in DMA-heavy embedded applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Memory Classification & Address Decoding `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Classify semiconductor memory in detail. Compare **SRAM, DRAM, EEPROM, and NOR Flash** with respect to cell structure, volatility, write mechanism, and typical application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design the address-decoding logic for a **16 KB SRAM** mapped to the address range `0x4000`–`0x7FFF` in a system with a 16-bit address bus. Show the chip-select Boolean expression and verify with a sample address. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Memory classification (7 marks):**

**Top-level classification (2 marks):**

1. **Volatile** — SRAM, DRAM
2. **Non-volatile** — Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash, FRAM

**Comparison table (5 marks):**

| Feature | SRAM | DRAM | EEPROM | NOR Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cell | 6 transistors | 1T + 1 capacitor | 1 floating-gate transistor | 1 floating-gate MOSFET |
| Volatile? | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Write mechanism | Direct drive | Word-line activate + cap. charge | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | Hot-electron injection |
| Erase mechanism | N/A (electrical overwrite) | N/A | Electrical byte-erase | Electrical sector/block erase |
| Refresh | Not required | Required every ~64 ms | Not required | Not required |
| Density | Low | Highest | Low | Medium-high |
| Speed | Fastest volatile | Slower than SRAM | Slow (ms) byte-write | Fast read, slow write |
| Application | Cache, register file, scratchpad | Main bulk memory | Calibration, config | Firmware, code execution (XIP) |

*(½ mark per cell of the table — 8 key entries × ½ = 4 marks; 1 mark for the brief introductory classification, 1 mark for the closing "typical application" remark aligned to embedded use.)*

**(b) Address decoding (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify range in binary (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x4000 &= 0100\,0000\,0000\,0000 \\
0x7FFF &= 0111\,1111\,1111\,1111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify fixed and variable bits (1 mark):**

- $A_{15} = 0$ (fixed)
- $A_{14} = 1$ (fixed)
- $A_{13} = 0$ (fixed) **for 0x4000**, but $A_{13}$ *varies* between 0x4000 and 0x7FFF. Hence the fixed prefix is **only $A_{15}A_{14} = 01$**.

Wait — verify: 0x4000 = 0100…, 0x7FFF = 0111…. Bits 15 and 14 stay as `01`; bit 13 varies from 0 to 1. So:

- **Fixed:** $A_{15} = 0, A_{14} = 1$ → 2 fixed bits → gives $2^{14} = 16{,}384 \approx 16$ KB. ✓

**Step 3 — Chip-select Boolean expression (2 marks):**

The chip must be selected when $A_{15}=0$ **and** $A_{14}=1$. All other 14 address lines ($A_{13}$ to $A_0$) feed the SRAM's internal address pins.

$$
\overline{\text{CS}} = \overline{\,\overline{A_{15}} \cdot A_{14}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with 0x6000 (sample) (2 marks):**

$$
0x6000 = 0110\,0000\,0000\,0000
$$

- $A_{15} = 0, A_{14} = 1$ → satisfies → $\overline{\text{CS}} = 0$ → chip enabled. ✓

**Internal addressing (0 marks, but expected to mention):** The lower 14 bits $A_{13}$–$A_0$ provide $2^{14} = 16{,}384$ unique internal locations, covering the full 16 KB capacity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Memory Questions — 2024 KTU Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "volatile" with "primary" memory.** Many students write "RAM = primary" but the correct embedded-system framing is **"RAM = volatile, working memory"**. Examiners *do* deduct for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the "CS active-low" convention.** When the Boolean expression is written as $\text{CS} = A_{15} \cdot A_{14}$ (without the overbar), the chip would *never* be selected because CS is conventionally **active-low**. Always write $\overline{\text{CS}}$ or "CS-bar" explicitly. *(~2 marks lost frequently)*
> 3. **Omitting the "why" behind the hierarchy.** A diagram without an explanation of *why* a hierarchy exists gets only 3 of the 7 marks.
> 4. **Wrong unit in the effective-access-time result.** The unit must be the same as the inputs (ns or µs). Mixing up units loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Using NOR vs. NAND Flash interchangeably.** They are *not* equivalent: NOR is for code execution (XIP), NAND is for bulk data. Examiners *will* catch this.
> 6. **Mis-stating the 8051 internal data memory as "64 KB".** Internal data RAM is **128 bytes (00H–7FH)**; the **64 KB** refers to the *external* code space. A common slip that costs full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Memory classification** = Volatile (SRAM, DRAM) vs. Non-Volatile (ROM family, Flash, FRAM).
- [x] **SRAM = 6T cell, no refresh, fast, low density.** **DRAM = 1T + 1 capacitor, refresh every ~64 ms, destructive read.**
- [x] **ROM hierarchy** → Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM (UV) → EEPROM (electrical byte) → Flash (electrical block).
- [x] **Flash cell = floating-gate MOSFET.** Programming = electron injection (threshold rises, reads 0). Erasing = tunneling (threshold falls, reads 1).
- [x] **NOR Flash = XIP (execute-in-place).** **NAND Flash = bulk storage, block access only.**
- [x] **Addressable memory** = $2^n$ bytes for an $n$-bit address bus. **8051 → 64 KB external; ARM Cortex-M → 4 GB.**
- [x] **CS pin = active-low**, enables bus-sharing and reduces power.
- [x] **Address-decoding Boolean expression** uses the *fixed* upper bits; the *variable* lower bits feed the internal address pins.
- [x] **Effective access time (2-level):** $t_{\text{eff}} = t_1 + (1 - h_1) t_m$.
- [x] **Effective access time (3-level):** $t_{\text{eff}} = t_1 + (1 - h_1) h_2 t_2 + (1 - h_1)(1 - h_2) t_m$.
- [x] **Cache locality** = temporal + spatial — a non-linear effect on average access time.
- [x] **8051 internal data RAM = 128 bytes** (00H–7FH). **SFRs = 128 bytes** (80H–FFH, accessed by direct addressing).
- [x] **Endurance:** EEPROM ~10⁶ cycles; NOR Flash ~10⁵ cycles; FRAM ~10¹⁰ cycles.
- [x] **Data retention:** Flash ~10–20 years; FRAM ~10 years; battery-backed SRAM ~5 years.
- [x] **Embedded rule of thumb:** Pick the *minimum* memory technology that meets speed, density, endurance, and cost targets — do not over-engineer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on memory, **always open with a labeled block diagram** (4 marks), **follow with the formula derivation** (3 marks), and **close with a comparison table** (3 marks) — this structure covers all KTU valuation checkpoints automatically. Do *not* leave a sub-part as a one-liner; every 7-mark sub-part needs at least **5–6 lines of dense, structured explanation** plus a small numerical example wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programming Interface (PI)** in the context of embedded systems refers to the **hardware and software mechanism** used to transfer the compiled machine code (firmware/hex file) from a host computer into the non-volatile program memory (ROM/Flash/EPROM) of a target microcontroller unit (MCU). It is a standardized set of pins, protocols, and electrical signaling rules that govern how external programming devices (programmers/debuggers) communicate with the MCU to write, verify, read, and protect its on-chip code memory.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCPT302), the *Programming Interface* topic specifically covers the **in-system programming methodologies** for common 8-bit and 32-bit microcontrollers (notably the **Intel 8051 family** and the **ARM Cortex-M architecture**), encompassing parallel programming, serial programming, ISP (In-System Programming), IAP (In-Application Programming), JTAG, and SWD (Serial Wire Debug).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Multilingual Receptionist:**
> Imagine your microcontroller as a highly intelligent but "mute" employee who only understands binary (0s and 1s) written in machine language. Your laptop, on the other hand, "speaks" high-level English (C code) and stores data in Windows files. The **Programming Interface acts as a multilingual receptionist** sitting at the front desk of the MCU. It receives a sealed envelope (the .hex file) from the laptop, translates it into a stream of electrical pulses (voltage transitions on specific pins), and personally writes the contents into the employee's notebook (Flash memory) one page at a time — all *while the employee is still seated at his desk doing his job* (in-circuit).

In simple terms:
- The **laptop** is the *programmer host*.
- The **.hex file** is the *message*.
- The **programming pins** (e.g., MOSI, MISO, SCK, RESET) are the *telephone wires*.
- The **MCU's on-chip bootloader** is the *receptionist* who accepts and files the message.

## 1.3 Why Programming Interfaces Are Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight:**
