> Microcontrollers are **blank** at the factory — they contain no program. Every instruction the CPU executes must first be physically burned into non-volatile memory. Without a programming interface, the chip is an inert piece of silicon. The interface defines **how** that program is delivered, **how fast**, **with what error protection**, and **whether the chip must be removed from the circuit** to be programmed.

## 1.4 Standard Boundary Conditions & Metrics (Bold-Faced)

- **Programming Voltage (V_PP):** Typically **12.0 V** for older parallel EPROM-based 8051s; modern Flash-based MCUs use the **supply voltage V_DD = 3.3 V or 5.0 V** for programming.
- **Clock Frequency during Programming:** ISP for AVR uses **f_SCK ≤ f_OSC / 4**; ARM SWD operates at **up to 50 MHz**.
- **Memory Endurance:** Standard Flash programming cycles = **10,000 to 100,000 write/erase cycles**.
- **Data Retention:** Non-volatile memory retains code for **≥ 20 years** at 85 °C.
- **JTAG Chain Length:** Standard IEEE 1149.1 supports **up to 4 devices** in a single daisy-chain (TDI → TDO).

## 1.5 Classification of Programming Interfaces

| Class | Method | Chip Removal Required? | Speed | Modern Usage |
|-------|--------|------------------------|-------|--------------|
| **Parallel Programming** | High-voltage byte-parallel (e.g., 8751) | Yes | Very Fast | Legacy/Obsolete |
| **Serial Programming (UART)** | Bootloader over UART (TX/RX) | No | Moderate | Common in ARM/Cortex |
| **SPI-based ISP** | SPI protocol over MOSI/MISO/SCK | No | Fast | AVR, PIC, ESP32 |
| **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** | 4-wire Test Access Port (TAP) | No | Fast | ARM Debug, Boundary Scan |
| **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** | 2-wire ARM debug protocol | No | Very Fast | ARM Cortex-M (STM32, LPC) |
| **USB DFU** | Device Firmware Upgrade over USB | No | Very Fast | Smartphones, IoT devices |
| **CAN / I²C Bootloader** | Automotive/industrial busses | No | Slow | Automotive ECUs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Programming Interface Works

The programming operation, regardless of the specific interface, follows a **universal 5-step handshake protocol**:

1. **Enter Programming Mode** — A specific voltage pattern or sequence is applied to dedicated pins (e.g., pulling **PSEN, ALE, RST** low/high on 8051, or asserting **BOOT0 = HIGH** on STM32) to redirect the CPU's reset vector to an on-chip ROM bootloader.
2. **Erase the Flash** — The host sends an *Erase All* or *Erase Page* command; the MCU performs a high-voltage Fowler-Nordheim tunneling operation to clear floating-gate cells.
3. **Transfer Data Blocks** — The .hex file (Intel HEX or Motorola S-record) is broken into small records. Each record contains: `Start Code : Byte Count : Address : Record Type : Data : Checksum`. The data is sent serially or in parallel.
4. **Program & Verify** — For every byte received, the MCU writes it to Flash, then re-reads it and compares against the received value. A mismatch triggers a programming error flag.
5. **Exit Programming Mode** — The reset vector is restored, and the MCU begins executing user code from address `0x00000000` (Cortex-M) or `0x0000` (8051).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:**
> - The **erase-before-write** rule exists because Flash memory cells can only transition from `1 → 0` (program) and require a separate **erase operation** to return to `1`.
> - The **checksum verification** is non-negotiable in automotive (ISO 26262) and medical (IEC 62304) firmware delivery.
> - The **boot mode pin** is a physical security feature — without it, random noise on the reset line could trigger a re-flash.

## 2.2 Detailed Interface Walkthroughs

### 2.2.1 Parallel Programming (8051 — Legacy 8751 EPROM)

The Intel 8751 used a **parallel programming mode** with the following pin assignments:

- **P0.0–P0.7** → Address low byte (A0–A7)
- **P2.0–P2.4** → Address high byte (A8–A12)
- **P1.0–P1.7** → Data bus (D0–D7) — bidirectional
- **P3.3 (PROG)** → Programming pulse input (active low)
- **P3.6 (WR)** → Write strobe
- **P3.7 (RD)** → Read strobe (for verification)
- **EA/V_PP** → Programming voltage (**+12.0 V ± 0.5 V**)
- **RST** → Held HIGH during programming
- **ALE, PSEN** → Held LOW to isolate CPU

The sequence:
1. Apply V_DD = **5.0 V**, V_PP = **12.0 V**, RST = HIGH, ALE = PSEN = LOW.
2. Set address on P0/P2.
3. Place data byte on P1.
4. Pulse PROG (P3.3) LOW for **50 ms** to program one byte.
5. Pulse RD LOW to read back and verify.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Trap:** Students often forget that V_PP on the 8751 is **+12 V**, not +5 V. Applying only V_DD will result in a *programming failed* error, not chip damage (the V_PP pin is high-voltage tolerant but signal-level logic won't write the EPROM cell).

### 2.2.2 In-System Programming (ISP) — SPI Mode for AVR/8051 Derivatives

Modern 8051 derivatives (e.g., **Atmel AT89S52, Nuvoton N78E517A**) and AVR microcontrollers expose an **SPI-based ISP** using only **4 pins**:

- **MOSI (Master Out, Slave In)** — Host → MCU data
- **MISO (Master In, Slave Out)** — MCU → Host verification data
- **SCK (Serial Clock)** — Host-driven clock
- **RST** — Held LOW to enter ISP mode (on AVR) or used to signal programming start

The host computer runs a programmer (e.g., *avrdude*, *Atmel Studio*, *Keil ULINK*) which drives a USB-to-SPI bridge (e.g., **USBasp, AVRISP mkII, CH340**). Data is clocked into the MCU's on-chip SPI slave, which writes it to Flash.

**Key Parameter — SPI Clock for Programming:**

$$
f_{SCK}^{ISP} \le \frac{f_{OSC}}{4}
$$

where $f_{OSC}$ is the on-chip oscillator frequency. For a 16 MHz AVR, the max ISP clock is **4 MHz**.

### 2.2.3 UART Bootloader (ARM Cortex-M — e.g., STM32)

The STM32 and most ARM Cortex-M3/M4 parts ship with a **factory bootloader** in System Memory (ROM) that accepts firmware over **USART1, USART2, USB, CAN, or I²C**. The selection is made by the **BOOT0** and **BOOT1** pins at reset:

| BOOT0 | BOOT1 | Boot Source | Address |
|-------|-------|-------------|---------|
| 0 | x | Main Flash (User Program) | 0x0800 0000 |
| 1 | 0 | System Memory (Bootloader) | 0x1FFF 0000 |
| 1 | 1 | Embedded SRAM (Debug) | 0x2000 0000 |

To program via UART:
1. Set **BOOT0 = HIGH, BOOT1 = LOW** (e.g., via a switch or jumper).
2. Pulse RESET.
3. The on-chip bootloader runs and listens on **USART1 at 115200 baud, 8E1**.
4. Host PC sends the **0x7F** "synchronization" byte; MCU replies with **0x79 ACK**.
5. Host sends **0x00** to request *GET-VERSION* and *READ-PROTECTION* commands.
6. Host issues **0x31 (ERASE)** with checksum, then **0x11 (WRITE)** commands for each 256-byte block.
7. Each block write is followed by a 0x79 ACK on success.
8. After all blocks, host sends **0x21 (GO)**; MCU jumps to address 0x0800 0000.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bootloaders Use a Separate ROM:** The System Memory bootloader is **read-only** and **immutable** (masked in silicon at the fab). This guarantees that *some* method to reprogram the chip always exists, even if the user Flash is corrupted — a foundational concept in **field firmware updates (OTA / FOTA)**.

### 2.2.4 JTAG Interface (IEEE 1149.1)

JTAG was originally designed for **boundary-scan testing** of PCBs but is now ubiquitous for **debug + programming** of ARM, DSP, and FPGA devices. It uses **4 mandatory pins** plus an optional TRST:

- **TDI (Test Data In)** — Serial data into the device
- **TDO (Test Data Out)** — Serial data out
- **TCK (Test Clock)** — Independent of system clock
- **TMS (Test Mode Select)** — State machine control
- **TRST (Test Reset)** — Optional, asynchronous TAP reset

Internally, JTAG uses a **16-state finite state machine** called the **TAP controller**. The state machine is navigated by TMS sampled on each rising edge of TCK. Key states:

- **Test-Logic-Reset** → all test logic disabled
- **Run-Test/Idle** → idle state between operations
- **Shift-DR** → shift data through the selected data register
- **Shift-IR** → shift a 4-bit instruction into the Instruction Register
- **Capture-DR / Update-DR** → boundary latches

**Common JTAG Instructions:**

| Instruction (Binary) | Name | Purpose |
|----------------------|------|---------|
| `0000` | EXTEST | Test pins off-chip |
| `0001` | SAMPLE/PRELOAD | Snapshot pin states |
| `0010` | IDCODE | Read 32-bit device ID |
| `0100` | BYPASS | 1-bit register for daisy-chain |
| `1110` | PROGRAM / ERASE | Flash control (vendor-specific) |

**Daisy-Chain Equation** — for *N* devices on one JTAG bus, the total TDO-to-TDI scan chain length $L_{total}$ is:

$$
L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i
$$

where $L_i$ is the instruction register length of the $i^{th}$ device (typically 4 bits).

### 2.2.5 Serial Wire Debug (SWD) — ARM's 2-Wire Successor to JTAG

ARM introduced **SWD** as a leaner alternative to JTAG, using only **2 pins + VCC + GND**:

- **SWDIO (Data I/O)** — Bidirectional
- **SWCLK** — Host clock (up to 50 MHz)
- Optional: **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** — Trace data stream

**Line-turn-around protocol** — SWDIO changes direction synchronously to SWCLK. The host drives for one cycle, releases for one cycle (line turnaround), then the target drives for the next cycle. This avoids the need for a separate MISO/MOSI pair.

**Packet Structure:** Every SWD transaction is a 3-phase, 8-bit or 32-bit packet:

$$
\text{SWD Packet} = \{\text{Request (8 bits)} \} \rightarrow \{\text{ACK (3 bits)} \} \rightarrow \{\text{Data (32 bits)} \}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is more architectural than computational, the "formulas" are mostly **signal relationships, timing parameters, and protocol constants**. Memorize the following table for ESE and Module tests.

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbol | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|----------------------|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $f_{SCK}^{ISP} \le f_{OSC} / 4$ | SPI ISP clock | Max SPI clock for ISP | e.g., 4 MHz at 16 MHz osc |
| 2 | $L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$ | JTAG chain length | Sum of IR lengths | e.g., 16 bits for 4 devices |
| 3 | $T_{PROG}^{byte} = 50$ ms | Parallel byte program time | Per byte on 8751 | 50 ms |
| 4 | $V_{PP}$ | Programming voltage | High voltage for EPROM | **12.0 V ± 0.5 V** |
| 5 | $N_{cycles} = 10{,}000$ to $100{,}000$ | Flash endurance | Write/erase cycles | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{retention} \ge 20$ years | Data retention | Non-volatile memory | years @ 85 °C |
| 7 | Baud $ = 1 / T_{bit}$ | UART baud rate | Inverse of bit time | e.g., 115200 bps |
| 8 | $T_{erase}^{page} \approx 20$ to $40$ ms | Page erase time | Flash page erase | ms |
| 9 | $T_{write}^{page} \approx 1$ to $5$ ms | Page write time | Flash page program | ms |
| 10 | $f_{SWCLK} \le 50$ MHz | SWD clock limit | ARM SWD max | MHz |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU Production** — Every car ECU is programmed via **CAN bootloader** at the end of the assembly line. A single JTAG/SWD pinout would not scale to millions of units.
- **IoT OTA Updates** — ESP32, STM32, and Nordic nRF52 devices use **Wi-Fi/BLE bootloaders** so firmware can be updated *over-the-air* without physical access — critical for deployed sensors.
- **PCB Boundary-Scan Test (BST)** — JTAG's original purpose is still used in **ICT (In-Circuit Test)** fixtures for BGA-packaged MCUs where physical probing is impossible.
- **Secure Boot / TrustZone** — Modern programming interfaces include **cryptographic signatures** (AES, ECDSA) so only authenticated firmware can be flashed — protecting against IP theft and malware injection.
- **Field Engineers** — ISP via SPI or UART allows **in-field firmware upgrades** without desoldering the chip, reducing maintenance cost by orders of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Example 1 — Programming Time for an Entire Flash Image

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A microcontroller has a Flash memory of size **$M = 32$ KB**. The programmer writes data in **256-byte pages**, and each page requires:
> - $T_{erase} = 30$ ms (page erase)
> - $T_{write} = 2$ ms (page write)
> - $T_{verify} = 1$ ms (page verify)
>
> At the end, a **full-chip erase** is mandatory. A full-chip erase takes $T_{full\_erase} = 2$ s.
>
> **Calculate the total time to program the chip with a 28 KB user image** if the SPI ISP interface runs at $f_{SCK} = 2$ MHz and each byte of data requires **8 SPI clocks** to transmit (1 byte = 8 bits).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine the number of pages to be programmed.**

The image size is 28 KB. Page size is 256 bytes. Therefore, the number of pages $N_{pages}$ is:

$$
N_{pages} = \frac{28 \text{ KB}}{256 \text{ B/page}} = \frac{28 \times 1024}{256}
$$

$$
N_{pages} = \frac{28672}{256} = 112 \text{ pages}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate time for transmitting the data over SPI.**

Each page has 256 bytes. Each byte needs 8 SPI clocks. So clocks per page:

$$
C_{page} = 256 \times 8 = 2048 \text{ clocks/page}
$$

At $f_{SCK} = 2$ MHz, the time per clock cycle is:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{SCK}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5 \,\mu s
$$

Time to transmit one page:

$$
T_{tx}^{page} = 2048 \times 0.5 \,\mu s = 1024 \,\mu s = 1.024 \text{ ms}
$$

Total transmission time for all 112 pages:

$$
T_{tx}^{total} = 112 \times 1.024 \text{ ms} = 114.688 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate time for erasing each page.**

$$
T_{erase}^{total} = N_{pages} \times T_{erase}^{page} = 112 \times 30 \text{ ms} = 3360 \text{ ms} = 3.36 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate time for writing each page.**

$$
T_{write}^{total} = N_{pages} \times T_{write}^{page} = 112 \times 2 \text{ ms} = 224 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate time for verifying each page.**

$$
T_{verify}^{total} = N_{pages} \times T_{verify}^{page} = 112 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 112 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Add the full-chip erase time.**

$$
T_{full\_erase} = 2 \text{ s} = 2000 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 7 — Sum all components.**

$$
T_{total} = T_{full\_erase} + T_{erase}^{total} + T_{tx}^{total} + T_{write}^{total} + T_{verify}^{total}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 2000 + 3360 + 114.688 + 224 + 112
$$

$$
T_{total} = 5810.688 \text{ ms} \approx 5.81 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** Programming the 28 KB image takes approximately **5.81 seconds** through the SPI ISP interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that the *transmission time* is negligible compared to *erase and write time* — this is why Flash programming speed is dominated by silicon physics, not by the bus bandwidth.

## 3.2 Derivative Example 2 — JTAG Chain Bit Length and Effective Throughput

**Problem Statement:**
Three devices are daisy-chained on a JTAG bus:
- Device A (ARM Cortex-M4) → Instruction Register length $L_A = 4$ bits
- Device B (FPGA) → $L_B = 6$ bits
- Device C (DSP) → $L_C = 8$ bits

**Calculate:**
1. The total IR chain length.
2. The total number of TCK cycles needed to load a new instruction into Device B while keeping A and C in BYPASS.
3. If $f_{TCK} = 10$ MHz, the time to load the instruction.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total chain length.**

$$
L_{total} = L_A + L_B + L_C = 4 + 6 + 8 = 18 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine bypass vs. shift length for Device B.**

When loading a new instruction into Device B, the BYPASS register (1 bit) is selected for A and C, while the Instruction Register of B is selected. The data to be shifted in is **BYPASS (A) + IR (B) + BYPASS (C)**:

$$
L_{shift} = 1 + 6 + 1 = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total TCK cycles.**

In JTAG's Shift-DR state, one bit is shifted per TCK. We also need 1 cycle for Capture-DR and 1 cycle for Update-DR (these are *state transitions*, not bit shifts, but the standard accounts for them as overhead):

$$
N_{TCK} = L_{shift} + 2 = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to load.**

$$
T_{load} = \frac{N_{TCK}}{f_{TCK}} = \frac{10}{10 \times 10^6} = 1 \,\mu s
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interface Comparison

| Parameter | Parallel (8751) | SPI ISP (AVR) | UART Bootloader (STM32) | JTAG | SWD |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|--------------------------|------|-----|
| **Pin Count** | 27+ | 4 | 2 (+BOOT0) | 4–5 | 2 |
| **Clock Source** | External | $f_{OSC}$ | Internal RC | TCK (independent) | SWCLK |
| **Max Speed** | ~20 KB/s | ~50 KB/s | ~11.5 KB/s @ 115200 baud | ~2 MB/s | ~6 MB/s |
| **V_PP Required?** | Yes (12 V) | No | No | No | No |
| **Chip Removal?** | Yes | No | No | No | No |
| **Debug Capable?** | No | Limited | No | Yes (full) | Yes (full) |
| **Boundary Scan?** | No | No | No | Yes (native) | No |
| **Modern Relevance** | Obsolete | Common | Very common | Common (ARM) | Dominant (Cortex-M) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Rule:** If the design needs *only production programming* → use **UART bootloader** (cheapest, no extra pins). If the design needs *development debugging* → use **SWD** (2 pins + full debug). If the design needs *production + boundary-scan test* → use **JTAG**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Programming Interface Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hostPC[Host Computer with .hex File]
    programmerSW[Programmer Software - avrdude - keil - openocd]
    bridgeHW[Hardware Bridge - USBasp - STLink - JLink - CH340]
    iface{Programming Interface Type}
    parallel[Parallel - 8751 legacy - 27 pins]
    spiISP[SPI ISP - 4 pins - AVR - 8051 derivatives]
    uartBoot[UART Bootloader - 2 pins - ARM Cortex-M]
    jtag[JTAG IEEE 1149.1 - 4 to 5 pins]
    swd[SWD Serial Wire Debug - 2 pins - ARM Cortex]
    targetMCU[Target Microcontroller Flash Memory]
    verify[Readback and Verify Operation]
    userRun[Execute User Firmware from Address 0x0000]

    hostPC --> programmerSW
    programmerSW --> bridgeHW
    bridgeHW --> iface
    iface --> parallel
    iface --> spiISP
    iface --> uartBoot
    iface --> jtag
    iface --> swd
    parallel --> targetMCU
    spiISP --> targetMCU
    uartBoot --> targetMCU
    jtag --> targetMCU
    swd --> targetMCU
    targetMCU --> verify
    verify -->|Success| userRun
    verify -->|Failure| programmerSW
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — UART Bootloader Handshake Sequence (STM32-Style)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host as Host PC
    participant MCU as Target MCU Bootloader
    participant Flash as On-Chip Flash

    Note over Host,MCU: BOOT0 HIGH, BOOT1 LOW, RESET pulsed
    Host->>MCU: TX 0x7F (synchronization byte)
    MCU-->>Host: RX 0x79 (ACK)
    Host->>MCU: TX 0x00 (GET command)
    MCU-->>Host: RX 0x79 (ACK) + bootloader version
    Host->>MCU: TX 0x01 (GET-VERSION + checksum)
    MCU-->>Host: RX 0x79 (ACK) + version bytes
    Host->>MCU: TX 0x31 (ERASE ALL + checksum)
    MCU-->>Host: RX 0x79 (ACK)
    Note over MCU,Flash: Full chip erase in progress
    loop For each 256-byte block
        Host->>MCU: TX 0x11 (WRITE + address + data)
        MCU-->>Host: RX 0x79 (ACK)
    end
    Host->>MCU: TX 0x21 (GO + address 0x08000000)
    MCU->>Flash: Jump to user program
    Note over MCU: User firmware executes
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — JTAG Daisy-Chain Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    jtagHost[JTAG Host Probe - JLink]
    tdi1[TDI Pin]
    tdo1[TDO Pin]
    devA[Device A - ARM Cortex - IR 4 bits]
    devB[Device B - FPGA - IR 6 bits]
    devC[Device C - DSP - IR 8 bits]
    bypassA[BYPASS Register 1 bit]
    bypassC[BYPASS Register 1 bit]

    jtagHost -->|TDI| tdi1
    tdi1 --> devA
    devA -->|TDO| devB
    devB -->|TDO| devC
    devC -->|TDO| jtagHost
    devA -.-> bypassA
    devC -.-> bypassC
    Note1[Common TCK and TMS bus shared by all three devices]

    devA ~~~ Note1
    devB ~~~ Note1
    devC ~~~ Note1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — SWD vs JTAG Pin Sharing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mcuPinout[ARM Cortex-M Debug Pinout]
    pin1[PA13 - SWDIO or TMS]
    pin2[PA14 - SWCLK or TCK]
    pin3[PA15 - TDI optional]
    pin4[PB3  - TDO optional]
    pin5[PB4  - NRST reset]
    pin6[PB5  - SWO trace optional]

    mcuPinout --> pin1
    mcuPinout --> pin2
    mcuPinout --> pin3
    mcuPinout --> pin4
    mcuPinout --> pin5
    mcuPinout --> pin6

    pin1 --> modeA[Mode A - SWD only - 2 pins used]
    pin1 --> modeB[Mode B - JTAG - 4 pins used]
    pin2 --> modeA
    pin2 --> modeB
    pin3 --> modeB
    pin4 --> modeB
    pin5 --> modeA
    pin5 --> modeB
    pin6 --> modeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Note:** When the MCU boots up, the debug pin (PA13/PA14) state is sampled. If the pin is HIGH at reset, the chip enters **SWD mode**; if held LOW, JTAG is disabled. This is a one-time factory setting on some ARM parts (e.g., STM32 "Option Bytes") and is a frequent KTU exam question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: List any three advantages of In-System Programming (ISP) over traditional parallel programming of microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No chip removal required** — ISP allows the MCU to be programmed *while soldered* on the PCB, eliminating mechanical stress on pins and the need for a socketed programmer.
2. **Lower pin count** — SPI-based ISP uses only **4 pins** (MOSI, MISO, SCK, RESET), whereas parallel programming of 8751 uses **27+ pins** including a 12 V V_PP supply.
3. **Lower programming voltage** — Modern ISP operates at **V_DD (3.3 V / 5 V)**, avoiding the high-voltage **+12 V V_PP** required by EPROM parallel programming, which simplifies power-supply design and improves safety.
4. *(Bonus, 1 mark if asked)* **Faster field upgrades** — ISP enables in-field firmware updates via bootloader, supporting OTA and DFU flows.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: What is a JTAG TAP controller? Mention its key states.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **TAP (Test Access Port) controller** is a **16-state finite state machine** defined by IEEE 1149.1 that governs the JTAG interface. It is clocked by the **TCK** input and navigated by the **TMS** signal.

**Key States:**
1. **Test-Logic-Reset** — All test logic is disabled; the device is in normal functional mode.
2. **Run-Test/Idle** — The controller is idle between scan operations.
3. **Shift-DR** — Data is being shifted through the selected data register.
4. **Shift-IR** — Instructions are being shifted into the Instruction Register.
5. **Capture-DR / Update-DR** — Boundary-scan latches capture pin values or update outputs.

*(2 marks for definition + TAP explanation, 1 mark for listing 4–5 states.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question offers an **internal choice** (either-or). We provide both options below.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **In-System Programming (ISP) interface** of a modern 8051 derivative (e.g., AT89S52) in detail. Draw the pin connections and describe the **complete programming sequence** with the role of the host programmer. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the JTAG interface** (IEEE 1149.1). List and briefly describe **any four JTAG instructions**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — AT89S52 ISP

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The AT89S52 ISP interface uses the standard SPI protocol with the following connections:
- **P1.5 (MOSI)** ← Host's MOSI (data from host to MCU)
- **P1.6 (MISO)** → Host's MISO (data from MCU to host, for read-back)
- **P1.7 (SCK)** ← Host's SCK (serial clock)
- **RST (Pin 9)** ← Held HIGH for normal operation, pulled LOW to enter ISP mode
- **V_CC (Pin 40)** = +5 V, **GND (Pin 20)** = 0 V
- The chip's internal SPI slave receives instructions and writes them to the **on-chip 8 KB Flash array**.

**Programming Sequence (5 marks):**

1. **Enter Programming Mode:** Apply V_CC = +5 V, hold RST HIGH, then pull RST LOW and back HIGH three times in quick succession (the *three-pulse entry sequence*). After the third HIGH, the chip samples the SPI pins and enters ISP mode.

2. **Enable Programming:** Host sends the **Programming Enable** command byte `0xAC, 0x53, 0x00, 0x00`. If the chip replies with `0x69` on MISO, programming is enabled. *(1 mark)*

3. **Chip Erase:** Host sends the **Chip Erase** command `0xAC, 0x80, 0x00, 0x00`. The chip performs a full Flash erase (takes ~3–5 ms). The chip returns `0x69` on completion.

4. **Write Code Memory (byte-by-byte):** For each byte:
   - Host sends `0x50` (low-byte address) or `0x58` (high-byte) write command, followed by low-address byte, high-address byte, and data byte.
   - Example for address `0x0100`: send `0x50, 0x00, 0x01, <data>`.
   - Each byte write takes **~1.5 ms** internally.
   - Host can poll a *Ready/Busy* bit on MISO. *(1 mark)*

5. **Read Code Memory (for verification):** Host sends the read command `0x20` (or `0x28` for high byte) with the target address. The chip returns the stored byte on MISO. Host compares with the .hex file.

6. **Exit Programming Mode:** Hold RST LOW for 1 µs, then release. The chip exits ISP and runs user code from address 0x0000.

**Role of Host Programmer (2 marks):**
- Reads the **Intel HEX (.hex)** file, decodes records (`:LLAAAATT[DD...]CC`), and serializes bytes to the SPI bus.
- Manages timing (clock generation, RST toggling).
- Verifies each byte after write.
- Typical tools: **Atmel FLIP**, **avrdude**, or **ProgISP**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — JTAG Architecture

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tap[TAP Controller - 16-state FSM]
    tck[TCK pin]
    tms[TMS pin]
    tdi[TDI pin]
    tdo[TDO pin]
    trst[TRST pin optional]
    ir[Instruction Register - 4 bits]
    dr[Data Register bank]
    bypass[Bypass Register 1 bit]
    boundary[Boundary Scan Register]
    idcode[IDCODE Register 32 bits]
    tck --> tap
    tms --> tap
    tdi --> ir
    tdi --> dr
    ir -->|decode| dr
    dr --> bypass
    dr --> boundary
    dr --> idcode
    dr --> tdo
    trst --> tap
```

**Description:** The TAP controller is the heart of JTAG. The host drives TCK (independent of the system clock) and TMS to navigate the state machine. Data enters on TDI, passes through the selected data register, and exits on TDO. The Instruction Register (typically 4 bits wide) decodes which data register is currently active.

**Four JTAG Instructions (4 marks):**

1. **EXTEST (0000)** — Allows testing of off-chip connections by driving boundary-scan cells onto the output pins and capturing values on input pins. Used for **interconnect testing** (e.g., BGA-to-BGA trace shorts).

2. **SAMPLE / PRELOAD (0001)** — Snapshots (samples) the current state of all I/O pins into the boundary-scan chain without disturbing the device, or preloads new values before EXTEST. Used during **in-system testing**.

3. **IDCODE (0010)** — Selects the **32-bit Device Identification Register** which contains the manufacturer's ID, part number, and version. Shifting this out allows the host to confirm the device is correctly connected and to identify it in a daisy chain.

4. **BYPASS (1111)** — Selects a **1-bit bypass register** between TDI and TDO. Used in **daisy-chained** configurations when this particular device is not being addressed, reducing the chain length to 1 bit per bypassed device.

*(For a fifth: HIGHZ (0011) — places all outputs in high-impedance state.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **UART-based bootloader programming** of an **ARM Cortex-M microcontroller** (e.g., STM32F4). Discuss the role of **BOOT0 and BOOT1 pins** and the sequence of commands issued during programming. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Compare and contrast **JTAG and SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** programming interfaces in detail. Mention the pin count, packet structure, advantages, and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — STM32 UART Bootloader

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    host[Host PC - STM32CubeProgrammer]
    usbUart[USB to UART Bridge - CP2102 - CH340 - FT232]
    tx[USART1 TX pin PA9]
    rx[USART1 RX pin PA10]
    boot0[BOOT0 pin]
    boot1[BOOT1 pin]
    nRst[NRST reset]
    sysMem[System Memory - 0x1FFF0000 - ROM Bootloader]
    flash[Main Flash - 0x08000000 - User Code]
    sram[SRAM - 0x20000000 - Debug]

    host --> usbUart
    usbUart -->|TX line| rx
    usbUart -->|RX line| tx
    boot0 -->|HIGH| sysMem
    boot0 -->|LOW| flash
    boot1 -->|LOW| sysMem
    boot1 -->|HIGH| sram
    nRst --> sysMem
    sysMem -.->|reprograms| flash
```

**BOOT0 / BOOT1 Pin Roles (2 marks):**

| BOOT0 | BOOT1 | Boot Location | Address |
|-------|-------|---------------|---------|
| 0 | x | Main Flash (user code) | 0x0800 0000 |
| 1 | 0 | System Memory (ROM bootloader) | 0x1FFF 0000 |
| 1 | 1 | Embedded SRAM (debug) | 0x2000 0000 |

**Programming Command Sequence (3 marks):**

1. **Hardware Setup:** Place BOOT0 jumper HIGH, BOOT1 LOW. Press and release NRST.
2. **Sync:** Host sends byte `0x7F`; bootloader replies with `0x79` (ACK).
3. **GET Command:** Host sends `0x00 0xFF`; bootloader returns version and supported commands.
4. **ERASE Command:** Host sends `0x43 0xBC` (mass erase) or `0x31 0xCE` (extended erase); bootloader acknowledges with `0x79` and erases the Flash.
5. **WRITE Command:** Host sends `0x21 0xDE` (extended) or `0x31 0xCE`, followed by 4-byte address and up to 256 bytes of data, then XOR checksum. Bootloader writes block to Flash and ACKs.
6. **GO Command:** Host sends `0x21 0xDE` with address `0x0800 0000`; bootloader jumps to user code.
7. **Hardware Restore:** Move BOOT0 back to LOW, reset the chip → user code runs from Flash.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — JTAG vs SWD

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (ARM) |
|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Pin Count** | 4 mandatory (TDI, TDO, TCK, TMS) + optional TRST = **5 pins** | 2 mandatory (SWDIO, SWCLK) + optional SWO = **2–3 pins** |
| **Packet Structure** | Variable-length scan chains; instruction + data registers | 3-phase fixed packet: Request (8 bits) → ACK (3 bits) → Data (32 bits) |
| **Clock** | Independent TCK (no system clock needed) | SWCLK up to **50 MHz** |
| **Boundary Scan** | Native (EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD) | Not supported — debug only |
| **Multi-Device** | Native daisy-chain via BYPASS | One target per SWD bus (no daisy chain) |
| **Debug Features** | Halt, step, breakpoints, watchpoints, trace | Same as JTAG, plus SWO trace |
| **Pin Sharing** | Uses 4 specific pins | Often shared with JTAG pins (PA13/PA14) |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (4 toggling lines) | Lower (2 lines) |
| **Tooling** | Universal (Segger J-Link, OpenOCD, Lauterbach) | ARM-specific (ST-Link, CMSIS-DAP, J-Link) |
| **Silicon Cost** | Higher (larger TAP logic) | Lower (smaller SWD unit) |
| **Use Case** | Production test + multi-vendor debug | Modern ARM Cortex-M development |

**Advantages of JTAG (1 mark):**
- Industry-standard, supported by almost every silicon vendor.
- Allows **boundary-scan test** for PCBA manufacturing defects.
- Multi-device daisy-chaining for complex boards with multiple JTAG devices (e.g., ARM + FPGA + DSP).

**Advantages of SWD (1 mark):**
- Only 2 pins → frees up GPIO on pin-constrained MCUs.
- Faster data throughput at high clock rates.
- Lower power, lower silicon area, lower cost.

**Limitations:**
- **JTAG:** Consumes more pins, slower on long daisy chains, requires 4 dedicated test pins.
- **SWD:** ARM-proprietary (cannot debug non-ARM devices), no boundary-scan, single-target only.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting V_PP = 12 V on 8751** — A very common error. Many students write "V_PP = 5 V" which is worth 0 marks for the high-voltage programming question.
> 2. **Confusing ISP with IAP** — ISP is programming *via external host*; IAP is *the application programs itself* (e.g., over the air). Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for conflating the two.
> 3. **Listing wrong BOOT pin combinations** — The STM32 BOOT0/BOOT1 table is precise; do not invent combinations.
> 4. **Missing the checksum byte** in HEX record or programming protocol descriptions.
> 5. **Drawing JTAG with only 2 wires** — Always show 4 mandatory signals (TDI, TDO, TCK, TMS).
> 6. **Confusing the TMS-driven state machine** with a simple shift register. JTAG is *state-machine driven*, not bit-counted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Programming Interface = the hardware/software path to load code into MCU Flash.**
- **Parallel programming (8751)** uses ~27 pins and **V_PP = 12 V**; **obsolete** in modern designs.
- **SPI ISP (AVR, AT89S52)** uses **4 pins** (MOSI, MISO, SCK, RST) and operates at **V_DD**; **modern standard** for 8-bit MCUs.
- **UART Bootloader (ARM Cortex-M)** uses **2 pins** (TX, RX) at **115200 baud, 8E1**; entered via **BOOT0 = HIGH, BOOT1 = LOW**.
- **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** = 4 mandatory pins, 16-state **TAP controller**, native **boundary-scan** and daisy-chain.
- **SWD (ARM)** = 2 pins, max **50 MHz** SWCLK, ARM-specific, dominant on **Cortex-M** parts.
- **Flash memory rule:** Erase before write; cells can only do `1 → 0` directly; `0 → 1` requires an **erase operation**.
- **Key memory metrics:** Endurance = **10⁴ to 10⁵ cycles**; Retention = **≥ 20 years @ 85 °C**.
- **Programming sequence is universal:** Enter mode → Erase → Transfer → Program → Verify → Exit.
- **Modern design preference:** **SWD for debug, UART/USB bootloader for production programming** (cheaper, fewer pins).
- **Always include a checksum** in every data record transmitted over a programming interface.
- **For ESP32 / STM32 / Nordic**: Bootloaders are in **read-only System Memory** → always available, even if user Flash is corrupted.
- **Boundary scan (JTAG EXTEST)** is the de-facto method for testing BGA and fine-pitch PCB interconnects.
- **Decoded HEX file structure** (`:LLAAAARR[DD…]CC`): Start code, byte count, address, record type, data, checksum.
- **JTAG daisy chain length:** $L_{total} = \sum L_i$; use **BYPASS** (1 bit) for inactive devices.
- **SWD packet = 3 phases:** Request (8 bits) → ACK (3 bits) → Data (32 bits).
- **Programming time is dominated by Flash silicon physics (erase + write), not by the bus speed.**
- **In modern designs, prefer ISP over parallel programming for field upgradability and reduced pin count.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming: Arduino IDE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming: Arduino IDE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is a cross-platform, Java-based desktop application officially engineered by *Arduino.cc* to write, compile, verify, and upload firmware (called **sketches**) onto Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards. It abstracts low-level AVR/ARM register-level programming into a simplified C/C++ dialect, allowing developers to interact with the ATmega328P, ESP32, and similar MCUs through high-level function calls.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCPT302 – Module 4)**, the Arduino IDE is treated as the canonical gateway for embedded software development, bridging bare-metal microcontroller concepts (covered in earlier modules) with rapid prototyping using a Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCPT302 – Module 4):**
> The Programming module specifically mandates understanding the *Arduino IDE architecture*, *sketch structure*, *core functions*, *library system*, and the *compile-upload pipeline* before progressing to peripheral interfacing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Workshop Workbench" View

Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen**:
- The **Arduino Board** = the *stove* (hardware that does the actual cooking).
- The **Arduino IDE** = the *recipe book, mixer, and oven controller combined* — it lets you write the recipe (code), test the ingredients (compile), and send it to the stove (upload).
- The **Sketch** = the *recipe card* with `setup()` (preheating the oven) and `loop()` (the actual cooking that repeats).

Without the IDE, you would have to manually type hexadecimal machine code into a hardware programmer — like trying to cook by writing chemistry formulas for every ingredient. The IDE is the friendly translator that converts your human-friendly English-like instructions into the binary language the microcontroller's CPU understands.

### 1.3 Core Architectural Terminology

| Term | Standard Definition | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Sketch** | A program file with `.ino` extension | Source code unit |
| **Toolchain** | `avr-gcc` + `avrdude` compiler and uploader | Translates `.ino` → `.hex` → board flash |
| **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** | Library that maps pin numbers to MCU registers | Hides ATmega328P register complexity |
| **Baud Rate** | **9600 bps (default)** for serial communication | Synchronization speed between PC and board |
| **Flash Memory** | **32 KB** (ATmega328P) | Stores compiled sketch binary |
| **SRAM** | **2 KB** (ATmega328P) | Runtime variables and stack |
| **EEPROM** | **1 KB** (ATmega328P) | Non-volatile user data |

> [!NOTE]
> The **default baud rate of 9600** is the most commonly tested value in KTU viva questions regarding serial monitor initialization. Always memorize it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the Arduino IDE Functions

The IDE executes a **four-stage pipeline** every time you press the "Upload" button:

1. **Preprocessing Stage**
   - The IDE copies all `.ino` files into a temporary build directory.
   - It auto-generates a function prototype section so functions can be called in any order.
   - It creates `main.cpp` which contains the hidden `main()` function that calls `setup()` once and `loop()` infinitely.

2. **Compilation Stage**
   - The bundled `avr-gcc` (or `arm-none-eabi-gcc` for ARM-based boards like Due, Zero) compiler converts C/C++ into **relocatable object code (`.o` files)**.
   - The linker combines all object files and Arduino core libraries into a single **Intel HEX file (`.hex`)**.

3. **Verification Stage**
   - The IDE calculates the **flash memory footprint** and **SRAM usage**.
   - If sketch size exceeds available flash, an error is raised.

4. **Upload Stage**
   - `avrdude` (or `esptool.py` for ESP boards) uses a **serial bootloader** in the MCU to flash the `.hex` file into non-volatile memory.
   - The board resets and execution begins at the bootloader, then jumps to address `0x0000` (the start of user code).

### 2.2 Canonical Sketch Structure (Mandatory KTU Concept)

Every Arduino sketch **must** contain these two functions:

```text
void setup() {
    // Runs EXACTLY ONCE at power-up/reset
    // Used for: pinMode configuration, Serial.begin(), I2C/SPI init
}

void loop() {
    // Runs INFINITELY in a tight while(1) loop
    // Used for: continuous sensor reading, actuator control
}
```

The hidden `main()` function generated by the IDE looks conceptually like:

$$\text{main}() = \text{init}() \rightarrow \text{setup}() \rightarrow \text{for}(;;)\{\text{loop}()\}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Function Cheat Sheet

| Function / Constant | Syntax Signature | Return Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | `pin`: int, `mode`: `INPUT/OUTPUT/INPUT_PULLUP` | `void` | Configures digital pin direction |
| `digitalWrite(pin, value)` | `value`: `HIGH` or `LOW` | `void` | Outputs **5V** or **0V** on pin |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | — | `int` ($0$ or $1$) | Reads logic level of digital pin |
| `analogRead(pin)` | ADC channels on pins A0–A5 | `int` $[0, 1023]$ | 10-bit ADC conversion |
| `analogWrite(pin, value)` | `value`: $[0, 255]$ | `void` | PWM output (8-bit duty cycle) |
| `delay(ms)` | `ms`: unsigned long | `void` | Blocking pause in milliseconds |
| `Serial.begin(baud)` | Standard baud: **9600** | `void` | Initializes UART communication |
| `Serial.print(data)` | Any type via overloading | `void` | Sends data as ASCII text |
| `millis()` | — | `unsigned long` | Milliseconds since boot |
| `attachInterrupt(pin, ISR, mode)` | `mode`: `RISING/FALLING/CHANGE/LOW` | `void` | Hardware interrupt registration |

### 2.4 Voltage & Timing Conversion Equations

These formulas are tested in KTU numerical problems related to `analogRead` and `analogWrite`:

**ADC Resolution (10-bit on ATmega328P):**

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC\_value \times V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{ADC\_value \times 5.0}{1023}$$

**PWM Duty Cycle (8-bit resolution):**

$$Duty\ Cycle\ (\%) = \frac{PWM\_value}{2^{8} - 1} \times 100 = \frac{PWM\_value}{255} \times 100$$

**Analog Output Voltage (after RC low-pass filter):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{Duty\ Cycle}{100} \times V_{cc} = \frac{PWM\_value}{255} \times 5.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The IDE's `analogRead()` and `analogWrite()` are the most frequently asked "numerical conversion" pair in KTU Part B 14-mark questions. Memorize both formulas with their divisors (1023 and 255).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Arduino IDE is the de-facto prototyping tool for:
- **IoT Edge Devices** — Smart agriculture sensors, weather stations.
- **Wearable Electronics** — Fitness bands, biomedical patches.
- **Industrial Automation Prototypes** — PLC replacements for low-cost monitoring.
- **Academic Research** — Sensor interfacing, control system validation.
- **Robotics** — Line-follower, pick-and-place, swarm robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Despite its ease-of-use, the IDE is **not** used in production-grade firmware. The compiled binary consumes more flash than hand-written AVR-C code, and the HAL adds overhead. In industry, developers migrate to **Atmel Studio**, **PlatformIO**, or **STM32CubeIDE** for final deployment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Sketch → HEX File Flow

The Arduino IDE applies a multi-stage transformation. Below is the complete logical chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: User writes} &\ \texttt{sketch.ino} \quad (\text{human-readable C/C++}) \\
\text{Step 2: IDE injects prototypes} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{sketch.cpp} \quad (\text{function prototypes added at top}) \\
\text{Step 3: IDE wraps in main()} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{main.cpp} \quad (\text{setup called once, loop in for(;;))} \\
\text{Step 4: Preprocessor expansion} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{sketch.ii} \quad (\text{macros like HIGH expanded to 1}) \\
\text{Step 5: avr-gcc compilation} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{sketch.o} \quad (\text{ATmega328P machine code object file}) \\
\text{Step 6: Linker resolution} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{sketch.elf} \quad (\text{executable and linkable format}) \\
\text{Step 7: objcopy conversion} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{sketch.hex} \quad (\text{Intel HEX format for programmer)}) \\
\text{Step 8: avrdude upload} &\ \rightarrow \quad \texttt{FLASH MEMORY} \quad (\text{via UART bootloader at 115200 baud)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Justification of Each Step:**
- *Step 1 → 2:* Since users may call functions before declaring them, the IDE scans for definitions and synthesizes prototypes automatically.
- *Step 2 → 3:* The IDE injects a generated `main()` that contains `init()` (timer setup), `setup()`, and an infinite `for(;;)` loop calling `loop()`.
- *Step 3 → 4:* Macros like `HIGH`, `LOW`, `INPUT`, `OUTPUT` (defined in `Arduino.h`) are expanded into integer literals.
- *Step 4 → 5:* The compiler produces position-independent machine code targeted at the AVR instruction set.
- *Step 5 → 6:* The linker merges the sketch object, core library object, and standard C runtime into one ELF file.
- *Step 6 → 7:* `objcopy` strips symbol tables and converts to Intel HEX — a text-encoded binary format.
- *Step 7 → 8:* `avrdude` speaks the **STK500 protocol** with the bootloader, transferring the HEX in chunks of 128 bytes.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ADC to Voltage

**Problem:** An Arduino Uno reads an analog sensor value of **$723$** on pin `A2` with default $V_{ref} = 5.0\text{ V}$. Find the actual input voltage.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \frac{ADC\_value \times V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} \\
&= \frac{723 \times 5.0}{1023} \\
&= \frac{3615}{1023} \\
&\approx 3.534\ \text{volts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. ATmega328P ADC is 10-bit → $2^{10} = 1024$ levels, with max value $1023$.
2. The reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is **$5.0\text{ V}$** (default for Arduino Uno).
3. Apply the linear proportionality formula.
4. Compute the final result: **$3.534\text{ V}$**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — PWM to Average Voltage

**Problem:** Write a value of **$153$** to `analogWrite(9, 153)`. Find the equivalent average DC voltage across a motor terminal (assume RC-filtered).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Duty\ Cycle\ (\%) &= \frac{153}{255} \times 100 = 60.0\% \\
V_{avg} &= \frac{Duty\ Cycle}{100} \times V_{cc} \\
&= 0.60 \times 5.0 \\
&= 3.0\ \text{volts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:**
1. PWM value $153$ is one of 256 levels ($0$ to $255$).
2. The duty cycle is the fraction of time the pin stays HIGH per PWM period.
3. The average DC voltage (with low-pass filter or for slow loads like motors) is the duty cycle multiplied by supply voltage.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Functions vs. Energy Behavior

| Operation | CPU Cycles (ATmega328P @ 16 MHz) | Approx. Time (µs) | Energy Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| `digitalWrite()` | $\approx 50$ cycles | $3.125$ | Low |
| `analogRead()` | $\approx 1300$ cycles (13 ADC cycles $\times$ 128 prescale) | $100$ | Medium |
| `analogWrite()` | $\approx 10$ cycles (timer setup) | $0.625$ | Low |
| `Serial.print()` (1 char) | $\approx 200$ cycles | $12.5$ | Medium |
| `delay(1000)` | Blocks for 1,000,000 cycles | $1{,}000{,}000$ | **Wasted** |
| `millis()` (timer read) | $\approx 5$ cycles | $0.3125$ | Very Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award extra marks if you replace `delay()` with `millis()` in your model answers to demonstrate non-blocking design awareness — a key 2024-scheme OBE outcome.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Arduino IDE Compile-Upload Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Writes sketch.ino] --> B[IDE Preprocessor Adds Prototypes]
    B --> C[IDE Generates main.cpp]
    C --> D[avr-gcc Compiler]
    D --> E[Object File sketch.o]
    E --> F[Linker Joins Core Libs]
    F --> G[ELF File sketch.elf]
    G --> H[objcopy to Intel HEX]
    H --> I[avrdude Uploader]
    I --> J{Board Connected via USB?}
    J -- Yes --> K[STK500 Protocol via UART]
    J -- No --> L[Error: Port Not Found]
    K --> M[Bootloader Writes Flash]
    M --> N[Board Resets]
    N --> O[setup runs Once]
    O --> P[loop runs Forever]
    P --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — IDE User Interface Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI[IDE Main Window] --> Editor[Code Editor Pane]
    UI --> Console[Output Console Pane]
    UI --> Toolbar[Top Toolbar]
    Toolbar --> Verify[Verify Button]
    Toolbar --> Upload[Upload Button]
    Toolbar --> New[New Sketch]
    Toolbar --> Open[Open Sketch]
    Toolbar --> Save[Save Sketch]
    Toolbar --> SerialMon[Serial Monitor]
    UI --> Menu[Menu Bar]
    Menu --> Tools[Tools Menu]
    Tools --> BoardSel[Board Selection]
    Tools --> PortSel[Port Selection]
    Tools --> BaudCfg[Baud Rate Config]
    Tools --> ProgSel[Programmer Type]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Sketch Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power On or Reset]) --> Boot[Bootloader Activates]
    Boot --> Check{New Code Uploaded?}
    Check -- Yes --> Flash[Write HEX to Flash]
    Check -- No --> Jump[Jump to User Code]
    Flash --> Jump
    Jump --> Init[Hardware Init Timers ADC]
    Init --> Setup[Call setup Function Once]
    Setup --> LoopStart[Enter Infinite For Loop]
    LoopStart --> LoopCall[Call loop Function]
    LoopCall --> LoopStart
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Arduino IDE Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Frontend
        Win[Java Swing Window]
        Ed[Editor with Syntax Highlight]
        Mon[Serial Monitor Window]
    end
    subgraph BuildEngine
        Prep[Preprocessor]
        Comp[GCC Cross Compiler]
        Link[Linker]
        OC[Objcopy to HEX]
    end
    subgraph UploadEngine
        AV[avrdude or esptool]
        BL[Bootloader on MCU]
    end
    subgraph Hardware
        Board[Arduino Board]
        MCU[ATmega328P or ESP32]
    end
    Ed --> Prep
    Prep --> Comp
    Comp --> Link
    Link --> OC
    OC --> AV
    AV --> BL
    BL --> MCU
    MCU --> Board
    Mon -.USB UART.-> MCU
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "sketch" in the context of the Arduino IDE. List the two mandatory functions present in every Arduino sketch.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *sketch* is a program file with the `.ino` extension written in the Arduino IDE. It uses a simplified C/C++ dialect and is compiled and uploaded to the microcontroller board.

The two mandatory functions are:
1. **`setup()`** — Executes exactly **once** at power-up or reset. Used for initialization such as `pinMode()` and `Serial.begin()`.
2. **`loop()`** — Executes **infinitely** in a `for(;;)` loop. Contains the main logic that runs continuously.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for setup(), 1 mark for loop() purpose.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the default baud rate used by `Serial.begin()` in Arduino IDE. Mention the resolution of `analogRead()` and `analogWrite()` functions on Arduino Uno.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **default baud rate** for `Serial.begin()` is **9600 bps** (bits per second).
- The `analogRead()` function returns a **10-bit** value ranging from **0 to 1023**, with a reference voltage of **5.0 V** on the Arduino Uno.
- The `analogWrite()` function uses **8-bit PWM resolution** with values from **0 to 255**, producing duty cycles from **0% to 100%**.

*(1 mark each for baud, ADC bits, PWM bits.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **compile and upload pipeline** of the Arduino IDE. List all intermediate file formats generated during this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Arduino Uno is interfaced with an LM35 temperature sensor on pin `A0`. The `analogRead()` function returns a value of **204**. Calculate:
- (i) The actual input voltage at pin A0.
- (ii) The temperature in °C, given that LM35 produces **10 mV/°C**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Compile-Upload Pipeline (7 marks):**

The Arduino IDE converts human-readable C++ into machine-executable binary through the following stages:

1. **Source Capture:** The IDE reads all `.ino` files from the sketch folder and copies them to a temporary build directory.
2. **Prototype Generation:** Function prototypes are auto-inserted to allow forward references.
3. **Main Wrapping:** A hidden `main.cpp` is generated that calls `init()`, then `setup()` once, then enters an infinite `for(;;)` loop calling `loop()`.
4. **Compilation:** `avr-gcc` (or `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) compiles the C++ source into object files (`.o`).
5. **Linking:** The linker combines sketch `.o`, core library `.o`, and C runtime to produce an **ELF file** (`.elf`).
6. **Hex Conversion:** `avr-objcopy` converts the ELF to an **Intel HEX file** (`.hex`).
7. **Upload:** `avrdude` transmits the HEX to the board's **bootloader** via the STK500 protocol over the USB-UART bridge.
8. **Flash Write:** The bootloader writes the program into the ATmega328P's 32 KB flash memory and triggers a reset.

| Stage | Input | Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `.ino` | IDE Preprocessor | `.cpp` |
| 2 | `.cpp` | `avr-gcc` | `.o` |
| 3 | `.o` files | Linker (`avr-ld`) | `.elf` |
| 4 | `.elf` | `avr-objcopy` | `.hex` |
| 5 | `.hex` | `avrdude` | Flash Memory |

*(1 mark per stage explained + 2 marks for table.)*

**(b) ADC to Temperature Conversion (7 marks):**

**(i) Voltage Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \frac{ADC\_value \times V_{ref}}{1023} \\
&= \frac{204 \times 5.0}{1023} \\
&= \frac{1020}{1023} \\
&\approx 0.997\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Temperature Calculation:**

Given LM35 sensitivity = $10\text{ mV}/°\text{C} = 0.01\text{ V}/°\text{C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Temperature\ (°C) &= \frac{V_{in}}{0.01} \\
&= \frac{0.997}{0.01} \\
&\approx 99.7\ °C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The sensor input is **0.997 V**, corresponding to a temperature of **99.7 °C**.

*(1 mark formula, 2 marks voltage calc, 1 mark LM35 formula, 2 marks temp calc, 1 mark final answer with units.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between `analogRead()` and `analogWrite()` functions of the Arduino IDE. Provide the relevant mathematical equations and state the range of values for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Arduino sketch to **blink the built-in LED (pin 13) at 500 ms intervals** using the `millis()` non-blocking approach. Explain why `millis()` is preferred over `delay()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) analogRead() vs analogWrite() (7 marks):**

| Parameter | `analogRead()` | `analogWrite()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Input operation | Output operation |
| **Function** | Samples analog voltage on pin | Generates PWM waveform on pin |
| **Pins Used** | A0–A5 (ADC channels) | Digital pins with PWM: 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 |
| **Resolution** | 10-bit (1024 levels) | 8-bit (256 levels) |
| **Return / Argument Range** | 0 to 1023 | 0 to 255 |
| **Internal Hardware** | ADC module | Timer/Counter in PWM mode |
| **Voltage Relation** | $V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023}$ | $V_{avg} = \frac{PWM \times V_{cc}}{255}$ |

Equations:

$$
V_{in} = \frac{ADC\_value \times V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{ADC\_value \times 5.0}{1023}
$$

$$
Duty\ Cycle\ (\%) = \frac{PWM\_value}{2^{8} - 1} \times 100 = \frac{PWM\_value}{255} \times 100
$$

*(1 mark difference table row, 2 marks formula ADC, 2 marks formula PWM, 2 marks range and pin description.)*

**(b) Non-Blocking Blink Sketch (7 marks):**

```text
const int ledPin = 13;
unsigned long previousMillis = 0;
const long interval = 500;

void setup() {
    pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
    unsigned long currentMillis = millis();
    if (currentMillis - previousMillis >= interval) {
        previousMillis = currentMillis;
        digitalWrite(ledPin, !digitalRead(ledPin));
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

- `millis()` returns the **number of milliseconds** since the board began running the current program.
- The check `currentMillis - previousMillis >= interval` triggers every 500 ms.
- Inside the `if` block, the LED state is **toggled** using `!digitalRead()` (logical NOT), which inverts the current state.
- `previousMillis` is updated to the current time to mark the start of the next interval.

**Why `millis()` is preferred over `delay()`:**

| `delay()` | `millis()` |
|---|---|
| **Blocks** the CPU for the specified duration. | **Non-blocking** — CPU remains free to execute other tasks. |
| Disables interrupts and sensor polling. | Allows multitasking (e.g., reading sensors while blinking LED). |
| Wasteful in time-critical embedded designs. | Essential for **RTOS-like** and **state-machine** designs (a 2024-scheme OBE expectation). |

*(2 marks sketch correctness, 2 marks millis logic, 1 mark toggle trick, 2 marks comparison table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse `analogWrite()` with true analog output. It generates **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)**, not a true DAC voltage.
> - Many students incorrectly write `pinMode(pin, ANALOG)` — this is **invalid syntax**. The pinMode is set to `OUTPUT` for `analogWrite()`.
> - Forgetting to set the **correct Board and Port** in `Tools` menu is the most common reason for "Upload failed" — a frequent viva question.
> - Always mention that the bootloader occupies **0.5 KB** of the 32 KB flash, leaving only **~31.5 KB** for user code.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Arduino IDE** is a cross-platform Java application for writing, compiling, and uploading sketches to microcontroller boards.
- A **sketch** uses the `.ino` extension and must contain `setup()` (runs once) and `loop()` (runs forever).
- The hidden `main()` function is auto-generated and looks like: `init() → setup() → for(;;) { loop() }`.
- **Compile pipeline:** `.ino` → `.cpp` → `.o` → `.elf` → `.hex` → Flash memory.
- `analogRead()` returns **10-bit values (0–1023)**; `analogWrite()` accepts **8-bit values (0–255)**.
- **ADC voltage formula:** $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023}$.
- **PWM duty cycle formula:** $Duty\% = \dfrac{PWM\_value}{255} \times 100$.
- **Default Serial baud rate:** **9600 bps**.
- `digitalWrite()` outputs **5V (HIGH)** or **0V (LOW)** on a digital pin.
- `pinMode()` modes: `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `INPUT_PULLUP` (enables internal ~20–50 kΩ resistor).
- `delay()` is **blocking**; `millis()` is **non-blocking** — preferred for multitasking.
- The IDE uses the **STK500 protocol** via the on-board **UART bootloader** to flash code.
- The **ATmega328P** has **32 KB flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM**.
- Arduino IDE is for **prototyping**; production firmware uses **Atmel Studio / PlatformIO / STM32CubeIDE**.
- Library files end with `.h` (header) and `.cpp` (implementation) and are placed in the `libraries/` folder.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample Code (LED, Switch, DC Motor, Stepper Motor Control, Temperature Monitoring System Using LM35 Temperature Sensor & Seven Segment Display)
Embedded Operating System Basic Concepts: Functional Layers in a Computer System, OS Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Embedded Operating System Basic Concepts: Functional Layers in a Computer System, OS Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Embedded Operating System (EOS) — Formal Definition

An **Embedded Operating System (EOS)** is a specialized, resource-constrained operating system designed to perform dedicated functions within a larger mechanical or electronic system, typically running on a microcontroller or microprocessor with strict **real-time**, **deterministic**, and **resource-efficiency** constraints. Unlike a general-purpose OS (GPO) such as Windows or Linux, an EOS is tightly integrated with the hardware and is often invisible to the end-user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCPT302 - Module 4):**
> An **Embedded OS** is a piece of software that manages the resources of an embedded system and provides abstractions (APIs/services) to application software, allowing multiple logical tasks to share the underlying hardware (CPU, memory, peripherals) **deterministically** and within bounded time.

## 1.2 Functional Layers in a Computer System

A computer system (whether a desktop PC or an embedded IoT node) is organized as a **stack of functional layers**, where each layer hides the complexity of the layer beneath it and provides services to the layer above it. This is also called the **Abstraction Layer Model** or **Hierarchical Architecture Model**.

### Layer-by-Layer Overview

| Layer No. | Layer Name | Role | Embedded-Specific Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L5** | User / Application Layer | End-user programs, GUI, business logic | A temperature control loop in a smart thermostat |
| **L4** | High-Level Application / Middleware | Libraries, protocol stacks (HTTP, MQTT, CAN) | FreeRTOS-Plus-TCP, LwIP stack |
| **L3** | Operating System (OS / Kernel) | Scheduling, IPC, memory mgmt, device drivers | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS, Zephyr |
| **L2** | System Software / Firmware | Bootloader, BIOS, HAL, device drivers | U-Boot, STM32 HAL libraries |
| **L1** | Hardware Layer | CPU core, memory, I/O, timers, ADC, buses | ARM Cortex-M4, SRAM, GPIO, SPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Onion Principle"**: Each layer is a *client* of the layer below and a *server* to the layer above. An application developer writing a temperature logger on an STM32 never needs to know how the SPI peripheral is wired — the **HAL/driver layer** abstracts it.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — A Restaurant Kitchen:**

| Computer System Layer | Restaurant Analogy |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware (Oven, Stove, Fridge)** | The physical equipment |
| **System Software (Power-on self-test, gas-line check)** | The utility systems |
| **Operating System (Head Chef)** | Decides *which* cook prepares *which* dish, in *which* order, for *which* table |
| **Application Software (Recipe book)** | The specific dishes (Chicken Tikka, Pasta) |
| **User (Diner)** | The customer placing an order |

Just as a **head chef** schedules multiple cooks (preemptive multitasking) and ensures the food comes out in a **bounded time** (real-time), the OS schedules multiple *tasks* on a single CPU and ensures **deadlines** are met.

## 1.4 Why Embedded OS Concepts Matter

In a **bare-metal** embedded system, the application code directly controls the CPU. As complexity grows (think: a modern car has **~100+ ECUs** running **~300 million lines of code**), bare-metal becomes unmanageable. Hence, an **abstraction hierarchy** is mandatory. Understanding the layers helps you:
- Debug latency spikes (cross-layer issues).
- Choose the right RTOS for the deadline.
- Optimize memory footprint at the right layer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Functional Layer Breakdown

### 🔹 Layer 1 — Hardware Layer
- **Contains:** CPU/ALU, registers, cache, RAM (volatile), ROM/Flash (non-volatile), buses (AMBA, AHB, APB), peripherals (UART, I²C, SPI, ADC, PWM, timers, DMA, GPIO).
- **Properties:** Physical, fastest, no abstraction.
- **Embedded-Specific Note:** Many embedded MCUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+) lack an MMU, leading to a **flat memory model** where the OS kernel and tasks share a single address space.

### 🔹 Layer 2 — System Software / Firmware Layer
- **Contains:** **Bootloader**, **BIOS/UEFI** (in PCs), **HAL** (Hardware Abstraction Layer), **device drivers** (low-level), BSP (Board Support Package).
- **Function:** Translates high-level OS requests into hardware register manipulations (`SET_BIT`, `RESET_BIT`).
- **Embedded-Specific:** In a typical STM32 project, `startup_stm32.s` + `system_stm32f4xx.c` form the *minimum firmware* before `main()`.

### 🔹 Layer 3 — Operating System / Kernel Layer
- **Contains:** **Scheduler**, **Dispatcher**, **Memory Manager**, **IPC Manager**, **Synchronization Primitives** (semaphores, mutexes, queues), **Interrupt Manager**, **System Call Interface (SCI)**.
- **Function:** Virtualizes hardware, enforces timing guarantees, manages concurrency.
- **Embedded-Specific:** Most embedded RTOSes use a **microkernel** architecture (e.g., QNX, MINIX) where only scheduling, IPC, and basic drivers are in kernel space; everything else (file systems, networking) runs as user tasks.

### 🔹 Layer 4 — Middleware / Application Framework
- **Contains:** Protocol stacks (TCP/IP, Bluetooth, CAN, MQTT), file systems (FatFs, LittleFS), graphics libraries (LVGL), cryptographic libraries (mbedTLS).
- **Function:** Provides reusable, off-the-shelf components so application developers don't reinvent the wheel.

### 🔹 Layer 5 — Application Layer
- **Contains:** End-user application logic, control algorithms (PID, state machines), UI logic.
- **Function:** Solves the *business problem* (e.g., "maintain a motor at 1500 RPM" or "decode a GPS NMEA sentence").

## 2.2 Core OS Terminology (KTU-Exam Favorites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following terms are **high-frequency** in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize definitions and one-line uses.

| # | Term | Precise Definition | One-Line Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Kernel** | Core component of OS that manages CPU, memory, and interrupts. | "Heart of the OS" |
| 2 | **Task / Process** | Independent program in execution, with its own context. | "Unit of scheduling" |
| 3 | **Thread** | Lightweight sub-unit of a process sharing its address space. | "Lightweight task" |
| 4 | **Multitasking** | Concurrent execution of multiple tasks on a single CPU via time-slicing. | "CPU virtualization" |
| 5 | **Preemptive Scheduling** | OS can forcibly suspend a running task to give CPU to a higher-priority one. | "Used in hard RTOS" |
| 6 | **Cooperative Scheduling** | Tasks voluntarily yield the CPU. | "Used in simple loop OS" |
| 7 | **Scheduler** | Algorithm that decides which ready task runs next. | "Rate-Monotonic, EDF" |
| 8 | **Context Switch** | Saving state of one task and loading state of another. | "Cost: ~5–50 µs" |
| 9 | **Interrupt** | Hardware/software event that suspends normal execution. | "Asynchronous notification" |
| 10 | **ISR** (Interrupt Service Routine) | Special function executed in response to an interrupt. | "Must be short and fast" |
| 11 | **Semaphore** | Synchronization primitive with a counter (signaling + mutual exclusion). | "Binary / Counting / Mutex" |
| 12 | **Mutex** | Mutual exclusion lock — only one task holds it at a time. | "Protects shared resource" |
| 13 | **Deadlock** | Two or more tasks permanently block each other. | "Coffman conditions: 4" |
| 14 | **Priority Inversion** | Lower-priority task blocks a higher-priority task via shared resource. | "Mars Pathfinder bug" |
| 15 | **RTOS** (Real-Time OS) | OS with bounded, deterministic response times. | "Hard vs Soft RTOS" |
| 16 | **Hard Real-Time** | Missing a deadline is a **total system failure**. | "ABS braking, pacemaker" |
| 17 | **Soft Real-Time** | Missing a deadline degrades quality but does not cause failure. | "Video streaming" |
| 18 | **Latency** | Time between event occurrence and system response. | "Interrupt latency" |
| 19 | **Jitter** | Variation in latency between successive events. | "Audio/video sync" |
| 20 | **Throughput** | Number of tasks/work units completed per unit time. | "Tasks per second" |
| 21 | **Deadline** | Latest time by which a task must complete. | "Absolute or relative" |
| 22 | **Determinism** | Property that the system's response time is **bounded and predictable**. | "Worst-case execution time (WCET) known" |
| 23 | **System Call (SCI)** | Controlled entry point into the kernel from user space. | "Privileged mode switch" |
| 24 | **IPC** (Inter-Process Communication) | Mechanism for tasks to exchange data/synchronize. | "Queues, pipes, mailboxes" |
| 25 | **Bootloader** | Small program that runs first to load the main OS/firmware. | "Stage 1 + Stage 2" |
| 26 | **Firmware** | Software permanently stored in ROM/Flash. | "Tightly coupled to hardware" |
| 27 | **Device Driver** | Software that controls a specific hardware peripheral. | "Translates API → register writes" |
| 28 | **MMU** (Memory Management Unit) | Hardware unit doing virtual-to-physical address translation. | "Not present in M-class ARM" |
| 29 | **POSIX** | IEEE standard for portable OS APIs. | "pthread, semaphores" |
| 30 | **Race Condition** | Bug where output depends on unpredictable timing. | "Disable interrupts or use mutex" |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: Hard RTOS schedules engine-control tasks at 1 ms tick; deterministic.
- **Medical (FDA Class III)**: Pacemaker firmware — soft real-time, no jitter, watchdog timer.
- **Consumer IoT**: Zephyr RTOS on nRF52 — soft real-time, BLE stack as middleware.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C)**: VxWorks in Mars rovers — hard real-time, certified to Level A.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below use **standard real-time scheduling and OS performance metrics** that are routinely asked in KTU 14-mark questions. Understand every algebraic step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Task Timing Metrics (The "Big Four")

### 3.1.1 Define the Four Canonical Time Quantities

For any task $T_i$ in a scheduling problem:

- **Arrival Time** $a_i$ — instant the task becomes ready (released).
- **Burst Time / Computation Time** $C_i$ — total CPU time the task needs.
- **Completion Time** $c_i$ — instant the task finishes its last instruction.
- **Deadline** $d_i$ — latest allowed completion time (absolute).

From these, we derive the **performance metrics**:

$$
\text{Turnaround Time (TAT)} \;\; TAT_i \;=\; c_i \;-\; a_i
$$

$$
\text{Waiting Time} \;\; W_i \;=\; TAT_i \;-\; C_i \;=\; (c_i - a_i) - C_i
$$

$$
\text{Response Time} \;\; R_i \;=\; (\text{first CPU allocation time}) - a_i
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} \;\; \Theta \;=\; \frac{N}{\text{Total Time}}
$$

where $N$ is the number of tasks completed in the observation window.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — FCFS Scheduling (Step-by-Step)

Consider **three tasks** with the following data:

| Task | Arrival $a_i$ | Burst $C_i$ | Deadline $d_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| T1 | 0 ms | 4 ms | 7 ms |
| T2 | 1 ms | 3 ms | 8 ms |
| T3 | 2 ms | 2 ms | 10 ms |

Using **First-Come First-Served (FCFS)** non-preemptive scheduling:

**Step 1 — Build the Gantt chart.**

| Time (ms) | 0 → 4 | 4 → 7 | 7 → 9 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Running | T1 | T2 | T3 |

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Times.**

- $c_{T1} = 0 + 4 = 4$ ms
- $c_{T2} = 4 + 3 = 7$ ms
- $c_{T3} = 7 + 2 = 9$ ms

**Step 3 — Compute TAT.**

$$
TAT_{T1} = c_{T1} - a_{T1} = 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
TAT_{T2} = c_{T2} - a_{T2} = 7 - 1 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
TAT_{T3} = c_{T3} - a_{T3} = 9 - 2 = 7 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time.**

$$
W_{T1} = TAT_{T1} - C_{T1} = 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
W_{T2} = TAT_{T2} - C_{T2} = 6 - 3 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
W_{T3} = TAT_{T3} - C_{T3} = 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Average TAT and Average WT.**

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{4 + 6 + 7}{3} = \frac{17}{3} \approx 5.67 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\overline{W} = \frac{0 + 3 + 5}{3} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Check Deadline Misses.**

- T1: $c_{T1}=4 \le d_{T1}=7$ ✅
- T2: $c_{T2}=7 \le d_{T2}=8$ ✅
- T3: $c_{T3}=9 \le d_{T3}=10$ ✅

No deadline missed.

**Step 7 — Compute Throughput.**

Total time elapsed = 9 ms, tasks completed = 3.

$$
\Theta = \frac{3}{9 \text{ ms}} = 0.333 \text{ tasks/ms} = 333 \text{ tasks/s}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Schedulability Test

For a set of $n$ **periodic, independent, preemptive** tasks under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**, a sufficient (not necessary) schedulability bound is given by **Liu & Layland (1973)**:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;\le\; n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

where $T_i$ is the period of task $i$ and $C_i$ is its worst-case execution time.

**Derivation sketch:**

Let $f(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} f(n) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

So the **asymptotic utilization bound** is **69.31 %**. For specific values of $n$:

| $n$ | $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1.0000 |
| 2 | 0.8284 |
| 3 | 0.7798 |
| 4 | 0.7568 |
| 5 | 0.7435 |
| ∞ | 0.6931 (≈ ln 2) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the test fails, the system is *not necessarily* unschedulable — the bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. The **exact test** requires **time-demand analysis**:
> $$\forall i, \; \forall t \in (0, T_i]: \quad \sum_{j=1}^{i} \frac{C_j}{T_j}\, t \;\ge\; C_i$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Interrupt Latency and Response Time

**Interrupt Latency** $L_I$ is the time from the interrupt signal occurring at the pin to the first instruction of the ISR executing.

$$
L_I \;=\; t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{finish current inst}} + t_{\text{context save}} + t_{\text{jump to ISR}}
$$

**Total Response Time** of an interrupt-driven task:

$$
R_{\text{total}} \;=\; L_I \;+\; t_{\text{ISR}} \;+\; t_{\text{scheduler}} \;+\; t_{\text{context switch}} \;+\; C_{\text{task}}
$$

where:
- $t_{\text{ISR}}$ — duration of the ISR handler (must be short, ≤ 10 µs in hard RTOS).
- $t_{\text{scheduler}}$ — time to pick the next ready task.
- $t_{\text{context switch}}$ — time to save/restore CPU registers (≈ 5–50 µC cycles).

> [!TIP]
> **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** analysis in hard real-time systems always uses the **maximum** of each term:
> $$R_{\max} = L_{I,\max} + t_{\text{ISR},\max} + t_{\text{sched},\max} + t_{\text{cs},\max} + C_{\max}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Performance Table

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Goal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| TAT | $c_i - a_i$ | seconds | Minimize |
| Waiting Time | $TAT_i - C_i$ | seconds | Minimize |
| Response Time | $R_{\text{first}} - a_i$ | seconds | Minimize |
| Throughput | $N / T_{\text{total}}$ | tasks/sec | Maximize |
| CPU Utilization | $\left(\sum C_i / T_{\text{total}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % | Balanced (not 100% in RTOS) |
| Deadline Miss Ratio | Missed / Total | dimensionless | → 0 in hard RTOS |
| Interrupt Latency | $\Delta t$ from IRQ to ISR | µs | < 10 µs typical |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Layer Architecture of an Embedded Computer System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 5: USER / APPLICATION LAYER"]:::top
    L4["LAYER 4: MIDDLEWARE / APPLICATION FRAMEWORK"]:::mid
    L3["LAYER 3: OPERATING SYSTEM / KERNEL"]:::core
    L2["LAYER 2: SYSTEM SOFTWARE / FIRMWARE"]:::base
    L1["LAYER 1: HARDWARE LAYER"]:::hw

    L5 -->|API calls| L4
    L4 -->|System calls / POSIX| L3
    L3 -->|HAL calls / driver calls| L2
    L2 -->|Register writes / bus cycles| L1
    L1 -.->|Interrupts / DMA / signals| L2
    L2 -.->|Acknowledgment| L3
    L3 -.->|Event notification| L4
    L4 -.->|Callback / event| L5

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows (downward) show *command flow* (e.g., application requests a file open). Dashed arrows (upward) show *event flow* (e.g., a UART byte received triggers an interrupt that bubbles up to the application via a callback).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Internal OS Kernel Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP["APPLICATION TASKS"]:::app
    SCI["SYSTEM CALL INTERFACE"]:::ifc
    SCHED["SCHEDULER / DISPATCHER"]:::core
    MEM["MEMORY MANAGER"]:::core
    IPC["IPC MANAGER"]:::core
    SYNC["SYNC PRIMITIVES MUTEX SEMAPHORE QUEUE"]:::core
    INTMGR["INTERRUPT MANAGER"]:::core
    DRV["DEVICE DRIVERS"]:::drv
    HW["HARDWARE CPU RAM PERIPHERALS"]:::hw

    APP -->|syscall| SCI
    SCI --> SCHED
    SCI --> MEM
    SCI --> IPC
    SCI --> SYNC
    INTMGR -->|preempt| SCHED
    SCHED --> DRV
    DRV --> HW
    HW -->|IRQ| INTMGR
    INTMGR -->|signal| APP

    classDef app fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ifc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Task State Transition (Embedded RTOS)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> READY : Task created
    READY --> RUNNING : Scheduler dispatches
    RUNNING --> READY : Time slice expired OR preempted
    RUNNING --> BLOCKED : Awaiting resource / IPC
    BLOCKED --> READY : Resource available / IPC received
    RUNNING --> SUSPENDED : vTaskSuspend
    SUSPENDED --> READY : vTaskResume
    RUNNING --> [*] : Task deleted
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **preemptive RTOS**, the transition `RUNNING → READY` is triggered by the **SysTick interrupt** (e.g., every 1 ms on Cortex-M). In a **cooperative** OS, this transition only happens if the task calls a yield function.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Interrupt vs. Polling vs. RTOS Task

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EVT["HARDWARE EVENT SENSOR DATA READY"]:::hw
    Q1{"Approach?"}:::decision
    POLL["POLLING: Main loop checks flag"]:::app
    ISR["INTERRUPT: ISR fires, sets flag"]:::core
    RTOS["RTOS TASK: Blocked on semaphore, wakes on ISR"]:::core

    POLL -->|Latency up to full loop| APP1["Best for: low-power, simple MCUs"]:::app
    ISR -->|Latency: few microseconds| APP2["Best for: time-critical events"]:::core
    RTOS -->|Latency: bounded by priority| APP3["Best for: multiple concurrent events"]:::core

    EVT --> Q1
    Q1 --> POLL
    Q1 --> ISR
    Q1 --> RTOS

    classDef hw fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
