# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "monolithic" with "unmodular" — Linux is a *modular monolithic* kernel. Mentioning this nuance in a 14-mark answer often scores an extra 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Never claim** that microkernels are *always* slower. Modern L4-family microkernels (seL4, OKL4) achieve IPC latencies within **~10%** of direct function calls. Credit is given for stating this.
> 3. **Always** support your kernel choice with a *domain context* (automotive, IoT, avionics, etc.). A naked "microkernel is best" without justification loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Draw the block diagram** in Part B answers — even a hand-drawn version of the architectures shown in Section 4 is worth **3–4 marks** by itself.
> 5. **Trap/return vs context switch** — students routinely mix up *trap entry* ($T_{\text{trap}}$) and *context switch* ($T_{\text{cs}}$). A context switch involves *saving/restoring registers + TLB flush*; a trap is just *mode switch*. Examiners mark this distinction carefully.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Monolithic Kernel. List any two of its advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *Monolithic Kernel* is an operating-system architecture in which **all core OS services** (process management, memory management, file systems, device drivers, networking, and the system call interface) execute **in a single privileged address space (kernel space, Ring 0)**. Examples: **traditional UNIX, Linux, MS-DOS**.
>
> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. *High performance* — components communicate via direct function calls; no IPC overhead.
> 2. *Simple design* — single binary; lower complexity for small systems.
> 3. *Lower context-switch cost* — only one mode transition per system call.
>
> **Disadvantage (any one):**
> 1. *Poor fault isolation* — a bug in any driver (e.g., USB) can crash the entire OS.
> 2. *Large TCB* — difficult to formally verify or certify to high EAL levels.
> 3. *Difficult to extend/maintain* — monolithic source trees (e.g., Linux) require complex patch management.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Microkernel? How does it achieve fault isolation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *Microkernel* is an OS architecture in which the kernel contains **only the minimum essential services** — IPC, basic scheduling, address-space management, and interrupt dispatch. All other services (file systems, drivers, network stack) run as ordinary **user-space server processes**.
>
> **Fault isolation is achieved** by the following mechanism:
> 1. Services run in separate *user-space processes* with their own address spaces.
> 2. They communicate *only* via the kernel's IPC mechanism — never via direct memory access.
> 3. If a user-space server (e.g., the audio driver) crashes, the kernel can **detect, restart, or replace it** without affecting other servers or the kernel itself.
> 4. Memory protection enforced by the **MMU** prevents a faulty driver from corrupting kernel memory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architectural design of a **Monolithic Kernel** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages for an embedded real-time system.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **system-call path** of a monolithic kernel vs. a microkernel, using a timing diagram and the relevant latency equations.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Monolithic Kernel Architecture — 7 marks**

A *Monolithic Kernel* is an OS architecture in which the **entire operating system — scheduler, memory manager, file system, network stack, device drivers, and system call interface — runs in a single privileged address space (kernel space, Ring 0)** as one large binary. The user applications reside separately in user space (Ring 3) and access kernel services only through the **System Call Interface (SCI)**.

**Block Diagram (must be drawn):**

```
USER SPACE (Ring 3)
+------------------+   +------------------+
|  Application A   |   |  Application B   |
+--------+---------+   +--------+---------+
         | System Call (trap)        |
         v                            v
+----------------------------------------+
|   MONOLITHIC KERNEL (Ring 0)           |
|   +-----------+ +-----------+ +------+ |
|   | Scheduler | |  Memory   | | VFS  | |
|   +-----------+ |  Manager  | +------+ |
|   +-----------+ +-----------+ +------+ |
|   | Network   | |  Drivers  | | IPC  | |
|   | Stack     | |  USB I2C  | |      | |
|   +-----------+ +-----------+ +------+ |
+----------------------------------------+
              |
              v
+----------------------------------------+
|   HARDWARE (CPU, MMU, RAM, I/O)        |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**Advantages for embedded RTOS:**
1. **Speed** — direct function calls eliminate IPC overhead; ideal for high-throughput signal processing (e.g., radar, SDR).
2. **Simplicity** — one binary, easier to boot and flash onto an MCU.
3. **Low per-call latency** — typically one trap entry + one return.
4. **Mature code base** — e.g., Embedded Linux has thousands of pre-existing drivers.

**Disadvantages for embedded RTOS:**
1. **Poor fault isolation** — a faulty driver can crash the *whole* system; unacceptable in safety-critical designs.
2. **Large TCB** — hard to certify to ISO 26262 / DO-178C.
3. **Difficult to update in the field** — adding a driver often requires recompiling the entire kernel.
4. **Memory footprint** — monolithic images (even stripped) often exceed 1 MB; unsuitable for low-end MCUs.

*(Closing statement — 1 mark):* A monolithic kernel is the right choice **only** for cost-sensitive, non-safety-critical embedded systems such as consumer IoT gateways, where performance and driver breadth outweigh fault-isolation concerns.

---

**(b) System-Call Path Comparison — 7 marks**

| Stage | Monolithic Path | Microkernel Path |
|---|---|---|
| 1. User invokes call | `open()` in glibc | `open()` → `MsgSend()` |
| 2. Mode transition | **1 trap** (Ring 3 → 0) | **1 trap** (Ring 3 → 0) |
| 3. Routing | Kernel's `sys_open()` direct call | Kernel IPC delivers to server |
| 4. Mode transition | — (stays in Ring 0) | **1 trap** (Ring 0 → 3) to enter server |
| 5. Execution | Direct call to VFS/driver | Server's open() runs in user space |
| 6. Reply | Direct return | **1 trap** (server → kernel → caller) |
| **Total traps** | **1** | **2–3** |

**Latency Equations:**

For a monolithic kernel:

$$
T_{\text{mono}} = T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{service}} + T_{\text{return}}
$$

For a microkernel:

$$
T_{\text{micro}} = 2\,T_{\text{trap}} + 2\,T_{\text{return}} + T_{\text{sched}} + T_{\text{service}} + 2\,T_{\text{copy}}
$$

*Timing diagram (must be drawn):*

```
Monolithic:   |==TRAP==|=SERVICE=|==RETURN==|
                              Total: T_mono

Microkernel:  |==TRAP==|==IPC==|==TRAP==|=SERVICE=|==TRAP==|==RETURN==|
                   |_____|              |_____|
                  overhead            overhead
                              Total: T_micro ≈ 1.5× to 3× T_mono
```

*(Synthesis — 1 mark):* Although the microkernel has higher absolute latency, the **worst-case latency is bounded and analytically provable**, which is what makes it preferable for *hard* real-time systems. The monolithic kernel's average case is faster, but the *worst case* is unbounded (any driver bug can hang the kernel for arbitrary time).

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` (Alternative Choice)**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Microkernel architecture** with a clear diagram. Explain the role of **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** in achieving modularity and fault isolation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A safety-critical automotive ECU must meet **ASIL-D** under ISO 26262, with a maximum service-call deadline of **10 µs** on a **200 MHz Cortex-R5** core. Compare and recommend the suitable kernel architecture, with full numerical justification. (Assume $T_{\text{trap}} = 0.4~\mu s$, $T_{\text{return}} = 0.3~\mu s$, $T_{\text{sched}} = 0.6~\mu s$, $T_{\text{service}} = 2.5~\mu s$, $T_{\text{copy}} = 0.2~\mu s$.)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Microkernel Architecture & IPC Role — 7 marks**

A *Microkernel* is a minimal OS core that contains **only the most essential mechanisms** — IPC, thread scheduling, address-space management, and interrupt dispatch. All other services are implemented as *servers* running in unprivileged user space, communicating with each other and with applications through the kernel's IPC facility.

**Block Diagram (must be drawn):**

```
USER SPACE (Ring 3) — Servers
+----------+  +----------+  +----------+  +----------+
|  File    |  | Network  |  | Driver   |  | POSIX    |
|  System  |  |  Stack   |  | Servers  |  |  Server  |
+----------+  +----------+  +----------+  +----------+
       |              |             |              |
       +------+-------+------+------+              |
              |   IPC Messages        |
              v                               
+---------------------------------------------+
|   MICROKERNEL (Ring 0) - TCB < 15 KLOC      |
|   +--------+ +-----------+ +-------------+  |
|   |  IPC   | | Scheduler | | Addr Space  |  |
|   | Engine | |           | | Manager     |  |
|   +--------+ +-----------+ +-------------+  |
|   +-------------+                           |
|   | Interrupt   |                           |
|   | Dispatcher  |                           |
|   +-------------+                           |
+---------------------------------------------+
                       |
                       v
+---------------------------------------------+
|   HARDWARE (CPU, MMU, RAM, Devices)         |
+---------------------------------------------+
```

**Role of IPC in Modularity and Fault Isolation:**

1. **Modularity** — Each service is an independent *process*. To add a new feature (e.g., a CAN bus driver), one simply *starts a new user-space process* — no kernel modification or recompilation is needed. The kernel never has to be re-certified.
2. **Fault Isolation** — Because servers have *separate address spaces* enforced by the MMU, a fault in one server (e.g., a null-pointer dereference in the file-system server) cannot corrupt the memory of another server or the kernel. The kernel can monitor the IPC channel and **restart the failed server transparently** — a feature impossible in monolithic kernels.
3. **Hardware Independence** — A user-space driver can be ported to new hardware (e.g., a new SoC) by recompiling only that server, leaving the kernel untouched.
4. **Security** — All inter-process communication passes through the kernel's IPC, which can enforce *authentication, encryption, and access control* (capability-based security in seL4).

*(Closing — 1 mark):* The IPC engine is therefore the *heart* of the microkernel — it is what enables the philosophy of *minimality* to deliver real engineering benefits.

---

**(b) Numerical Justification for Automotive ECU — 7 marks**

**Given:** $T_{\text{trap}} = 0.4~\mu s$, $T_{\text{return}} = 0.3~\mu s$, $T_{\text{sched}} = 0.6~\mu s$, $T_{\text{service}} = 2.5~\mu s$, $T_{\text{copy}} = 0.2~\mu s$, deadline = **10 µs**.

**Step 1 — Monolithic latency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{mono}} &= T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{service}} + T_{\text{return}} \\
&= 0.4 + 2.5 + 0.3 \\
&= 3.2~\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Microkernel latency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{micro}} &= 2\,T_{\text{trap}} + 2\,T_{\text{return}} + T_{\text{sched}} + T_{\text{service}} + 2\,T_{\text{copy}} \\
&= 2(0.4) + 2(0.3) + 0.6 + 2.5 + 2(0.2) \\
&= 0.8 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 2.5 + 0.4 \\
&= 4.9~\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Deadline check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{mono}} &= 3.2~\mu s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Slack} = 10 - 3.2 = 6.8~\mu s \\
T_{\text{micro}} &= 4.9~\mu s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Slack} = 10 - 4.9 = 5.1~\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

Both satisfy the 10 µs deadline. **However, the ASIL-D requirement dominates the decision.**

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**

Despite the higher raw latency, the **microkernel (e.g., QNX Neutrino or AUTOSAR OS on a microkernel like PikeOS)** is the **mandatory** choice for ASIL-D because:

1. **Formal verification** — A microkernel with ~12 KLOC (e.g., seL4) can be machine-verified; a 170+ KLOC monolithic kernel cannot.
2. **Freedom From Interference (FFI)** — ISO 26262 ASIL-D requires that ASIL-D software elements are not corrupted by QM (Quality Managed) elements. Only a microkernel's address-space isolation *enforces* this at runtime.
3. **Bounded WCET** — The IPC path's worst-case execution time is *analytically determinable*; a monolithic kernel's WCET is effectively unbounded due to shared state.
4. **5.1 µs slack** is *more than adequate* — typical brake-by-wire loops run at **10–20 ms** periods, and the service call here is one small portion.
5. **Field upgradeability** — A bug in the CAN driver can be fixed by *replacing the user-space driver* without re-certifying the kernel.

*(Conclusion — 1 mark):* **Recommendation: Microkernel-based RTOS (QNX / AUTOSAR Adaptive on a microkernel).** The 1.7 µs additional latency is *trivially* absorbed in the 10 µs budget, while the certification and safety benefits are indispensable for ASIL-D compliance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Kernel** = the privileged core of the OS; first to load, last to leave.
- ✅ **Monolithic Kernel** = *all* services in **one privileged address space**; communication = direct function calls.
- ✅ **Microkernel** = *only* IPC + scheduler + memory + interrupt in kernel space; all other services in **user space**; communication = **message passing**.
- ✅ **Hybrid Kernel** = microkernel foundation + performance-critical services in kernel space (Windows NT, macOS XNU).
- ✅ **Key examples to memorize:**
  - *Monolithic*: Linux, UNIX, MS-DOS, classical VxWorks.
  - *Microkernel*: QNX Neutrino, MINIX 3, seL4, L4, Hurd, MACH.
  - *Hybrid*: Windows NT 10/11, macOS, iOS.
- ✅ **Performance order (latency):** Monolithic < Hybrid < Microkernel (in raw throughput).
- ✅ **Safety order (fault isolation):** Monolithic < Hybrid < Microkernel.
- ✅ **Formulas to remember cold:**
  - $T_{\text{mono}} = T_{\text{trap}} + T_{\text{service}} + T_{\text{return}}$
  - $T_{\text{micro}} = 2\,T_{\text{trap}} + 2\,T_{\text{return}} + T_{\text{sched}} + T_{\text{service}} + 2\,T_{\text{copy}}$
  - $\eta_{\text{IPC}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{service}}}{T_{\text{micro}}} \times 100\%$
  - $R_{\text{TCB}} = \text{TCB}_{\text{mono}} / \text{TCB}_{\text{micro}}$
- ✅ **Rule of thumb for design questions:** Safety-critical (ASIL-D, DO-178C) → **Microkernel**; cost-sensitive consumer → **Monolithic**; general-purpose desktop/server → **Hybrid**.
- ✅ **Always draw the block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it's worth 3–4 marks *by itself*.
- ✅ **Context-switch count is the smoking gun:** Monolithic = 1 per syscall; Microkernel = 2–3 per syscall.
- ✅ **L4 family microkernels** (seL4, OKL4) bridge the performance gap to within ~10% of monolithic — mention this for *extra credit*.
- ✅ **Modular Monolithic ≠ Microkernel** — Linux can dynamically load `.ko` modules but is *not* a microkernel. Be precise.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tasks/Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT302 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tasks/Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Task (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** and embedded systems, a **Task** (also called a **Thread** in lightweight kernels) is defined as:

> A **Task** is an independent, sequential program execution path that shares the processor and most system resources with other tasks, but maintains its own private context consisting of a stack, register set (CPU context), and a unique **Task Control Block (TCB)**.

A **Process**, in classical OS terminology, is a heavier entity — a program in execution with its own **isolated memory address space**. In most embedded RTOS environments (e.g., **FreeRTOS**), the terms *task* and *thread* are used interchangeably, and there is **no separate address space** between them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In PCCPT302 Module 4, the syllabus uses the term **"Task"** — and you must define it as a *lightweight, single-thread of execution within a shared address space* (NOT a heavy process). Examiners mark strictly on this distinction.

### Formal Characteristics of a Task

- **Single Entry Point** — Every task is a `void pvTaskCode(void *pvParameters)` style function in C.
- **Infinite Loop** — A task is typically written as `for(;;) { ... }` and never returns.
- **Private Stack** — Each task has its own statically or dynamically allocated stack memory.
- **Shared Code/Data** — All tasks share global variables, peripherals, and heap of the kernel.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏭

Imagine a **small restaurant kitchen with 3 chefs** but only **1 stove (the CPU)**:

- The **stove** = the single CPU core
- Each **chef** = a task
- The **manager standing at the door** = the **Scheduler / Dispatcher**
- The **recipe card pinned to each chef's apron** = the **Task Control Block (TCB)** — it contains the chef's current step, ingredients ready, and time remaining.
- The **"Order Ticket Queue"** = the **Ready Queue**
- The **refrigerator door (closed)** = the **Blocked state** (chef is waiting for an ingredient to arrive)

The manager (scheduler) keeps **switching chefs** at the stove. When a chef is interrupted mid-recipe, his apron-card (TCB) is updated so he can resume *exactly* where he left off. This switching is **Context Switching** — and each chef never knows they were paused.

## 1.3 Task vs. Process — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded systems primarily use TASKS, not Processes**, because embedded MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) lack Memory Management Units (MMU) and cannot afford the overhead of process isolation.

| Feature | Task (Thread) | Process |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | Shared with all tasks | Private, isolated |
| Context Switch Overhead | **Low (~microseconds)** | High (page tables, TLB flush) |
| Communication | Shared variables, queues | IPC (pipes, sockets) |
| Memory Footprint | Small (few KB) | Large (MB) |
| Embedded Suitability | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Rarely used |
| Example OS | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, μC/OS-II | Linux, Windows |

## 1.4 Physical/Engineering Constants in Task Design

- **Typical FreeRTOS Tick Rate (configTICK_RATE_HZ):** between **1 Hz** and **1000 Hz** (commonly **1000 Hz** = 1 ms tick)
- **Stack Size per Task:** ranges from **64 bytes** (simple blinking LED) to **4 KB+** (complex parsing)
- **Maximum Tasks (FreeRTOS default):** **Unlimited** by `configMAX_PRIORITIES` × heap (practically limited by RAM)
- **Context Switch Time:** typically **1–10 μs** on ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz
- **Priority Levels:** `configMAX_PRIORITIES` is usually set to **5, 7, or 32**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task Life-Cycle — The Five Canonical States

Every RTOS task exists in exactly one of the following **five states** at any given instant:

### State 1: **Running** 🟢
- The task currently **owns the CPU** and is actively executing instructions.
- **Only ONE task per CPU core** can be in the Running state at any time.
- When the system has *N* cores, at most *N* tasks run simultaneously.

### State 2: **Ready** 🟡
- The task is **fully prepared to execute** but is waiting for the CPU because a higher- or equal-priority task is currently running.
- Tasks in the Ready state are stored in a **Priority-ordered Ready Queue** (often implemented as a bitmap + linked list in FreeRTOS).

### State 3: **Blocked** 🔵
- The task is **waiting for an event** (e.g., a delay expiration, semaphore give, queue data, interrupt flag).
- It is **NOT** in the Ready queue and will not be considered for scheduling until the event occurs.
- This is the most common "idle" state in well-designed embedded firmware.

### State 4: **Suspended** 🟣
- The task is **explicitly put to sleep** by API calls (`vTaskSuspend()`) and **does not run** until another task calls `vTaskResume()`.
- Unlike Blocked, it has **no timeout** — it stays suspended indefinitely.

### State 5: **Deleted (Terminated)** ⚫
- The task has **finished execution** or has been explicitly deleted (`vTaskDelete()`).
- Its TCB and stack are freed from memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick: "B-R-S-D-R"** → **B**locked, **R**eady, **S**uspended, **D**eleted, **R**unning. Examiners frequently draw state diagrams and ask for transitions.

## 2.2 Task State Transition Diagram Logic

The transitions follow strict rules:

1. **Created → Ready:** `xTaskCreate()` is called and the task becomes eligible to run.
2. **Ready → Running:** Scheduler dispatches it (highest priority Ready task wins).
3. **Running → Ready:** Preempted by a higher-priority task, or its time slice (`time quantum`) expires.
4. **Running → Blocked:** Task calls a blocking API (`vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueReceive()`, `xSemaphoreTake()`).
5. **Blocked → Ready:** Blocking event occurs (delay timeout, semaphore given, queue has data).
6. **Running → Suspended:** `vTaskSuspend()` is called on itself or by another task.
7. **Suspended → Ready:** `vTaskResume()` is called.
8. **Running → Deleted:** `vTaskDelete()` invoked.
9. **Any State → Deleted:** Some kernels allow forced deletion (not recommended in safety-critical systems).

## 2.3 Task Control Block (TCB) — The Task's "Passport"

The **TCB** (called `TCB_t` in FreeRTOS) is a `struct` that the kernel allocates for each task. It stores **everything needed to resume the task exactly where it left off**.

### Typical TCB Members:
- `pxTopOfStack` — Pointer to the last item on the task's stack (critical for context restore)
- `uxPriority` — Task priority (0 = lowest, `configMAX_PRIORITIES-1` = highest)
- `pxStack` — Base of the task's stack memory
- `usStackDepth` — Allocated stack size in words
- `xStateListItem` — Links the TCB into state lists (Ready, Blocked, Suspended)
- `xEventListItem` — Used to queue the TCB for event lists (e.g., waiting on a queue)
- `uxCriticalNesting` — Critical section nesting counter
- `pcTaskName` — Human-readable name (max `configMAX_TASK_NAME_LEN` = 16 chars default)
- `*pxTLS` — Thread-Local Storage pointer (optional)

## 2.4 Context Switching — The Heart of Multitasking

A **Context Switch** is the procedure of:
1. **Saving** the CPU registers of the currently running task into its TCB stack.
2. **Loading** the saved registers of the next selected task from its TCB stack.
3. **Resuming** execution of the newly loaded task.

This is triggered by:
- **SysTick Interrupt** (preemptive, periodic, every 1 ms in FreeRTOS at 1000 Hz)
- **Explicit yield** (`taskYIELD()`)
- **Blocking API calls** that cause the scheduler to run

> [!NOTE]
> In ARM Cortex-M, context switching is highly optimized: the hardware **automatically pushes R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR** onto the PSP (Process Stack Pointer) on exception entry, and the PendSV handler does the rest.

## 2.5 Scheduling Algorithms (Brief Overview)

| Algorithm | Mechanism | Used In | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cooperative** | Tasks voluntarily yield | FreeRTOS (config option 0) | ⭐⭐ |
| **Preemptive (Priority)** | Highest-priority Ready task always runs | FreeRTOS (config option 1) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Round-Robin** | Time-slicing among equal-priority tasks | FreeRTOS (config option 2) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Rate Monotonic (RMS)** | Static priority = inverse of period | Hard real-time | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic priority by deadline | Soft real-time | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tick Period** | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | seconds; $f_{tick}$ = `configTICK_RATE_HZ` |
| 2 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | where $C_i$ = execution time, $T_i$ = period |
| 3 | **Rate Monotonic Bound (Liu & Layland)** | $U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | n = number of tasks |
| 4 | **Schedulability (RMS)** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Guarantee if met |
| 5 | **Stack Word Size (32-bit)** | $\text{Bytes} = 4 \times W$ | $W$ = words |
| 6 | **Context Switch Time (est.)** | $T_{cs} = t_{save} + t_{load}$ | typically 1–10 μs |
| 7 | **Idle Time Fraction** | $U_{idle} = 1 - U$ | usable for low-power modes |
| 8 | **Max Tasks (practical)** | $N_{max} \leq \dfrac{RAM_{free}}{S_{stack} + S_{TCB}}$ | $S_{TCB} \approx 80$ bytes |
| 9 | **Round-Robin Quantum** | $Q = k \cdot T_{tick}$ | k = ticks per slice (e.g., 5) |
| 10 | **Priority Inversion Time Bound (Priority Inheritance)** | $T_{pi} \leq C_{critical}$ | prevents unbounded inversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** I have used `\vert` style for absolute values (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) implicitly through `≤` etc. **NEVER write** `|x|` inside markdown tables — the pipe breaks table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control):** Multiple tasks for fuel injection, ABS, dashboard, CAN bus.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Task for sensor sampling, task for Wi-Fi transmission, task for OTA updates.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, Ventilators):** Hard real-time tasks with strict deadlines — RMS analysis is **mandatory for FDA certification**.
- **Consumer Electronics (Smart Watches):** UI task, BLE task, heart-rate sensing task.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Deterministic task scheduling ensures millisecond-level control loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Utilisation Bound

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. We derive the upper bound on CPU utilisation below which RMS is guaranteed to schedule *n* periodic tasks without missing any deadline.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

- We have **n** periodic, independent tasks.
- Task $T_i$ has period $P_i$ (also the deadline) and worst-case execution time $C_i$.
- Priorities are assigned such that **shorter period → higher priority**.
- We want to find the maximum $U$ such that **all deadlines are always met**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define total CPU utilisation for *n* tasks.

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i}
$$

**Step 2:** The **critical instant** for any task $T_i$ occurs when **all higher-priority tasks release jobs at the same time as $T_i$**. This causes the longest response time.

**Step 3:** At the critical instant, task $T_i$'s response time $R_i$ must satisfy:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 4:** For schedulability, $R_i \leq P_i$ for all $i$. The worst case (tightest bound) occurs when all $C_i / P_i$ ratios are equal:

$$
\frac{C_i}{P_i} = \frac{U}{n} \quad \text{for all } i
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and observe that the ceiling function $\lceil x \rceil$ is maximised (worst case) when periods are in geometric progression: $P_i = P_n / 2^{n-i}$. With this arrangement:

$$
\left\lceil \frac{P_n}{P_j} \right\rceil = 2^{j-1} + 1 \quad \text{(boundary term)}
$$

**Step 6:** Plugging into the response time inequality and simplifying via induction (Liu & Layland, 1973):

$$
R_n \leq \frac{U}{n} \cdot P_n \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{1}{2^k}
$$

**Step 7:** The geometric series sum is:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{1}{2^k} = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \cdots + \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} = 2 - \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}
$$

**Step 8:** Setting $R_n \leq P_n$ for schedulability:

$$
\frac{U}{n} \cdot \left( 2 - \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \right) \leq 1
$$

**Step 9:** Solve for the upper bound $U_{bound}$:

$$
U \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 10:** Final result — the **Liu & Layland Utilisation Bound**:

$$
\boxed{\,U_{RMS} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)\,}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Evaluation Table

| n | $2^{1/n}$ | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Approx. % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0000 | 1.0000 | 100.00% |
| 2 | 1.4142 | 0.8284 | 82.84% |
| 3 | 1.2599 | 0.7798 | 77.98% |
| 4 | 1.1892 | 0.7568 | 75.68% |
| 5 | 1.1487 | 0.7435 | 74.35% |
| ∞ | 1.0000 | ≈ 0.6931 | **69.31% (ln 2)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** As $n \to \infty$, the bound converges to $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. This is the famous **"69% rule"** — in RMS, you should never plan to use more than **~69%** of the CPU in a large system, leaving 31% as safety margin.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: CPU Utilisation Calculation (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
A system has **3 periodic tasks** scheduled under RMS. Compute the total CPU utilisation and check schedulability.

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) | Period $P_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | 1 | 5 |
| T2 | 2 | 10 |
| T3 | 3 | 20 |

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute individual utilisation.

$$
u_1 = \frac{C_1}{P_1} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{C_2}{P_2} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.200
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{C_3}{P_3} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.150
$$

**Step 2:** Sum for total utilisation.

$$
U = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.550
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the RMS bound for $n=3$.

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798
$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

$$
U = 0.550 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Context Switch Overhead Budget

### Problem
If each context switch takes $T_{cs}$ and the tick rate is $f_{tick}$, what fraction of CPU is **wasted on context switching**?

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Number of context switches per second (assuming one switch per tick and the system is never idle):

$$
N_{cs} = f_{tick} \quad \text{(switches/second)}
$$

**Step 2:** Time spent switching per second:

$$
T_{cs,total} = N_{cs} \cdot T_{cs} = f_{tick} \cdot T_{cs}
$$

**Step 3:** Fractional overhead:

$$
\eta_{cs} = \frac{T_{cs,total}}{1 \text{ second}} = f_{tick} \cdot T_{cs}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example: $f_{tick} = 1000$ Hz, $T_{cs} = 5$ μs:

$$
\eta_{cs} = 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.005 = 0.5\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why FreeRTOS uses **1 kHz tick**, not 10 kHz — overhead must stay below ~1% for hard real-time guarantees.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Scheduling Strategy

| Application Domain | Recommended Algorithm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hard real-time control loop | **Rate Monotonic** | Static priorities, simple, certified |
| Multimedia streaming | **EDF** | High utilisation (~100%) |
| Battery-powered IoT | **Cooperative** | Less wakeups, less overhead |
| Multi-core server | **Linux CFS** (fair scheduling) | Throughput-oriented |
| Industrial PLC | **Preemptive Fixed Priority** | Determinism required |
| Consumer UI | **Round Robin** | Fairness between UI threads |

## 4.1 Task State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

This is the **most frequently drawn diagram** in KTU exams for this module. We render it as a state machine.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReadyA : xTaskCreate()
    ReadyA --> RunningA : Scheduler dispatches
    RunningA --> ReadyA : Preempted / Time slice expires
    RunningA --> BlockedA : vTaskDelay / xQueueReceive / xSemaphoreTake
    BlockedA --> ReadyA : Event occurs / Timeout
    RunningA --> SuspendedA : vTaskSuspend
    SuspendedA --> ReadyA : vTaskResume
    RunningA --> [*] : vTaskDelete
    BlockedA --> [*] : vTaskDelete
    ReadyA --> [*] : vTaskDelete
    SuspendedA --> [*] : vTaskDelete

    note right of RunningA : Only ONE task per core
    note right of BlockedA : Not in ready queue
    note left of SuspendedA : No timeout - manual resume
```

## 4.3 Context Switch Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The physical layout of how an ARM Cortex-M context switch is performed (using PendSV exception):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[SysTick Interrupt Fires] --> Save1[Hardware auto-pushes R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR onto PSP]
    Save1 --> Save2[Save remaining R4 R5 R6 R7 R8 R9 R10 R11 in software]
    Save2 --> Save3[Store PSP into Old TCB pxTopOfStack]
    Save3 --> Sched[Invoke Scheduler vTaskSwitchContext]
    Sched --> Load1[Select new highest-priority Ready task]
    Load1 --> Load2[Load new PSP from New TCB pxTopOfStack]
    Load2 --> Load3[Restore R4 R5 R6 R7 R8 R9 R10 R11 in software]
    Load3 --> Load4[Hardware auto-pops R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 LR PC xPSR on exception return]
    Load4 --> Run[New task executes]

    style Start fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Sched fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style Run fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style Save2 fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style Load3 fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Task and a Process with respect to an RTOS environment.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Task | Process |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Shared address space | Isolated address space |
| Weight | Lightweight (few hundred bytes) | Heavyweight (MBs) |
| Switch Cost | Low (1–10 μs) | High (page table swap) |
| Communication | Shared variables / kernel objects | IPC mechanisms |
| Usage in RTOS | Very common | Rare |

> A **Task** is a lightweight thread of execution sharing memory with other tasks, whereas a **Process** is a heavy execution unit with its own isolated address space. In FreeRTOS-style embedded kernels, **only tasks are used** because microcontrollers typically lack an MMU.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for each valid point, 1 mark for the RTOS angle)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the five states of a task in an RTOS and briefly explain the "Blocked" state.**

**Model Answer:**

The five states are:
1. **Running** — currently executing on the CPU
2. **Ready** — eligible to run but not scheduled
3. **Blocked** — waiting for an event (delay, semaphore, queue)
4. **Suspended** — explicitly put to sleep, no timeout
5. **Deleted** — terminated, resources freed

**Blocked State Explanation (extra detail):**
A task enters the Blocked state when it calls a blocking API like `vTaskDelay(ticks)`, `xQueueReceive()` on an empty queue, or `xSemaphoreTake()` on an unavailable semaphore. The task is **removed from the Ready list** and placed on an event-waiting list. It consumes **zero CPU time** during this period and is not considered for scheduling. The task transitions back to **Ready** automatically when the awaited event occurs (or a timeout expires).

**(3 marks — 1 for naming states, 2 for explanation)**

---

## 📌 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> KTU 2024 scheme mandates **internal choice** within a module. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **task state transition model** in an RTOS. Discuss all valid transitions and indicate which transitions are triggered by **preemption** vs. **blocking APIs**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three periodic tasks are scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**. Determine whether the task set is schedulable.

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) | Period $P_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | 1 | 4 |
| T2 | 2 | 6 |
| T3 | 1 | 10 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Task State Transitions

**Step 1:** Draw the 5-state diagram (as shown in Section 4.1 above).

**Step 2:** Explain each transition:

1. **Created → Ready:** `xTaskCreate()` allocates TCB, stack, and adds the task to the Ready list. *(Not preemption, not blocking — it's an API.)*

2. **Ready → Running:** The scheduler dispatches the **highest-priority Ready task** when the CPU is free. *(Trigger: Scheduler decision.)*

3. **Running → Ready (Preemption):** A **higher-priority task** becomes Ready (e.g., due to interrupt + unblock). The current task is **preempted** without its consent. The SysTick interrupt at 1 kHz also forces preemption for time-slicing (Round-Robin among equal-priority tasks). *(Triggered by: Preemption.)*

4. **Running → Blocked:** Task voluntarily calls `vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueReceive()` on empty queue, `xSemaphoreTake()` on unavailable semaphore, etc. *(Triggered by: Blocking API.)*

5. **Blocked → Ready:** The awaited event occurs (timeout, data arrives, semaphore given by ISR or another task). *(Triggered by: Event.)*

6. **Running → Suspended:** `vTaskSuspend(taskHandle)` called by itself or another task. No timeout applies. *(Triggered by: API.)*

7. **Suspended → Ready:** `vTaskResume(taskHandle)` explicitly called. *(Triggered by: API.)*

8. **Running/Deleted:** `vTaskDelete()` is called — the TCB and stack are freed. *(Triggered by: API.)*

**Step 3:** Highlight the **preemption pathway** (Ready ↔ Running) versus the **voluntary blocking pathway** (Running → Blocked).

> *(Award 7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for transitions, 1 for preemption distinction, 1 for clarity.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(b) — RMS Schedulability

**Given:**
$T_1: C_1 = 1$ ms, $P_1 = 4$ ms
$T_2: C_2 = 2$ ms, $P_2 = 6$ ms
$T_3: C_3 = 1$ ms, $P_3 = 10$ ms

**Step 1: Verify RMS priority order.** Periods ranked: $4 < 6 < 10$, so priorities are $T_1 > T_2 > T_3$. ✅ Order is correct.

**Step 2: Compute individual utilisations.**

$$
u_1 = \frac{C_1}{P_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{C_2}{P_2} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{C_3}{P_3} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.100
$$

**Step 3: Total utilisation.**

$$
U = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.100 = 0.683
$$

**Step 4: Compute RMS bound for $n=3$.**

$$
U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 5: Compare.**

$$
U = 0.683 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS** (sufficient test, though not necessary — exact response-time analysis may also be required in some KTU papers).

> *(Award 7 marks: 2 for RMS bound, 2 for U calculation, 1 for comparison, 1 for clean conclusion, 1 for the exact value computation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Task Control Block (TCB)**. List its key fields and explain the role of the **stack pointer** in context switching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **context switching** process in an ARM Cortex-M based RTOS. Why is the **PendSV exception** specifically used for this purpose? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(a) — TCB

**Step 1: Definition.**
A **Task Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel-allocated data structure (`struct TCB_t` in FreeRTOS) that stores **all per-task information** required to manage and resume the task.

**Step 2: Key fields (list at least 6 for full marks):**

1. **`pxTopOfStack`** — Pointer to current stack top; used during context save/restore.
2. **`uxPriority`** — Numeric priority (0 = lowest).
3. **`pxStack`** — Base of allocated stack memory.
4. **`usStackDepth`** — Total stack size in words.
5. **`xStateListItem`** — Linked-list pointers to insert TCB into Ready / Blocked / Suspended lists.
6. **`xEventListItem`** — Linked-list pointer for event waiting (queues, semaphores).
7. **`pcTaskName`** — String identifier (max 16 chars by default).
8. **`uxCriticalNesting`** — Counts nested critical sections to allow re-entry.
9. **`*pxTLS`** — Thread-local storage pointer (portable layer feature).

**Step 3: Role of stack pointer in context switch.**
When a task is preempted, the hardware **automatically pushes** R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR onto the task's stack (using PSP — Process Stack Pointer). The remaining callee-saved registers (R4–R11) are saved by **software** in the PendSV handler. The updated `pxTopOfStack` value is stored in the TCB. Later, when the task is re-dispatched, the reverse operation restores all registers from `pxTopOfStack`, and the task resumes **exactly** where it left off — as if it had never been interrupted.

> *(Award 7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for TCB fields, 2 for stack pointer role.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(b) — Context Switching & PendSV

**Step 1: Draw the flow** (as shown in Section 4.3 above).

**Step 2: Step-by-step procedure on ARM Cortex-M:**

1. **SysTick interrupt** fires every 1 ms (at `configTICK_RATE_HZ = 1000`).
2. Inside the SysTick ISR, the kernel sets the **PendSV pending bit** (`SCB->ICSR |= (1<<28)`).
3. The CPU completes the SysTick ISR.
4. **PendSV exception** is taken — this is the **lowest-priority exception** in the system, ensuring it runs only when nothing more urgent is active.
5. The hardware **auto-saves** R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR onto the current task's PSP.
6. The PendSV handler **software-saves** R4–R11.
7. The current PSP is stored in the **outgoing TCB's `pxTopOfStack`**.
8. The **scheduler** (`vTaskSwitchContext`) is called to pick the highest-priority Ready task.
9. The new task's `pxTopOfStack` is loaded into PSP.
10. R4–R11 are software-restored.
11. Exception return (`bx LR` with specific bit pattern) auto-restores R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR from the new stack.
12. Execution continues in the **new task** — at the precise instruction where it was last preempted.

**Step 3: Why PendSV specifically?**

- **Lowest priority** → guarantees it cannot preempt a real ISR. This prevents **interrupt latency** for hardware events.
- **Programmable pending bit** → SysTick and other ISRs can request a context switch without doing it directly inside an ISR (which would be unsafe).
- **Cortex-M standard** → portable across all Cortex-M3/M4/M7 devices.
- **Lazy stacking** on Cortex-M4F with FPU is also handled gracefully by PendSV.

> *(Award 7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for steps, 2 for PendSV justification.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tasks/Processes Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Task and Process** — Writing "process" when the question asks about RTOS *task*. (-1 to -2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the RMS formula — writing `C/P` without ms or stating `T_i` and `C_i` units. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the diagram** in state-transition questions — minimum 2 marks reserved for a *neat* labeled diagram.
> 4. **Missing the priority assignment check** in RMS — students often skip verifying that priority order matches period order. Examiners look for this **explicitly**.
> 5. **Confusing "Blocked" with "Suspended"** — Blocked has a timeout, Suspended does NOT. This distinction is tested every year.
> 6. **Writing `vTaskDelay(0)` as yielding** — it does not yield; only `taskYIELD()` or higher-priority preemption does. Examiners flag this.
> 7. **Not showing derivation steps in RMS** — directly writing the formula without derivation loses 2 marks on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Task = lightweight thread**, Process = heavyweight isolated program. RTOS uses **tasks only**.
- ⭐ Five task states: **Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted** — remember "B-R-S-D-R" mnemonic.
- ⭐ **TCB** is the "passport" of a task — contains stack pointer, priority, name, list links.
- ⭐ **Context switch** is triggered by SysTick (preemptive), blocking API (voluntary), or explicit yield.
- ⭐ **Preemptive priority scheduling** is the default in FreeRTOS and is the most tested algorithm.
- ⭐ **RMS Utilisation Bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; converges to **~69.3%** for large n.
- ⭐ **EDF** can theoretically achieve **100% utilisation** but is harder to implement.
- ⭐ **Blocked ≠ Suspended** — Blocked has timeout, Suspended needs explicit `vTaskResume()`.
- ⭐ **PendSV** is used for context switching on ARM Cortex-M because it's the lowest-priority exception.
- ⭐ **Stack overflow is the #1 cause of embedded RTOS crashes** — always enable `configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW`.
- ⭐ **Priority inversion** can be solved by **Priority Inheritance Mutexes** (e.g., `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()` in FreeRTOS, not binary semaphores).
- ⭐ Typical tick rate = **1 kHz (1 ms)**, giving context switch overhead of **< 1%**.
- ⭐ **Idle task** always runs at priority 0 and is where you should put the CPU into low-power sleep mode (`__WFI()`).
- ⭐ **Deadlock** is a real risk when two tasks hold one mutex each and wait for the other — design lock hierarchies.
- ⭐ Always quote **FreeRTOS API function names** correctly in answers: `xTaskCreate`, `vTaskDelete`, `vTaskDelay`, `vTaskSuspend`, `vTaskResume`, `xQueueReceive`, `xSemaphoreTake`.
